title: Apache Airflow for Data Engineers: Junior-Level Track
description: Understand why unorchestrated data pipelines fail at scale, what workflow orchestration means, how Apache Airflow fits into the data engineering stack, and how the SaulėGrid scenario maps to a real-world orchestration context.

chapters.title: Why Workflow Orchestration Exists
chapters.description: Understand why unorchestrated data pipelines fail at scale, what workflow orchestration means, how Apache Airflow fits into the data engineering stack, and how the SaulėGrid scenario maps to a real-world orchestration context.

sections.title: When Pipelines Run on Cron and Hope

blocks.content: Every data pipeline starts simple. You write a Python script that downloads a file, transforms it, and loads the result into a database. You schedule the script to run every night at midnight using cron, the built-in scheduler on every Linux server. For weeks it works perfectly — the script runs, the data arrives, and nobody thinks about it. Then one night the source file arrives two hours late. The script ran on time, found no file, wrote an empty dataset to the database, and the morning dashboard showed zero energy consumption across the entire country. Nobody was alerted. Nobody knew the file was late. The script did exactly what it was told — run at midnight — but midnight was the wrong answer. This lesson is about why scheduling alone is not enough, and what SaulėGrid learned when their first pipelines broke.
The Cron Approach

Cron is a time-based job scheduler that has existed on Unix systems since 1975. It does exactly one thing: run a command at a specific time. You write a cron expression like 0 0 * * * (every day at midnight) and attach it to a script. When the clock strikes midnight, cron executes the script. Cron does not know what the script does, whether it succeeded, whether its input data is ready, or whether another script needs to finish first. It fires on the clock, blind to everything else. For a single script with no dependencies, this is perfectly adequate.

SaulėGrid, a Lithuanian solar energy company, started with exactly this approach. Their data engineering team — Matas and Ieva — wrote three Python scripts for the nightly data pipeline. The first script downloaded meter readings from an SFTP server. The second script cleaned and validated the readings. The third script aggregated the validated data into regional summaries for the billing team. Each script was scheduled as a separate cron job, staggered by 30 minutes to give the previous step time to finish.

# SaulėGrid's original cron schedule — three independent jobs
# Each job runs at a fixed time, with no awareness of the others

# Download meter readings from SFTP at midnight
0 0 * * *   /scripts/download_meters.py

# Clean and validate readings at 00:30
30 0 * * *  /scripts/validate_meters.py

# Aggregate regional summaries at 01:00
0 1 * * *   /scripts/aggregate_regions.py
The 30-Minute Assumption

The 30-minute gap between jobs was an assumption: the download usually takes 10 minutes, so 30 minutes leaves a comfortable buffer. But assumptions about timing are fragile. On March 3rd, the SFTP server was slow and the download took 45 minutes. The validation script started at 00:30 as scheduled, found a partially downloaded file, processed it, and produced a corrupt Silver dataset. The aggregation script ran at 01:00 on the corrupt data and produced billing summaries with numbers that were clearly wrong. The billing team noticed at 9 AM — eight hours after the failure began.

blocks.title: What SaulėGrid expected vs what actually happened
blocks.content:
Expected (download finishes in 10 minutes):

00:00       00:30       01:00
  │           │           │
  ▼           ▼           ▼
[Download]  [Validate]  [Aggregate]
  10 min      15 min      12 min
  ✓ Done      ✓ Done      ✓ Done

What actually happened (download took 45 minutes):

00:00       00:30       01:00
  │           │           │
  ▼           ▼           ▼
[Download────────────────]
  Still downloading...
            [Validate]
             Read partial
             file → corrupt
                          [Aggregate]
                           Used corrupt
                           data → wrong totals
blocks.caption: The 30-minute buffer was a timing assumption, not a dependency. When the assumption broke, the downstream scripts had no way to know their input was not ready.

visual-recommendation.description: Timeline visualization showing the expected vs actual execution of SaulėGrid's three cron jobs, with color-coded task bars showing overlap and failure propagation.

blocks.content: Three Problems With Cron

SaulėGrid's cron-based approach had three fundamental problems, and each one is a consequence of the same root cause: cron knows about time, but it knows nothing about dependencies. The first problem is timing assumptions. Every gap between jobs is a guess about how long the previous job will take. When the guess is wrong, downstream jobs run on incomplete or missing data. The second problem is invisible failures. When a script fails silently — it runs but produces wrong results — there is no mechanism to detect the failure or prevent downstream scripts from consuming bad data. The third problem is manual recovery. When something breaks, Matas had to log into the server, check each script's log file, figure out which step failed, rerun it manually, then rerun everything downstream. A 2-minute failure could take an hour to recover from.

blocks.title: The root cause
blocks.content: Cron answers the question 'when should this run?' An orchestrator answers a different question: 'when is it safe to run this?' The difference is dependencies. A time-based schedule assumes the world is predictable. A dependency-based schedule adapts when the world is not.
SaulėGrid's Growing Pain

When SaulėGrid had three scripts, the cron approach was inconvenient but manageable. Matas could hold the entire pipeline in his head and fix problems in 30 minutes. But SaulėGrid grew. They added solar inverter telemetry processing. They added weather forecast ingestion. They added battery state-of-charge calculations for prosumer customers. They added a regulatory reporting pipeline for VERT, Lithuania's energy regulator. Within a year, the three scripts had become twelve, with complex dependencies between them. The weather forecast had to arrive before the generation prediction could run. The generation prediction had to finish before the net metering balance could be calculated. The net metering balance had to be ready before the billing aggregation could include prosumer credits.

Twelve cron jobs with timing assumptions and no dependency tracking became a daily source of anxiety. Every morning, Ieva's first task was to check whether all twelve jobs had completed successfully and in the right order. Some mornings, a late weather API response had cascaded into three downstream failures. Other mornings, everything ran perfectly but the billing team reported numbers that did not match yesterday's pattern — and the investigation revealed that a validation script had silently produced an empty file. SaulėGrid needed something better than cron. They needed orchestration.

blocks.title: Common misconception
blocks.content: The problem is not that cron is bad software. Cron does exactly what it promises: run a command at a time. The problem is that data pipelines need more than time-based execution. They need dependency awareness, state tracking, and failure recovery. Using cron for data pipelines is like using a hammer to drive screws — the tool is fine, but it is the wrong tool for the job.

The previous lesson introduced SaulėGrid's cron-based pipeline and the problems that emerged when timing assumptions failed. This lesson looks deeper at what goes wrong when pipeline steps execute without any awareness of each other. The failures are not dramatic explosions — they are quiet, gradual, and often invisible until a business user notices that a dashboard looks wrong. Understanding these failure modes is the motivation for everything else in this course, because orchestration exists precisely to prevent them.
Silent Data Loss

The most dangerous failure in an unorchestrated pipeline is silent data loss — a job runs, completes successfully, and produces output that is missing data. The job itself does not fail, so there is no error message, no alert, and no indication that anything went wrong. SaulėGrid experienced this on April 12th when their meter download script timed out after downloading 280,000 of 400,000 meters. The script exited with code 0 (success) because it wrote whatever it had to disk before the timeout killed the SFTP connection. The validation script read the partial file, validated the 280,000 rows, and passed them all. The aggregation script produced regional summaries that were 30% lower than expected.

The billing team noticed the drop at 10 AM and escalated to Matas. It took him two hours to trace the problem: the download log showed a timeout warning buried in 500 lines of output, but the script's exit code was 0. The validation script had no way to know that 120,000 rows were missing — it validated what it received, and what it received was valid. Every step in the pipeline did its job correctly. The pipeline as a whole produced wrong results. This is the fundamental challenge of unorchestrated pipelines: individual correctness does not guarantee collective correctness.
Duplicate Processing

The second common failure is duplicate processing. When a pipeline step fails and is rerun manually, the operator must decide which steps to rerun and which to skip. If the download succeeded but the validation failed, only the validation and aggregation need to rerun. But if the operator accidentally reruns the download too, and the SFTP server has already rotated the file to tomorrow's data, the pipeline processes tomorrow's meters as if they were today's. SaulėGrid hit this problem twice in their first month. The second time, it produced double-counted billing that was not caught until the monthly reconciliation.

blocks.title: The rerun problem
blocks.content: Without an orchestrator, the question 'which steps do I need to rerun?' has no automated answer. The operator must trace the dependency chain mentally, determine the failure point, and manually execute each downstream step in the correct order. With 12 pipeline steps, this is error-prone. With 50 steps, it is practically impossible.
The Debugging Nightmare

Each of SaulėGrid's cron scripts wrote its own log file to a different directory. When a pipeline failed, Matas had to SSH into the server, navigate to each script's log directory, open the log for the correct date, and search for errors. There was no single place to see the status of all twelve jobs. There was no timeline showing when each job ran and how long it took. There was no way to see that the generation prediction job was waiting for the weather forecast job without reading the code. Debugging a cascade failure — where one late job caused three downstream failures — required opening four separate log files and mentally reconstructing the timeline.

blocks.title: Debugging an unorchestrated pipeline vs an orchestrated one
blocks.content:
Without orchestration:

  Developer must:
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  1. SSH into production server               │
  │  2. Check /var/log/download_meters.log       │
  │  3. Check /var/log/validate_meters.log       │
  │  4. Check /var/log/aggregate_regions.log     │
  │  5. Mentally reconstruct the timeline        │
  │  6. Determine which step failed first        │
  │  7. Manually rerun failed + downstream steps │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  Time: 30-120 minutes per incident

With orchestration:

  Developer opens the web UI:
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  1. See all jobs on one screen               │
  │  2. Red = failed, green = success            │
  │  3. Click the red task → see the log         │
  │  4. Click 'Retry' → downstream reruns auto   │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  Time: 2-5 minutes per incident
blocks.caption: An orchestrator centralizes status, logs, and recovery into one interface. The time savings compound: a team handling 5 incidents per week saves 10-20 hours compared to the manual approach.

visual-recommendation.description: Split-screen comparison showing a terminal with scattered log files on the left and a clean orchestrator web UI with color-coded task statuses on the right.

blocks.content: The Alert Gap

SaulėGrid's cron jobs had no built-in alerting. Matas wrote a wrapper script that sent an email if a job's exit code was non-zero, but this only caught hard failures — scripts that crashed with an exception. It did not catch silent failures (exit code 0 but wrong results), partial failures (some data processed, some not), or dependency violations (a job ran before its input was ready). The team learned about most problems from the billing department or the operations dashboard, hours after the failure occurred. By the time they knew something was wrong, the bad data had already been consumed by three downstream reports.
The Cost of Manual Coordination

After three months, Ieva calculated the team's overhead from manual pipeline management. Matas spent an average of 45 minutes each morning verifying that all jobs had run correctly. When a failure occurred — roughly twice per week — recovery took 1 to 3 hours. The team spent approximately 10 hours per week on pipeline babysitting rather than building new pipelines or improving data quality. For a two-person team, that was 25% of their working time spent on a problem that should have been automated. This overhead was the final motivation for adopting an orchestration platform.

rows: Cascade failure (one late job breaks many)

blocks.title: Why this matters
blocks.content: Every failure mode in this table is caused by the same underlying problem: pipeline steps that execute without awareness of each other. An orchestrator does not eliminate the possibility of SFTP timeouts or late files. What it eliminates is the gap between failure and detection, and it automates the recovery process. The failures still happen — the response becomes instant instead of hours.

The previous two lessons showed what breaks when pipelines run without coordination. This lesson defines what coordination actually means in the context of data engineering. Workflow orchestration is a specific term with a specific meaning — it is not just 'better scheduling' or 'running scripts in order.' Understanding the precise definition is the foundation for understanding why Airflow is designed the way it is, and why every feature you will learn in this course exists.
Scheduling vs Orchestration

Scheduling answers one question: when should this job run? A scheduler fires a job at a specified time. It does not know whether the job's input data is ready, whether prerequisite jobs have completed, or whether the job's output will be consumed by other jobs. Cron is a scheduler. Windows Task Scheduler is a scheduler. A cloud-based timer that triggers a Lambda function every hour is a scheduler. Schedulers are simple, reliable, and fundamentally limited — they operate on time alone.

Orchestration answers a different question: when is it safe to run this job? An orchestrator knows about dependencies between jobs. It knows that the validation job should not start until the download job has succeeded. It knows that the aggregation job depends on both the validation job and the weather forecast job. It knows that if the download fails, everything downstream should wait rather than running on missing data. An orchestrator adds three capabilities on top of scheduling: dependency management, state tracking, and failure recovery.

left.label: Scheduling (cron)
points: Fires jobs based on time
No awareness of job relationships
No job state tracking between runs
No automatic retry on failure
No centralized monitoring
Manual recovery after failures

right.label: Orchestration (Airflow)
points: Fires jobs based on dependencies and time
Knows the full dependency graph
Tracks every run: success, failed, running, waiting
Configurable retries with backoff
Web UI showing all jobs, states, and logs
One-click retry that reruns downstream automatically

blocks.content: The Three Pillars of Orchestration

Workflow orchestration rests on three pillars, and every orchestration tool — Airflow included — must provide all three. The first pillar is dependency management: the ability to declare that task B depends on task A, so that B only runs after A succeeds. Dependencies can be simple (A then B) or complex (B depends on A and C; D depends on B or E). The dependency graph replaces timing assumptions with explicit relationships. SaulėGrid's 30-minute gap between cron jobs becomes a direct dependency: validate_meters depends on download_meters. No gap, no assumption, no timing risk.

The second pillar is state tracking: the orchestrator records the outcome of every job execution. Did the download succeed at 00:12? Did the validation fail at 00:35? Is the aggregation still running? State tracking provides the single source of truth that SaulėGrid's team lacked when they were checking individual log files. It also enables the third pillar: failure recovery. When a task fails, the orchestrator marks it as failed, holds all downstream tasks in a waiting state, and offers mechanisms for retry — automatic retries with configurable delays, or manual retry from the UI. When the failed task is retried and succeeds, downstream tasks automatically become eligible to run.

blocks.title: The three pillars of workflow orchestration
blocks.content:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    WORKFLOW ORCHESTRATION                        │
├──────────────────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────────────┤
│   Dependency     │   State          │   Failure                │
│   Management     │   Tracking       │   Recovery               │
│                  │                  │                          │
│  'B runs after   │  Every run is    │  Failed tasks are        │
│   A succeeds'    │  recorded:       │  retried automatically   │
│                  │  success, fail,  │  or manually.            │
│  Replaces timing │  running, queued │  Downstream tasks wait.  │
│  assumptions     │                  │  Recovery is one click,  │
│  with explicit   │  Single source   │  not a manual chain      │
│  relationships   │  of truth        │  of SSH and reruns.      │
└──────────────────┴──────────────────┴──────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: All three pillars must work together. Dependency management without state tracking cannot detect failures. State tracking without failure recovery can only report problems, not fix them. Recovery without dependency awareness would rerun tasks in the wrong order.

blocks.content: Observability as a Consequence

When an orchestrator tracks dependencies and state, a powerful side effect emerges: observability. Because the orchestrator knows every task, every dependency, and every execution outcome, it can render this information visually. You can see a graph of your pipeline, color-coded by status. You can see a timeline of when each task ran and how long it took. You can see a calendar of which days succeeded and which days failed. You can click on any failed task and read its log without SSH-ing into a server. This observability is not a luxury — it is the mechanism that reduces SaulėGrid's 45-minute morning check to a 2-minute dashboard glance.
Orchestration as Code

There are two ways to define orchestrated workflows. Some tools use a graphical interface: you drag boxes onto a canvas, draw lines between them, and configure each box through a form. This works for simple workflows but becomes unwieldy as complexity grows — a pipeline with 30 tasks and conditional branches is hard to manage visually. The alternative is orchestration as code: you define your workflow in a programming language, using the same version control, code review, and testing practices you use for any other code. Apache Airflow takes the code approach. You write Python files that define tasks, dependencies, schedules, and configuration. These files are stored in Git, reviewed in pull requests, tested in CI, and deployed like any other software artifact.

blocks.title: Why code wins at scale
blocks.content: When SaulėGrid's pipeline grew from 3 tasks to 30, the code approach proved essential. A pipeline defined in code can be version-controlled (what changed last Tuesday?), reviewed (does this new dependency make sense?), duplicated (create a test version of the pipeline), and parameterized (run the same pipeline for different regions). A pipeline defined in a GUI cannot easily be diff'd, reviewed, or tested. Orchestration as code treats pipelines as software — because they are.
SaulėGrid's Requirements

After three months of cron-based pain, Matas and Ieva compiled a list of requirements for an orchestration platform. They needed dependency-aware execution so that the validation script would wait for the download to complete, not just hope that 30 minutes was enough. They needed centralized state tracking so that one dashboard could show the status of all twelve jobs. They needed automatic retries for transient failures like SFTP timeouts. They needed alerting that would notify them within minutes of a failure, not hours. And they needed all of this defined in Python code so it could live in the same Git repository as their data processing scripts. Every item on that list maps to a specific Airflow feature that you will learn in this course.

blocks.title: Orchestration is not a silver bullet
blocks.content: An orchestrator prevents dependency violations and automates recovery, but it does not fix bad data, optimize slow queries, or write correct transformation logic. If SaulėGrid's validation script has a bug that accepts negative meter readings, an orchestrator will faithfully run the buggy script on schedule. Orchestration ensures that tasks run in the right order at the right time — the quality of what those tasks do is still the engineer's responsibility.

The previous lesson defined workflow orchestration and its three pillars: dependency management, state tracking, and failure recovery. This lesson introduces the specific tool that delivers those pillars: Apache Airflow. Airflow is not the only orchestration platform — alternatives exist, and some organizations use them — but it is by far the most widely adopted orchestrator in data engineering. Understanding what Airflow is, where it came from, and why it became the industry standard is the foundation for everything else in this course.
Origin at Airbnb

Airflow was created at Airbnb in 2014 by Maxime Beauchemin, a data engineer who faced the same problems SaulėGrid faced — growing numbers of data pipelines with complex dependencies, scheduled by cron, with no centralized monitoring. Beauchemin's insight was that a workflow should be defined as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) in Python code, with each node representing a task and each edge representing a dependency. The Python definition is the single source of truth: it declares what tasks exist, how they relate to each other, and when they should run. Airflow's scheduler reads these Python files, evaluates the dependency graph, and executes tasks in the correct order.

Airbnb open-sourced Airflow in 2015, and it became an Apache Software Foundation top-level project in 2019. The open-source model attracted a large community of contributors who built integrations with databases, cloud services, messaging systems, and data processing engines. Today, Airflow has over 2,000 contributors, 40,000 GitHub stars, and integrations with every major data platform. It is the default orchestration choice at companies ranging from startups to enterprises — and it is the orchestrator that SaulėGrid adopted to replace their cron-based pipeline.
The Core Idea — Workflows as Python Code

The defining characteristic of Airflow is that workflows are defined in Python. You write a Python file that describes your tasks, their dependencies, and their schedule. Airflow reads this file, builds the dependency graph, and manages execution. You do not configure workflows in a graphical interface, in a YAML file, or in a database. You write Python. This means your workflows benefit from everything Python offers: variables, functions, loops, conditionals, libraries, version control, code review, and testing. If you know Python — which this course assumes you do — you can write Airflow workflows.

# A simplified version of SaulėGrid's Bronze ingestion DAG
# This is what an Airflow workflow looks like — it is just Python

from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime

# The DAG defines the workflow: its name, schedule, and settings
with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    schedule='@daily',           # Run once per day
    catchup=False,               # Do not backfill historical dates
) as dag:

    # Each task is one step in the pipeline
    download = PythonOperator(
        task_id='download_meters',
        python_callable=download_from_sftp,  # a Python function
    )

    validate = PythonOperator(
        task_id='validate_readings',
        python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    )

    load = PythonOperator(
        task_id='load_to_bronze',
        python_callable=write_to_bronze_layer,
    )

    # Dependencies — this is the key difference from cron
    download >> validate >> load
    # validate runs ONLY after download succeeds
    # load runs ONLY after validate succeeds

The last line of this code is the most important. The >> operator defines the dependency chain: download must complete before validate starts, and validate must complete before load starts. This single line replaces SaulėGrid's 30-minute timing assumption with an explicit guarantee. Validate will never run on a partial file because it cannot start until the download task reports success. If the download fails, validate and load remain in a waiting state — they never touch bad data. The orchestrator enforces the dependency, not the clock.
What Makes Airflow Different

Several features distinguish Airflow from other orchestration tools. First, Airflow is open source with a permissive Apache license — there is no vendor lock-in and no licensing cost. Second, Airflow has the largest ecosystem of integrations (called providers) of any orchestrator: databases, cloud storage, APIs, messaging queues, data processing engines, and more. Third, Airflow includes a web UI that provides visual monitoring of all your pipelines, with drill-down into individual task logs and execution history. Fourth, Airflow is battle-tested at massive scale — organizations run thousands of DAGs with millions of daily task executions on Airflow.

rows: Your workflows are Python files — same language, same tools, same version control
No licensing cost, large community, transparent development
Connect to any database, cloud service, or API without writing custom code
See all pipelines, task states, and logs in a browser — no SSH required
Questions get answered, bugs get fixed, features get added continuously
Cloud Composer (GCP), MWAA (AWS), and Astronomer provide hosted Airflow

blocks.content: The DAG Mental Model

Airflow uses the term DAG — Directed Acyclic Graph — for its workflows. You will learn the precise definition of a DAG in lesson 8 of this module, but the intuition is straightforward. A DAG is a collection of tasks with arrows between them showing the execution order. The arrows go in one direction (directed), and there are no loops (acyclic). SaulėGrid's download >> validate >> load is the simplest possible DAG: three tasks in a straight line. Real-world DAGs can have parallel branches, conditional paths, and dozens of tasks — but they all follow the same principle of directed, acyclic dependency relationships.

blocks.title: Why Airflow won
blocks.content: Airflow became the industry standard not because it was first (Luigi and Oozie existed earlier), not because it is the simplest (Prefect and Dagster have simpler APIs), but because it combined three things at the right time: Python-native workflow definition, a rich web UI, and an open-source community that built integrations for every platform. For SaulėGrid — and for most data engineering teams — the ecosystem and community are as important as the core features.

Airflow does not exist in isolation. It is one tool in a data stack that includes processing engines, storage systems, databases, and visualization tools. Understanding where Airflow fits — what it does and what it relies on other tools to do — prevents the common confusion of trying to use Airflow for tasks it was not designed for. Airflow is the conductor of an orchestra: it decides when each instrument plays, but it does not play the instruments itself. This lesson maps Airflow's role in SaulėGrid's data stack.
The Data Stack Layers

A modern data stack has four layers, and each layer uses different tools. The ingestion layer moves raw data from source systems into the data platform — SFTP downloads, API calls, database extracts, streaming consumers. The processing layer transforms raw data into clean, validated, enriched datasets — PySpark, SQL engines, Python scripts. The storage layer persists data at every stage — cloud storage, data lakes, warehouses, databases. The consumption layer delivers data to business users — dashboards, reports, APIs, machine learning models. Airflow sits above all four layers. It does not ingest, process, store, or serve data. It tells the tools in each layer when to run.

blocks.title: Where Airflow fits in SaulėGrid's data stack
blocks.content:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    APACHE AIRFLOW                        │
│           Orchestrates when each tool runs               │
│                                                         │
│     Triggers    Triggers     Triggers     Triggers       │
│     ingestion   processing   storage      reporting      │
└────┬──────────┬────────────┬───────────┬───────────────┘
     │          │            │           │
     ▼          ▼            ▼           ▼
┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────────┐
│Ingestion│ │Processing│ │ Storage │ │ Consumption  │
│         │ │         │ │         │ │              │
│ SFTP    │ │ PySpark │ │ S3/ADLS │ │ Dashboards   │
│ APIs    │ │ Python  │ │ Parquet │ │ Billing      │
│ DB pull │ │ SQL     │ │ Delta   │ │ VERT reports │
└─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────────┘
blocks.caption: Airflow orchestrates the entire stack but does not replace any layer. It triggers SFTP downloads, kicks off PySpark jobs, and initiates report generation — but the actual work is done by the tools in each layer.

visual-recommendation.description: Layered architecture diagram showing Airflow as the orchestration layer above four data stack layers (ingestion, processing, storage, consumption), with SaulėGrid-specific tool names in each layer and directional arrows showing trigger relationships.

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's Stack

SaulėGrid's data stack uses specific tools in each layer. For ingestion, they use Python scripts that download meter CSVs from an SFTP server, call a weather forecast REST API, and pull regulatory tariff data from the VERT API. For processing, they use PySpark for heavy transformations (cleaning 400,000 meter readings, computing regional aggregates) and plain Python for lighter tasks (parsing API responses, validating file checksums). For storage, they use a cloud data lake organized into Bronze, Silver, and Gold layers as Parquet files. For consumption, the billing team queries Gold tables directly, the operations team uses a dashboard, and the regulatory team receives automated VERT compliance reports.

Airflow's role is to ensure that these tools run in the correct order at the correct time. The SFTP download must complete before PySpark reads the file. PySpark must finish writing Silver before the aggregation job reads Silver to produce Gold. The billing report must wait for Gold to be fresh. Airflow does not download files, run PySpark transformations, or generate reports — it triggers the tools that do, tracks whether they succeeded, and decides what runs next. This separation of concerns is fundamental: Airflow handles the when and the order, not the what.
Airflow and Processing Engines

A common source of confusion for new Airflow users is the relationship between Airflow and processing engines like PySpark. Airflow can trigger a PySpark job, but Airflow does not execute PySpark code itself. When SaulėGrid's DAG has a task that runs a PySpark transformation, Airflow submits the PySpark script to the Spark cluster, monitors its status, and records whether it succeeded or failed. The actual data processing — reading Parquet files, filtering rows, computing aggregates — happens on the Spark cluster, not inside Airflow. This distinction matters because it means Airflow's resource requirements are modest: it needs enough compute to manage tasks, not to process data.

blocks.title: The conductor analogy
blocks.content: Airflow is the conductor, not the musicians. A conductor tells the violins when to start, the drums when to come in, and the whole orchestra when to stop. But the conductor does not play violin, does not hit drums, and could not produce music alone. Similarly, Airflow tells PySpark when to run and SFTP when to download, but it does not process data or transfer files itself. The conductor's value is coordination — ensuring the right thing happens at the right time in the right order.
What Airflow Replaces

At SaulėGrid, Airflow replaced three things: cron for scheduling, manual log checking for monitoring, and ad-hoc scripts for recovery. It did not replace PySpark for data processing, the SFTP client for file transfers, or the database for storage. The processing scripts that Matas and Ieva wrote are largely the same — they still download files, validate data, and compute aggregates. What changed is how those scripts are triggered, monitored, and recovered. The scripts went from isolated cron jobs into tasks within a DAG, gaining dependency awareness, state tracking, and centralized visibility without changing their internal logic.

This is a reassuring point for teams adopting Airflow: your existing Python scripts, PySpark jobs, and SQL queries do not need to be rewritten. Airflow wraps them in tasks that add orchestration capabilities around the logic you already have. The migration from cron to Airflow is primarily about defining dependencies and schedules in Python files rather than in crontab — the business logic stays the same.

blocks.title: The separation principle
blocks.content: Airflow handles orchestration. Other tools handle processing, storage, and serving. This separation keeps each tool focused on what it does best. When SaulėGrid needs faster data processing, they upgrade PySpark — not Airflow. When they need better monitoring, they upgrade Airflow — not PySpark. Each tool evolves independently because each tool has a clear, bounded responsibility.

Every lesson in this course uses the same fictional company to illustrate Airflow concepts: SaulėGrid, a Lithuanian solar energy company that manages residential and commercial solar installations, battery storage systems, and prosumer feed-in programs across four regions. SaulėGrid is not a toy example — it is modeled on the data realities of real European energy operators, scaled to a size that is large enough to create genuine orchestration challenges but small enough to understand completely. The company, its data, and its pipeline architecture will be your constant reference point throughout every module. This lesson introduces the scenario in full so that when later lessons say 'SaulėGrid's meter readings DAG,' you know exactly what that means.
The Company

SaulėGrid operates solar panel installations and battery storage systems across Lithuania. The company name comes from saulė, the Lithuanian word for sun. Unlike a traditional utility that only delivers electricity, SaulėGrid also manages prosumers — customers who both consume and generate electricity through rooftop solar panels. When a prosumer's panels generate more electricity than the household uses, the surplus is fed back into the grid and credited against their bill. This bidirectional energy flow — consumption flowing in, generation flowing out — creates data engineering challenges that a consumption-only utility does not face, and those challenges make SaulėGrid's orchestration story interesting.

SaulėGrid is part of the European Energy Data Alliance (EEDA), a consortium of energy companies that share data standards for cross-border interoperability. The EEDA standard defines common column names and types — meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type — that are identical across all member companies. This means the column names you learn in this course are the same ones used in the PySpark course (which uses NordGrid, a German wind energy company) and the Fabric course. The data standards are shared; the business domains and orchestration challenges are different.
The Four Regions

SaulėGrid divides its operations into four geographic regions: VILNIUS, KAUNAS, KLAIPEDA, and SIAULIAI. Each region has its own solar installations, battery sites, substations, and smart meters. The region code is one of the most common columns in SaulėGrid's data — it appears in meter readings, inverter telemetry, and substation records. When you see the column name region in a code example, it always contains one of these four values. The four-region structure keeps examples simple while giving you enough variety to practice grouping, filtering, and branching by region in Airflow DAGs.

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's four-region operating structure
blocks.caption: SaulėGrid's 400,000 meters and 2,000 inverters are distributed across four Lithuanian regions. VILNIUS has the highest prosumer density — a pattern that creates interesting data skew in the orchestration pipeline.

blocks.content: The Data Sources

SaulėGrid's data engineering team works with four primary data sources. Each source has a consistent schema that remains the same throughout this course. You will see these columns hundreds of times in code examples, so it is worth becoming familiar with them now. The meter readings are the most commonly used dataset — they appear in nearly every lesson from Module 3 onward.

rows: Daily energy consumption and generation from 400,000 smart meters
meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, generation_kw
Hourly output data from 2,000 solar inverters
Solar irradiance, temperature, and cloud cover predictions
JSON via REST API, every 6 hours
VERT-published feed-in tariff rates and net metering rules

blocks.content: The Team

At the junior level, SaulėGrid has one data engineering team with two engineers: Matas, who handles the ingestion and transformation pipelines, and Ieva, who manages the analytics and reporting pipelines. Together they maintain all of SaulėGrid's DAGs, connections, and data quality rules. The small team size keeps the scenario simple — you can focus on the Airflow concepts without worrying about multi-team governance or access control. In the mid-level and senior-level tracks, the team grows: additional engineers join, consumer teams emerge, and organizational complexity introduces new orchestration challenges.
The Prosumer Challenge

SaulėGrid's most distinctive data challenge is the prosumer model. A traditional utility meter records only consumption — how much electricity a household used. A prosumer meter records both consumption and generation. The daily_kw column can be positive (net consumption) or negative (net generation — the household produced more than it used and fed surplus into the grid). The generation_kw column, specific to SaulėGrid's data, tracks the total solar generation before netting. This bidirectional data flow creates orchestration dependencies that a consumption-only utility does not have: the billing DAG depends on the net metering balance, which depends on both the generation forecast and the actual meter readings.

blocks.title: Why a consistent scenario matters
blocks.content: Using the same company, the same column names, and the same pipeline throughout the course means you build familiarity over time. By Module 5, you will not need to read the column descriptions — you will know that meter_id identifies a meter, daily_kw is the daily energy reading in kilowatts, and region is one of VILNIUS, KAUNAS, KLAIPEDA, or SIAULIAI. This familiarity frees your mental energy to focus on the new Airflow concept each lesson teaches, rather than constantly adapting to unfamiliar data.

sections.title: Bronze, Silver, and Gold at SaulėGrid

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's data does not go directly from a smart meter to a billing report. Raw meter readings arrive as messy CSV files: some rows have null meter IDs from decommissioned sensors, generation_kw is sometimes negative from miscalibrated inverters, and date formats vary between regional SFTP servers. Before any business process can use this data, it must be cleaned, validated, and transformed. SaulėGrid organizes this work into three layers — Bronze, Silver, and Gold — where each layer has a specific job and a clear contract about what the data looks like. This pattern is called the medallion architecture, and it is the structural backbone that SaulėGrid's Airflow DAGs orchestrate.
Bronze — Raw Ingestion

The Bronze layer is the landing zone for raw data. It stores data exactly as it arrived from the source, with no transformations, no cleaning, and no filtering. SaulėGrid's Bronze layer receives the nightly meter CSV files from the SFTP server, the hourly solar inverter Parquet files, the weather forecast JSON from the external API, and the weekly tariff data from the VERT regulatory API. The only operation in the Bronze layer is copying: the raw file is written to storage as-is. If a meter exports a negative daily_kw because of a sensor malfunction, that negative value appears in Bronze. If a CSV column contains a date formatted as '2026/03/22' instead of the standard '2026-03-22,' Bronze stores it unchanged.

The purpose of keeping raw data untouched is debuggability. When something goes wrong in a downstream transformation — a billing report shows unexpected values, or a generation forecast is off — the team can always go back to Bronze and look at exactly what arrived from the source. If Bronze had already been cleaned, the original evidence would be lost. Bronze is the audit trail, the source of truth, the 'before' picture that makes every 'after' picture verifiable. In Airflow terms, the Bronze layer is populated by ingestion DAGs — the first DAGs to run each night.
Silver — Cleaned and Validated

The Silver layer contains data that has been cleaned, validated, and converted to a consistent format. When SaulėGrid's Silver pipeline reads the raw meter CSV from Bronze, it performs a series of transformations: null meter IDs are removed, daily_kw values are cast from strings to numeric types, date formats are standardized to ISO 8601, and generation_kw values are separated from consumption values so that downstream processes can work with each independently. The output is written as Parquet files — a columnar format that is faster to read and smaller to store than CSV.

blocks.title: The medallion architecture at SaulėGrid
blocks.content:
Raw Sources               Bronze               Silver               Gold
┌─────────────┐       ┌───────────┐     ┌────────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│ SFTP meters │ copy  │ Raw CSV   │     │ Validated      │  │ Regional     │
│ SFTP invert.│ as-is │ Raw Parq  │ ──▶ │ Typed          │──▶│ summaries    │
│ Weather API │       │ Raw JSON  │     │ Separated      │  │ Billing      │
│ VERT API    │       │ Raw JSON  │     │ (consumption   │  │ Forecasts    │
└─────────────┘       └───────────┘     │  vs generation)│  │ VERT reports │
                                        └────────────────┘  └──────────────┘
                      No changes        PySpark cleans,     PySpark          
                      Exact copy        validates, casts,   aggregates,      
                                        separates flows     joins, computes  
                                                            business metrics 

                      Debuggable        Reliable             Business-ready  

Airflow DAG:         dag_bronze_meters  dag_silver_transform dag_gold_aggregate
blocks.caption: Each layer has a clear purpose and a dedicated Airflow DAG. Bronze preserves raw evidence. Silver produces reliable, typed, validated data. Gold produces business-ready outputs. Airflow ensures that each layer runs only after its upstream layer is complete.

visual-recommendation.description: Three-column architecture diagram showing Bronze, Silver, and Gold layers with SaulėGrid-specific data sources, transformation descriptions, and the Airflow DAG names that orchestrate each layer.

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's Silver layer also handles the prosumer data split. In the raw meter file, consumption and generation are combined in a single row. The Silver transformation separates them: consumption readings go into one table, generation readings go into another, and the net metering balance (consumption minus generation) is calculated and stored separately. This separation is critical because the billing pipeline needs consumption data, the generation forecast pipeline needs generation data, and the regulatory reporting pipeline needs the net balance. Without the Silver layer's separation, each downstream pipeline would have to repeat the same splitting logic — a duplication that invites inconsistency.
Gold — Business-Ready Aggregates

The Gold layer contains the final, business-ready datasets. These are not raw records — they are aggregated, joined, and computed outputs designed for specific consumers. SaulėGrid's Gold layer includes regional consumption summaries (total and average daily_kw per region), billing aggregates (monthly totals per customer with prosumer credits applied), generation forecasts (predicted output based on weather and historical patterns), and VERT regulatory reports (quarterly compliance submissions). Each Gold table serves a specific business audience and is computed by joining Silver data with reference tables and applying business rules.
Why Layers Help Orchestration

The three-layer architecture makes orchestration cleaner because each layer has a single responsibility and a clear dependency. The Bronze DAG depends only on external sources (SFTP, APIs). The Silver DAG depends only on Bronze. The Gold DAG depends only on Silver. This creates a linear dependency chain that is easy to define in Airflow: dag_bronze >> dag_silver >> dag_gold. If the Bronze DAG fails, Silver and Gold automatically wait. If Silver completes but Gold fails, Bronze and Silver do not need to rerun — only Gold is retried. The layers create natural checkpoints in the pipeline that simplify both orchestration and recovery.

blocks.title: Layers as contracts
blocks.content: Each layer makes a promise to the next. Bronze promises that the data is an exact copy of the source. Silver promises that the data is clean, typed, and validated. Gold promises that the data is business-ready and up to date. When a layer breaks its promise — Silver produces data with null meter IDs that should have been filtered — the problem is isolated to that layer. Debugging starts at the layer that broke its contract, not across the entire pipeline.
The PySpark Connection

The actual data transformations within each layer — filtering nulls, casting types, computing aggregates, joining tables — are performed by PySpark or Python scripts. Airflow does not execute these transformations itself. Airflow's DAGs trigger the PySpark jobs, wait for them to finish, check whether they succeeded, and then trigger the next layer. If you have taken the PySpark Junior-Level Track (which uses NordGrid Energy), the Bronze-Silver-Gold transformations will feel familiar — the same medallion pattern, the same kinds of operations, the same Parquet output format. The difference is the orchestration: in the PySpark course, you learned what the transformations do; in this course, you learn how to ensure they run in the right order, at the right time, with the right recovery behavior.

blocks.title: Common confusion
blocks.content: New Airflow users sometimes try to write transformation logic inside Airflow tasks — reading files, cleaning data, computing aggregates all within the DAG code. This works for small datasets but is an anti-pattern at production scale. Airflow's tasks should trigger external processing engines (PySpark, SQL, dbt), not replace them. The DAG code should be lightweight: call the PySpark job, check if it succeeded, move on. SaulėGrid's DAG files are typically 50–100 lines of orchestration logic, while the PySpark scripts they trigger are hundreds of lines of transformation logic.

Every workflow in Airflow is called a DAG — a Directed Acyclic Graph. You have seen the term several times in this module, and lesson 4 showed what a simple DAG looks like in code. This lesson explains the concept precisely. Understanding what directed, acyclic, and graph mean — and why each property matters for orchestration — is the foundation for Module 3, where you will learn to create, configure, and manage DAGs in detail.
What Is a Graph?

In computer science, a graph is a collection of nodes connected by edges. Nodes represent things; edges represent relationships between them. In Airflow, each node is a task (a unit of work), and each edge is a dependency (a relationship that says 'this task runs after that task'). SaulėGrid's Bronze DAG has three nodes — download_meters, validate_readings, load_to_bronze — and two edges — download to validate, validate to load. The graph structure makes the relationships between tasks explicit and visual.
What Does Directed Mean?

Directed means the edges have a direction — they point from one task to another, not both ways. In SaulėGrid's DAG, the edge from download to validate means 'validate runs after download.' It does not mean 'download runs after validate.' Direction matters because orchestration is about order: certain tasks must happen before others. If edges had no direction, the scheduler would not know which task to run first. The >> operator in Airflow code defines direction: download >> validate means the edge points from download to validate.
What Does Acyclic Mean?

Acyclic means there are no cycles — no path that leads back to where it started. If task A depends on B, and B depends on C, and C depends on A, you have a cycle: A → B → C → A → B → C → forever. A cycle is an infinite loop — the scheduler could never determine which task to run first because each task is waiting for another. Airflow enforces the acyclic property when it parses your DAG file. If you accidentally create a circular dependency, Airflow will reject the DAG with an error rather than running it. This is a safety mechanism, not a limitation — cycles in a dependency graph are always a design error.

blocks.title: A valid DAG vs an invalid cycle
blocks.content:
Valid DAG (no cycles — Airflow accepts this):

  download_meters ──▶ validate_readings ──▶ load_to_bronze

  Execution order is clear:
  1. download_meters
  2. validate_readings
  3. load_to_bronze


Invalid cycle (Airflow rejects this):

  download_meters ──▶ validate_readings ──▶ load_to_bronze
       ▲                                        │
       └────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Execution order is impossible:
  download waits for load, load waits for validate,
  validate waits for download — deadlock.
blocks.caption: The acyclic property guarantees that the scheduler can always find at least one task with no unresolved dependencies. That task runs first, resolves a dependency, and the process repeats until all tasks are complete.

blocks.content: Why DAGs Fit Orchestration

DAGs are not an Airflow invention — they are a mathematical structure that has been used in computer science for decades to model dependency relationships. Compilers use DAGs to determine the order in which to compile modules. Build systems like Make and Gradle use DAGs to determine which files need to be rebuilt. Spreadsheets use DAGs to determine the order in which to recalculate cells when a value changes. The common thread is dependencies: whenever you have tasks that depend on other tasks, and you need to find a valid execution order, a DAG is the natural representation.

For data pipeline orchestration, DAGs provide three specific benefits. First, the dependency relationships are explicit — you can see which task depends on which by looking at the graph, rather than inferring dependencies from timing gaps. Second, the scheduler can automatically determine a valid execution order by finding tasks with no unresolved dependencies and running them first (this is called topological sorting). Third, the graph structure supports parallelism: tasks that do not depend on each other can run simultaneously, and the scheduler identifies these opportunities automatically.
Beyond Linear Chains

SaulėGrid's Bronze DAG is a simple chain: download >> validate >> load. But DAGs can be much more complex. SaulėGrid's Silver DAG, for example, reads validated meter data and branches into three parallel transformations: consumption aggregation, generation aggregation, and battery state computation. These three tasks do not depend on each other — they all depend on the validated data, but they can run simultaneously. After all three complete, a final task combines the results. This is a diamond-shaped DAG: one task fans out to three, and three fan back in to one.

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's Silver DAG — a diamond pattern
blocks.content:
validate_silver
                         │
              ┌──────────┼───────────┐
              ▼          ▼           ▼
        aggregate    aggregate    compute
        consumption  generation   battery_soc
              │          │           │
              └──────────┼───────────┘
                         ▼
                    write_silver
blocks.caption: Three tasks run in parallel after validation, then converge before writing. The scheduler automatically identifies the parallel opportunity — the engineer only needs to define the dependencies.

visual-recommendation.description: Diamond-shaped DAG visualization showing SaulėGrid's Silver pipeline with color-coded task nodes and directional edges, demonstrating fan-out and fan-in patterns.

blocks.title: Key takeaway
blocks.content: A DAG is a collection of tasks connected by directed, non-circular dependencies. The directed property establishes order. The acyclic property prevents infinite loops. The graph structure makes relationships visual and enables automatic parallel execution. Every DAG you will write in this course — from SaulėGrid's simplest Bronze ingestion to its most complex multi-branch Silver transformation — follows these three properties.

When you first encounter Airflow, it is tempting to see it as a universal tool for running any kind of automated task. Airflow is powerful, but it has a specific purpose: orchestrating batch workflows. Understanding what Airflow does not do is as important as understanding what it does — it prevents you from trying to use Airflow in ways that lead to frustration and poor architecture. This lesson draws clear boundaries around Airflow's role.
Airflow Is Not a Data Processing Engine

Airflow triggers data processing jobs, but it does not process data itself. When SaulėGrid needs to clean 400,000 meter readings, Airflow does not load the CSV, filter rows, cast types, or write Parquet. It calls a PySpark script that does those things, monitors the script's progress, and records whether it succeeded or failed. The actual data manipulation happens in PySpark, SQL, Python, or whatever processing tool the task invokes. Trying to use Airflow itself for heavy data processing — reading large files inside PythonOperator callables, transforming DataFrames within DAG tasks — is an anti-pattern because Airflow's workers are designed for orchestration overhead, not compute-intensive processing.
Airflow Is Not a Streaming Platform

Airflow processes batch workflows — pipelines that run on a schedule or on demand, process a finite dataset, and complete. It is not designed for continuous streaming where data arrives constantly and must be processed in real time. SaulėGrid's meter readings arrive once per day as a batch file — a perfect Airflow use case. If SaulėGrid needed to process individual meter readings as they arrived every second, Airflow would be the wrong tool. Streaming platforms like Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, or Spark Structured Streaming are designed for that pattern. Airflow can orchestrate streaming infrastructure — starting a Kafka consumer, checking if a Flink job is healthy — but it does not process the stream itself.
Airflow Is Not a Cron Replacement

This statement might seem contradictory after spending two lessons explaining why cron is inadequate for data pipelines. But the distinction is important: Airflow replaces cron for orchestrating complex workflows with dependencies. For simple, isolated tasks that genuinely have no dependencies — rotating log files, cleaning temporary directories, sending a weekly reminder email — cron is perfectly adequate and much simpler than Airflow. Airflow adds value when tasks have relationships: dependencies, retry logic, shared state, and centralized monitoring. Using Airflow for tasks that have no dependencies is like using a database when a text file would suffice — it works, but the overhead is not justified.

left.label: Good Airflow Use Cases
points: Multi-step data pipelines with dependencies between steps
Scheduled batch ETL from sources to warehouse
Triggering and monitoring external jobs (Spark, SQL, API calls)
Complex schedules with backfill and catchup logic
Centralized monitoring and alerting for pipeline health

right.label: Better Handled by Other Tools
points: Real-time stream processing (use Kafka, Flink)
Heavy data transformations (use PySpark, dbt)
Storing and querying data (use a database or warehouse)
Simple isolated scheduled tasks with no dependencies (use cron)
Application health monitoring (use Prometheus, Grafana)

blocks.content: Airflow Is Not a Data Store

Airflow has a metadata database that stores information about DAG runs, task states, connections, and variables. This database is an internal component that Airflow uses to track orchestration state — it is not a place to store business data. SaulėGrid does not store meter readings in Airflow's metadata database. The meter data lives in the data lake (Bronze, Silver, Gold layers). Airflow's database stores the fact that dag_bronze_meters ran successfully on March 22nd and took 12 minutes. The data flows through Airflow's tasks, but the data itself is stored in the data platform — not in Airflow.

blocks.title: A tempting anti-pattern
blocks.content: Airflow's XCom feature (which you will learn about in Module 5) allows tasks to pass small amounts of data between them. New Airflow users sometimes use XCom to pass entire datasets — DataFrames, large JSON objects, complete file contents — between tasks. This overwhelms Airflow's metadata database and degrades performance. XCom is designed for small values: file paths, row counts, status flags. Large data should be written to storage by one task and read from storage by the next.
The Boundary Summary

Airflow's boundary is clear: it handles the when, the order, the monitoring, and the recovery of pipeline steps. It does not handle the what — the actual data processing, storage, or serving. This boundary is by design, not a limitation. By focusing on orchestration and delegating processing to specialized tools, Airflow stays lightweight, fast, and reliable. SaulėGrid's Airflow installation manages 12 DAGs with dozens of tasks, runs on a modest server, and uses a fraction of the resources that the PySpark cluster consumes. The orchestrator should be the lightest component in your data stack, not the heaviest.

blocks.title: Remember the conductor
blocks.content: Airflow is the conductor: it coordinates the orchestra but does not play the instruments. PySpark plays the heavy data processing. Cloud storage holds the data. Dashboards serve the business users. Airflow ensures they all work together at the right time, in the right order, with visibility into what succeeded and what failed.

The previous nine lessons introduced the core concepts: the fragility of unorchestrated pipelines, the failure modes that cron-based scheduling cannot prevent, the three pillars of orchestration, Apache Airflow as the platform that delivers those pillars, Airflow's position in the data stack, the SaulėGrid scenario, the Bronze-Silver-Gold architecture, the DAG as the central abstraction, and the clear boundaries of what Airflow does and does not do. This lesson takes a step back and previews the rest of the course, showing how each module maps to a specific need in SaulėGrid's orchestration journey.
What You Know Now

You know why orchestration exists: because time-based scheduling cannot express dependencies, track state, or recover from failures. You know what Airflow is: an open-source, Python-native orchestration platform that defines workflows as DAGs. You know where Airflow fits: above the processing, storage, and consumption layers, coordinating when each tool runs without replacing any of them. And you know SaulėGrid's story: a Lithuanian solar energy company whose growing pipeline complexity demanded something better than cron. These foundations are the context for everything that follows.
The Course Structure

The remaining nine modules build systematically on this foundation. They are organized into four phases. Modules 1 and 2 are the foundation phase — you have completed Module 1, and Module 2 introduces Airflow's architecture: the scheduler, web server, metadata database, and executor. Modules 3 through 5 are the core mechanics phase: DAGs in depth, tasks and operators, and the TaskFlow API. Modules 6 through 8 are the essential operations phase: dependencies and control flow, scheduling and time, and connections to external systems. Modules 9 and 10 are the monitoring and synthesis phase: the Airflow UI and complete SaulėGrid pipelines.

rows: Why orchestration exists, how Airflow's architecture works
The problems that motivated Airflow adoption; the components running SaulėGrid's Airflow instance
Writing SaulėGrid's first DAGs; choosing operators for each task type
SaulėGrid's dependency graph; scheduling strategy; SFTP, API, and database connections
Daily monitoring workflow; full Bronze-Silver-Gold pipeline with error handling

blocks.content: Module 2 — The Airflow Architecture

The next module introduces the four components that run every Airflow installation: the scheduler that decides when tasks are ready, the web server that renders the monitoring UI, the metadata database that stores pipeline state, and the executor that runs your task code. Understanding these components is necessary before writing DAGs because you need to know where your code runs, how state is tracked, and what happens when a component fails. Module 2 also introduces SaulėGrid's development and production environments and traces a complete DAG Run through every component.
Modules 3–5 — The Core Toolkit

Module 3 teaches DAGs in full detail: creation, parameterization, parsing, state management, and organization. Module 4 teaches tasks and operators — the units of work inside a DAG, including the BashOperator for shell commands, the PythonOperator for Python functions, and the EmptyOperator for organizational checkpoints. Module 5 introduces the TaskFlow API, a modern Python-native way to write Airflow tasks using function decorators that automatically handle data passing between tasks. After completing these three modules, you will be able to write, read, and understand any Airflow DAG.
Modules 6–8 — Making Pipelines Reliable

Module 6 covers dependency patterns: chains, fan-out, fan-in, branching, trigger rules, and conditional execution. Module 7 addresses scheduling and time: cron expressions, data intervals, logical dates, catchup, backfilling, and timezone handling. Module 8 introduces connections (how Airflow authenticates to external systems), hooks (Python wrappers around connections), variables (pipeline configuration), and provider packages (community-built integrations). These three modules transform a basic DAG into a production-ready pipeline with proper scheduling, dependency management, and external system integration.
Modules 9–10 — Monitoring and Synthesis

Module 9 teaches the Airflow web UI in depth: the DAG list, Grid View, Graph View, Gantt chart, Calendar View, log viewer, and a systematic troubleshooting workflow. Module 10 brings everything together by building SaulėGrid's complete orchestration platform — Bronze, Silver, and Gold DAGs with cross-DAG dependencies, error handling, logging, alerting, and documentation. The final lesson of Module 10 is the course closer, synthesizing every concept into a single narrative and bridging to the mid-level track.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 2, you will learn how Airflow's four components work together to execute a DAG. You will see the scheduler scan the DAG directory, the executor pick up tasks, and the web server render the result. The orchestration concepts from this module will become concrete: dependencies become task instances with tracked states, and recovery becomes a button in the web UI. The foundation is set. Now it is time to build on it.

chapters.title: The Airflow Architecture
chapters.description: Understand the four core components of Airflow (scheduler, web server, metadata database, executor), how they collaborate to execute pipelines, and how the architecture scales from local development to production deployment.

blocks.content: Module 1 introduced Airflow as an orchestration platform that manages dependencies, tracks state, and recovers from failures. But Airflow is not a single program — it is a system built from four distinct components that collaborate to run your pipelines. Each component has a specific job, runs as its own process, and can be deployed on its own machine. Understanding these four components is essential before you write your first DAG, because when something goes wrong — a task is stuck, a DAG is not appearing, the UI is slow — the fix depends on knowing which component is responsible.
The Four Components

Every Airflow installation consists of four core components: the scheduler, the web server, the metadata database, and the executor. The scheduler is the brain — it continuously scans DAG files, determines which tasks are ready to run, and submits them to the executor. The web server is the face — it renders the monitoring UI where you view DAG status, inspect logs, and trigger manual runs. The metadata database is the memory — it stores every DAG run, task instance, connection, variable, and configuration that Airflow needs to track. The executor is the muscle — it actually runs your task code on one or more workers.

blocks.title: The four core components of Airflow
blocks.caption: The metadata database sits at the center — every other component reads from it and writes to it. The scheduler writes task state, the executor updates task results, and the web server reads everything to render the UI.

visual-recommendation.description: Architecture diagram showing the four Airflow components with directional arrows indicating communication paths, the metadata database in the center, and SaulėGrid-specific labels for DAGs being processed.

blocks.content: The SaulėGrid Operations Center Analogy

Think of SaulėGrid's operations center. The dispatch board shows which field tasks are scheduled for today and which crews are assigned to each — this is the scheduler. The monitoring screen on the wall shows live status of every active task with color-coded indicators — this is the web server. The logbook records every task that was completed, every issue that occurred, and every decision that was made — this is the metadata database. The field crews drive to the sites, perform the work, and call in their results — these are the executor's workers. No single component can run the operations center alone. The dispatch board without field crews produces plans that nobody executes. Field crews without the dispatch board do not know what to work on. The monitoring screen without the logbook has nothing to display.
Why Separate Components?

Airflow could have been built as a single program that does everything — scan DAGs, run tasks, serve the UI, and store state in memory. Early prototyping tools sometimes work this way. But the four-component design provides three critical advantages. First, each component can be scaled independently: if the UI is slow, you scale the web server without touching the scheduler. Second, each component can fail independently: if the web server crashes, the scheduler continues running tasks — you lose monitoring temporarily but not execution. Third, each component can be deployed on separate machines in production: the scheduler and executor on powerful compute nodes, the web server on a lightweight web tier, and the database on a managed database service.

rows: Scans DAGs, evaluates dependencies, queues tasks
No new tasks are scheduled. Running tasks continue.
Renders the monitoring UI, serves the REST API
No UI access. Scheduler and executor are unaffected.
Stores all state: DAG runs, task instances, connections
All components stall. Nothing works without state.
Runs task code on one or more workers
Depends on executor type (local process, Celery, Kubernetes)
No tasks execute. Scheduler queues tasks but nothing picks them up.

blocks.content: The Metadata Database Is Central

Notice that the metadata database is the only component that every other component depends on. The scheduler writes task state to the database. The executor reads queued tasks from the database and writes results back. The web server queries the database to render the UI. If the database goes down, everything stops — the scheduler cannot record state, the executor cannot report results, and the web server has nothing to show. This makes the database the most critical component for reliability. SaulėGrid runs their metadata database on a managed PostgreSQL service with automated backups and failover, because a database outage means a complete Airflow outage.

blocks.title: Key mental model
blocks.content: The scheduler decides. The executor does. The database remembers. The web server shows. When debugging, ask: is the problem about decisions (scheduler), execution (executor), memory (database), or visibility (web server)? The answer tells you which component to investigate.
What Comes Next

The next four lessons examine each component in detail: what it does, how it works, and what happens when it fails. After that, lessons 7 through 10 show how the components collaborate — the communication flow, the development versus production deployment, the lifecycle of a DAG run, and the failure scenarios that every Airflow operator should recognize. By the end of this module, you will have a complete mental model of the machine that runs SaulėGrid's pipelines.

The scheduler is the heart of Airflow — the component that makes orchestration work. Without the scheduler, Airflow would be a collection of Python files that describe pipelines but never run them. The scheduler continuously reads your DAG files, builds the dependency graph in memory, checks which tasks are eligible to run, and submits eligible tasks to the executor. It runs in an infinite loop, waking up every few seconds to re-evaluate the state of every DAG. This lesson explains how the scheduler loop works and why its behavior matters for SaulėGrid's pipeline reliability.
The Scheduler Loop

The scheduler operates in a continuous cycle with three phases. In the first phase, it reads all Python files from the DAG directory and parses them to discover DAG objects. Any new DAGs are registered; any changed DAGs are updated; any deleted files remove the corresponding DAGs. In the second phase, the scheduler evaluates each active DAG against its schedule: has enough time passed since the last run? If so, the scheduler creates a new DAG Run — a record in the metadata database representing one execution of the DAG. In the third phase, the scheduler examines every task in every active DAG Run, checks whether each task's dependencies are satisfied (all upstream tasks succeeded), and queues the eligible tasks for the executor.

blocks.title: The scheduler's three-phase loop
blocks.caption: The scheduler never stops. It parses, schedules, and queues tasks in a continuous loop. A faster loop means tasks are picked up sooner; a slower loop reduces CPU usage on the scheduler machine.

visual-recommendation.description: Animated loop diagram showing the scheduler cycling through parse, schedule, and queue phases with SaulėGrid DAG names appearing as they are discovered and tasks being queued.

blocks.content: Parsing DAG Files

The scheduler does not just read your Python files — it executes them. When the scheduler processes dag_bronze_meters.py, it runs the file as a Python module. The file's top-level code executes: import statements run, variables are assigned, and the DAG object is created in memory. The scheduler then extracts the DAG object and stores its structure (tasks, dependencies, schedule) in the metadata database. This parsing happens every scheduler loop iteration, which means your DAG files are executed repeatedly — every few seconds for the entire lifetime of the Airflow installation.

blocks.title: Top-level code runs on every parse
blocks.content: Because the scheduler executes your DAG file on every loop, any heavy code at the top level runs repeatedly. If your file imports a large library, reads a configuration file, or makes an API call at module level, that operation happens every few seconds — not just once. This is the most common performance mistake new Airflow users make. Keep your DAG file lightweight: imports, DAG definition, and task definitions. Heavy logic belongs inside task callables, not at module level.
Creating DAG Runs

When the scheduler determines that a DAG is due for execution — the current time has passed the next scheduled interval — it creates a DAG Run in the metadata database. A DAG Run is a specific execution instance of a DAG, identified by a logical date (which you will learn about in Module 7). SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters runs daily, so the scheduler creates one DAG Run per day. Each DAG Run starts in the queued state, transitions to running when its first task begins, and ends in success or failed when all tasks have completed.
Evaluating Dependencies

The scheduler's most important job is dependency evaluation. For each task in an active DAG Run, the scheduler checks: have all upstream tasks completed successfully? If task B depends on task A, the scheduler will not queue B until A's state is success. This is the fundamental mechanism that replaced SaulėGrid's 30-minute timing assumptions — the scheduler enforces the dependency graph, not the clock. If SaulėGrid's download_meters task takes 45 minutes instead of the usual 10, the validate_readings task simply waits. No timing gap, no assumption, no risk of processing partial data.
Scheduler Configuration

Two configuration settings control the scheduler's behavior at a level that junior engineers should be aware of. The parsing interval (scheduler_heartbeat_sec) determines how often the scheduler re-parses DAG files — the default is 5 seconds, which means a new DAG file appears in Airflow within seconds of being placed in the DAG directory. The minimum file process interval (min_file_process_interval) sets the minimum time between re-parsing the same file — the default is 30 seconds, which prevents the scheduler from wasting CPU on files that have not changed. SaulėGrid uses the defaults for both, which is appropriate for their 12-DAG installation.

blocks.title: Why the scheduler matters most
blocks.content: If the web server goes down, you lose visibility but pipelines keep running. If a worker goes down, one task fails but others continue. If the scheduler goes down, nothing new gets scheduled — every pipeline stops progressing. The scheduler is the single most critical component in a production Airflow installation. SaulėGrid runs the scheduler with automatic restart on failure and monitors its heartbeat with an external health check.

The web server is the component you will interact with most directly as an Airflow user. It serves the web-based user interface where you view DAG status, inspect task logs, trigger manual runs, manage connections, and configure variables. The web server is a Flask application served by Gunicorn, and it communicates exclusively with the metadata database — it does not talk to the scheduler or executor directly. Everything the web server displays is a reflection of what is stored in the database. This lesson introduces the web server's role and capabilities; Module 9 provides a complete guide to using the UI.
What the Web Server Provides

The web server provides four categories of functionality. First, it provides pipeline visualization: you can see every DAG as a graph of tasks, color-coded by status. Second, it provides operational monitoring: you can see which DAG Runs are active, which tasks are running or failed, and how long each task took. Third, it provides debugging tools: you can click on any task instance to read its log output, see its execution timeline, and inspect its parameters. Fourth, it provides management capabilities: you can trigger DAG Runs manually, pause and unpause DAGs, clear failed task instances for retry, and manage connections and variables.

rows: Matas drew pipeline diagrams on a whiteboard
Checking individual log files via SSH
Navigating to each script's log directory
Four different log directories on the server
SSH into server, run scripts manually in order
1-3 hour manual recovery process per incident
Hardcoded credentials in scripts or env files
SFTP password in three different config files

blocks.content: How the Web Server Works

The web server is a read-heavy component. When you open the DAG list page, the web server queries the metadata database for all DAGs and their recent run statuses, then renders an HTML page with that information. When you click on a task instance's log, the web server reads the log file from the configured log storage location (local filesystem or cloud storage) and displays it in the browser.
