When you trigger a manual DAG Run, the web server inserts a record into the metadata database — the scheduler picks it up on its next loop and begins scheduling the DAG Run's tasks.

This architecture means the web server has no direct control over task execution. It cannot start a task, stop a task, or change a task's state without going through the database. When you click 'Clear' on a failed task (which triggers a retry), the web server updates the task instance's state in the database to 'scheduled.' The scheduler sees this state change on its next loop and re-queues the task for the executor. The web server is a control panel, not an engine — it sends signals through the database that the scheduler and executor respond to.
The REST API
Beyond the Browser

In addition to the browser-based UI, the web server exposes a REST API that provides programmatic access to the same capabilities. You can trigger DAG Runs, query task states, manage connections, and read logs through HTTP requests. SaulėGrid does not use the API at the junior level, but it becomes important in the mid-level track for CI/CD integration and automated pipeline testing. For now, knowing that the API exists is sufficient — the browser UI is the primary interface for junior engineers.
Web Server Independence

The web server is the most independent component in the Airflow architecture. If the web server stops, the scheduler continues scheduling, the executor continues running tasks, and the database continues storing state. You lose the ability to monitor and manage pipelines through the UI, but the pipelines themselves are unaffected. This independence is by design — monitoring should not be a single point of failure for execution. SaulėGrid experienced this firsthand when a web server restart during a deployment briefly made the UI unavailable. Ieva checked the logs afterward and confirmed that all three nightly DAGs had run on schedule without interruption.

blocks.title: The web server is a window, not a door
blocks.content: The web server lets you see what is happening (monitoring) and request changes (triggering, clearing). But the actual execution happens in the scheduler and executor. If the UI shows a task as 'running,' that status comes from the database — the web server is reporting what the executor told the database, not observing the task directly. This indirection means the UI can occasionally show stale state if the database update is delayed by a few seconds.

blocks.title: Do not confuse the UI with the scheduler
blocks.content: A common mistake is thinking that pausing a DAG in the UI stops the scheduler from processing it. Pausing a DAG tells the scheduler not to create new DAG Runs, but already-running DAG Runs continue to completion. Similarly, toggling a DAG on does not immediately trigger a run — it enables the scheduler to create the next scheduled run when the time comes. The UI sends requests; the scheduler decides what to do with them.

The metadata database is Airflow's memory. Every DAG definition, every DAG Run, every task instance, every connection, every variable, and every configuration setting is stored in a relational database — typically PostgreSQL in production or SQLite in local development. Without the database, Airflow would have no way to track which tasks have run, which are pending, or which have failed. The scheduler would not know whether to create a new DAG Run. The web server would have nothing to display. The executor would not know which tasks to pick up. The database is the component that makes all other components stateful.
What the Database Stores

The metadata database contains dozens of tables, but five are most important for understanding how Airflow works. The dag table stores the metadata for every DAG: its ID, schedule, last parse time, and whether it is paused. The dag_run table stores every execution of every DAG: the DAG ID, the logical date, the state (queued, running, success, failed), and the start and end timestamps. The task_instance table stores every execution of every task: the DAG ID, the task ID, the DAG Run ID, the state, the start and end time, the retry count, and the log reference. The connection table stores credentials for external systems. The variable table stores key-value configuration pairs.

rows: DAG metadata: ID, schedule, pause state
Each execution: logical date, state, timing
Each task execution: state, retries, log path

blocks.content: State as the Foundation

The database's most important role is tracking task instance state. When the scheduler queues a task, it creates a task_instance row with state 'scheduled.' When the executor picks up the task, the state changes to 'queued,' then 'running.' When the task completes, the state changes to 'success' or 'failed.' Every state transition is written to the database, which means the complete execution history of every task in every DAG Run is permanently recorded. SaulėGrid can look up whether dag_bronze_meters succeeded on any specific date, how long each task took, how many retries were needed, and what the log output was — all from the database.
SQLite vs PostgreSQL
Database Backend Options

Airflow supports two database backends. SQLite is the default for local development: it stores the entire database in a single file, requires no separate database server, and works out of the box. SQLite is convenient but limited — it does not support concurrent writes, which means only one scheduler process can run at a time. PostgreSQL (or MySQL) is the production choice: it runs as a separate server, supports concurrent access from multiple scheduler and web server processes, and provides the reliability features (replication, backups, failover) that production systems require.

left.label: SQLite (development)
points: Single file on disk — no server needed
No concurrent writes — one scheduler only
No backups or replication built in
Perfect for learning and local testing
SaulėGrid uses for: development laptops

right.label: PostgreSQL (production)
points: Dedicated database server — requires setup
Full concurrency — multiple schedulers supported
Automated backups, point-in-time recovery, replication
Required for production reliability
SaulėGrid uses for: production Airflow instance

blocks.content: Why the Database Is the Weakest Link

The metadata database is the only component that cannot be replaced while Airflow is running. If the scheduler crashes, a watchdog process can restart it in seconds and it resumes where it left off (because state is in the database). If the web server crashes, a restart brings it back with no data loss. If a worker crashes, the executor reschedules the failed task. But if the database is unreachable, every component stalls immediately — the scheduler cannot read or write state, the executor cannot update task results, and the web server returns errors. This makes the database the highest-priority component for operational reliability.

blocks.title: Database is not for business data
blocks.content: The metadata database stores Airflow's operational state — DAG runs, task instances, connections. It does not store your pipeline's data. SaulėGrid's meter readings live in the data lake, not in Airflow's PostgreSQL instance. Trying to store large datasets in the metadata database (through XCom or custom tables) degrades Airflow's performance because the scheduler and web server query this database constantly. Keep the metadata database lean: operational state only.

blocks.title: Key mental model
blocks.content: The metadata database is the source of truth for everything Airflow knows. If a task's state is 'success' in the database, it is success — regardless of what the log says, what the executor remembers, or what you think happened. When debugging, the database state is the authoritative answer.

The executor is the component that actually runs your task code. When the scheduler determines that a task is eligible — all upstream dependencies are satisfied — it places the task in a queue. The executor picks tasks from this queue and runs them on one or more workers. The executor's behavior varies dramatically depending on which executor type is configured: some executors run tasks as local processes, others distribute tasks across a cluster of worker machines, and others spin up a new container for every task. Understanding the executor types is essential because the choice affects parallelism, isolation, and how SaulėGrid scales from development to production.
SequentialExecutor

The SequentialExecutor is the simplest executor: it runs one task at a time, in sequence, within the scheduler process itself. If three tasks are queued, the executor runs the first, waits for it to finish, runs the second, waits again, and runs the third. No parallelism, no separate workers, no distributed processing. The SequentialExecutor is the default when Airflow is initialized with SQLite, and it exists for one purpose: letting you learn Airflow without setting up any infrastructure. It is never used in production because it cannot run tasks in parallel — a pipeline with three independent tasks takes three times as long as it should.
LocalExecutor

The LocalExecutor runs tasks as separate processes on the same machine as the scheduler. When the scheduler queues three independent tasks, the LocalExecutor spawns three Python processes simultaneously, each running one task's code. The tasks run in parallel, limited only by the number of CPU cores and the configured parallelism setting. The LocalExecutor requires PostgreSQL or MySQL (not SQLite) because it needs concurrent database access. It is the right choice for small to medium installations — SaulėGrid uses the LocalExecutor in production because their 12 DAGs and 30-40 daily tasks fit comfortably on a single machine.
CeleryExecutor

The CeleryExecutor distributes tasks across multiple worker machines using Celery, a distributed task queue. The scheduler places tasks in a message broker (Redis or RabbitMQ), and worker processes running on separate machines pick up tasks from the broker and execute them. This enables horizontal scaling: if one machine cannot handle the task volume, you add more workers. The CeleryExecutor is the production standard for large installations with hundreds of DAGs and thousands of daily tasks. SaulėGrid does not need it yet, but the mid-level track covers it for organizations that outgrow a single machine.

blocks.title: The three executor types compared
blocks.content:
SequentialExecutor (development only):
┌──────────────────────┐
│  Scheduler + Executor│
│  Task A → Task B → C │
│  (one at a time)      │
└──────────────────────┘

LocalExecutor (small-medium production):
┌──────────────────────┐
│  Scheduler            │
│  ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐│
│  │ A  │ │ B  │ │ C  ││
│  │    │ │    │ │    ││
│  └────┘ └────┘ └────┘│
│  (parallel processes) │
└──────────────────────┘

CeleryExecutor (large production):
┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
│ Scheduler │   │ Worker 1 │   │ Worker 2 │
│           ├──▶│ Task A   │   │ Task B   │
│           │   │ Task C   │   │ Task D   │
│           │   └──────────┘   └──────────┘
└──────────┘   (separate machines)
blocks.caption: Each executor type trades simplicity for scalability. Sequential is simplest but cannot parallelize. Local parallelizes on one machine. Celery distributes across many machines.

blocks.content: KubernetesExecutor

The KubernetesExecutor takes a different approach: instead of maintaining a pool of long-running workers, it creates a new Kubernetes pod for every task. When the scheduler queues a task, the executor requests a pod from the Kubernetes cluster, the pod runs the task, and the pod is destroyed when the task completes. This provides perfect isolation (each task runs in its own container with its own dependencies) and automatic scaling (Kubernetes handles resource allocation). The KubernetesExecutor is common in cloud-native environments but adds complexity that is beyond the junior level. Knowing it exists is sufficient for now.

blocks.title: Choosing an executor
blocks.content: For learning and local development, the SequentialExecutor with SQLite is fine — it works out of the box. For a small team with a single machine (like SaulėGrid), the LocalExecutor with PostgreSQL provides parallelism without distributed complexity. For large organizations with hundreds of DAGs, the CeleryExecutor or KubernetesExecutor distributes work across machines. The executor choice is a deployment decision, not a code decision — your DAG code is identical regardless of which executor runs it.
Parallelism and Concurrency

SaulėGrid's choice of LocalExecutor reflects a common progression. Matas started with the SequentialExecutor during development to learn the Airflow concepts. When the team moved to production, they switched to LocalExecutor and PostgreSQL — gaining parallelism with minimal deployment complexity. If SaulėGrid's pipeline grows to hundreds of DAGs, they will consider the CeleryExecutor. Each step up adds capability and complexity in roughly equal measure, which is why starting simple and scaling when needed is the recommended approach.

Two configuration settings control how many tasks run simultaneously. The parallelism setting (default 32) is the maximum number of tasks that Airflow will run at the same time across all DAGs. The dag_concurrency setting (default 16) is the maximum number of tasks per DAG. SaulėGrid's LocalExecutor with default settings can run up to 32 tasks in parallel, which is more than enough for their pipeline's peak of 8 concurrent tasks during the Silver fan-out phase. You do not need to tune these settings at the junior level, but knowing they exist helps you understand why some tasks wait in the queue even when the executor has capacity.

The DAG directory is the folder on the filesystem where Airflow looks for DAG definitions. By default, it is a folder called dags/ inside the Airflow home directory. Every Python file in this folder is parsed by the scheduler on every loop iteration. If the file contains a DAG object, the DAG is registered and becomes visible in the web UI. If the file is removed, the DAG disappears. This simple mechanism — drop a Python file into a folder, and it becomes a live pipeline — is one of Airflow's most practical features. But it also creates pitfalls that you need to understand.
How Discovery Works

The scheduler scans the DAG directory recursively, looking for Python files that contain DAG objects. It does not import every Python file blindly — it looks for files that, when executed, create at least one DAG variable at the module level. A Python file with utility functions but no DAG object is ignored. A Python file that creates a DAG inside a function that is never called is also ignored — the DAG must exist at module scope after the file is executed. SaulėGrid organizes their DAG directory with one file per DAG: dag_bronze_meters.py, dag_silver_transform.py, dag_gold_aggregate.py, and so on.
File Organization

There is no mandatory structure for the DAG directory — Airflow scans it recursively and finds DAGs wherever they are. But teams adopt conventions to keep things manageable. SaulėGrid uses a flat structure with a naming convention: all files in the root of the dags/ folder, named dag_{layer}_{domain}.py. Other teams use subdirectories: dags/bronze/, dags/silver/, dags/gold/. Both approaches work. The important rule is consistency within the team — everyone should know where to find a DAG file and what the naming convention means.

# SaulėGrid's DAG directory structure
# Flat organization with consistent naming convention

dags/
├── dag_bronze_meters.py          # Meter ingestion from SFTP
├── dag_bronze_generation.py      # Solar inverter telemetry
├── dag_bronze_weather.py         # Weather forecast API
├── dag_silver_transform.py       # Clean, validate, separate flows
├── dag_gold_aggregate.py         # Regional summaries, billing
├── dag_gold_regulatory.py        # VERT compliance reports
└── helpers/
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── sftp_utils.py             # Shared SFTP download functions
    ├── validation.py             # Shared validation functions
    └── alerting.py               # Shared alerting functions
The helpers/ Pattern

Notice the helpers/ subdirectory in SaulėGrid's structure. DAG files often share utility functions — SFTP download logic, validation checks, alerting helpers. These shared functions live in regular Python modules inside the DAG directory, importable by any DAG file. The key rule: helper files must not contain DAG objects at module scope. If a helper file accidentally creates a DAG (even a test DAG left behind during development), the scheduler will register it as a real pipeline. SaulėGrid's convention is that only files starting with dag_ contain DAG definitions; everything else is a helper.
The .airflowignore File

If you have files in the DAG directory that the scheduler should not parse — test files, documentation, templates — you can create an .airflowignore file. This file works like .gitignore: each line is a pattern, and files matching any pattern are skipped during parsing. SaulėGrid uses .airflowignore to exclude their test_*.py files and their README.md from the scheduler's scan. This is a small optimization — the scheduler will skip files that do not contain DAGs anyway — but it prevents unnecessary log noise about non-DAG files and speeds up parsing slightly.

blocks.title: Parsing is execution
blocks.content: The scheduler does not just read your DAG file — it executes it as a Python module on every parsing cycle. This means syntax errors crash the parser for that file (the DAG disappears from the UI), heavy imports slow down every parse cycle, and side effects (printing, API calls, database writes) at module level happen repeatedly. Treat your DAG file as code that runs constantly, not code that runs once.
When a DAG Appears in the UI

When you place a new Python file in the DAG directory, it does not appear in the web UI instantly. The scheduler must discover the file (next parsing cycle), parse it successfully (the file must be valid Python with a DAG object), and register the DAG in the metadata database. Only then does the web server see the new DAG when it queries the database. The typical delay from file creation to UI visibility is 10–30 seconds, depending on the scheduler's parsing interval. If a DAG does not appear after a minute, check the scheduler logs for parsing errors — a syntax error in the DAG file is the most common cause.

blocks.title: The DAG directory is the contract
blocks.content: The DAG directory is the single source of truth for what Airflow runs. If a file is in the directory and contains a valid DAG, Airflow runs it. If the file is removed, the DAG stops. If the file has a syntax error, the DAG disappears until the error is fixed. This simplicity is powerful: deploying a new pipeline means copying a Python file to a folder. Rolling back means removing it. There is no registration step, no configuration UI, no deployment wizard — just the filesystem.

The previous five lessons introduced each component in isolation. This lesson shows how they work together by tracing the communication flow during a normal Airflow operation. Understanding the communication paths is essential for diagnosing problems: when a task is stuck, you need to know whether the bottleneck is the scheduler (not queuing the task), the executor (not picking it up), the database (not accessible), or the worker (failing during execution). Every Airflow debugging session starts with the question: where in the communication chain did things break?
The Communication Paths

The four components communicate through two channels: the metadata database and the task queue. The metadata database is the primary coordination mechanism — the scheduler writes state, the executor reads queued tasks and writes results, and the web server reads everything. The task queue is a secondary channel used only between the scheduler and the executor: when the scheduler determines a task is ready, it places it in a queue (either an in-process queue for LocalExecutor or a message broker for CeleryExecutor), and the executor picks it up. There is no direct communication between the scheduler and the web server, or between the web server and the executor — they coordinate entirely through the database.

blocks.title: Communication paths between Airflow components
blocks.caption: All state flows through the metadata database. The scheduler writes task eligibility decisions. The executor writes task results. The web server reads both. The task queue is the only direct scheduler-to-executor path.

visual-recommendation.description: Component communication diagram with labeled directional arrows showing read/write paths, the database as the central hub, and the task queue as a secondary channel between scheduler and executor.

blocks.content: A Normal Cycle

Consider what happens when SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters is due for its daily run. The scheduler reads the DAG file from the dags/ directory and confirms the DAG definition has not changed. It checks the schedule: the last DAG Run was yesterday, and it is now past midnight, so a new run is due. The scheduler creates a dag_run record in the database with state 'queued.' It then evaluates the first task (download_meters): no upstream dependencies, so it is eligible. The scheduler places download_meters in the task queue and creates a task_instance record in the database with state 'scheduled.'

The executor picks download_meters from the queue, spawns a worker process, and updates the task_instance state to 'running.' The worker executes the download function, which connects to SaulėGrid's SFTP server and downloads the meter file. When the function returns successfully, the executor updates the task_instance state to 'success.' The scheduler, on its next loop, sees that download_meters is now complete and evaluates the next task (validate_readings). Its upstream dependency is satisfied, so the scheduler queues it. The cycle repeats until all tasks are complete and the DAG Run state is updated to 'success.'
No Direct Component Communication

A crucial detail: the scheduler and the web server never talk to each other directly. The web server does not notify the scheduler when you trigger a manual run — it writes a record to the database, and the scheduler discovers it on its next loop. The scheduler does not push updates to the web server when a task completes — it writes the new state to the database, and the web server reads it the next time you refresh the page. This database-mediated communication is simple and reliable but introduces a small delay: changes are visible after the next read cycle, not instantly.
The Refresh Delay

Because all communication flows through the database, there is a small delay between an event and its visibility. When the executor marks download_meters as 'success,' the web server does not know about it until its next database query — typically on the next page refresh. Similarly, when you trigger a manual DAG Run from the UI, the scheduler does not see it until its next loop iteration. In practice, these delays are measured in seconds and are rarely noticeable. But they explain why you occasionally see stale status in the UI: the page was rendered between the executor's write and the web server's read.

blocks.title: Debugging principle
blocks.content: When a task is stuck, trace the communication chain. Is the scheduler running? Check the scheduler process and logs. Did the scheduler queue the task? Check the task_instance state in the database. Did the executor pick it up? Check the executor logs and the worker process. Is the database accessible? Check the database connection from each component. Most Airflow issues are communication breakdowns at one of these four points.
The Log Flow

Task logs follow their own path. When a worker executes a task, the log output is written to the configured log storage — typically a local file on the worker machine, or a cloud storage bucket in production deployments. The metadata database does not store the log content; it stores a reference to the log location. When you view a task's log in the web UI, the web server reads the log from the storage location using that reference. SaulėGrid stores logs locally on the Airflow server, which works because their LocalExecutor runs all workers on the same machine. In a CeleryExecutor setup, logs from remote workers must be centralized to a shared location — a topic covered in the mid-level track.

Airflow can run as a single process on your laptop or as a distributed system across multiple servers. The same DAG code runs in both environments — the difference is the deployment configuration: which executor, which database, and how the components are arranged. Understanding the difference between local (development) mode and production mode helps you interpret what you see when learning Airflow and prepares you for the transition that every team makes when moving from development to production.
Local Development Mode

When you install Airflow on your laptop and run airflow standalone, you get a complete Airflow environment in a single process. The scheduler, web server, and executor run as threads within one Python process. The metadata database is a SQLite file in your home directory. The DAG directory is a local folder. Everything runs on one machine with one command. This is local mode, and it is the fastest way to start learning Airflow. SaulėGrid's engineers use local mode on their development laptops to test new DAGs before deploying them.

# Starting Airflow in local development mode
# This single command starts everything: scheduler, web server, database

$ airflow standalone

# Output shows all components starting:
# [scheduler] Starting scheduler...
# [webserver] Starting Flask app...
# [sqlite] Using /home/matas/airflow/airflow.db
# [standalone] Airflow is ready. Visit http://localhost:8080
#
# Default login: admin / (auto-generated password shown in output)
# DAG directory: /home/matas/airflow/dags/
Production Mode

In production, each component runs as a separate process — often on separate machines. The metadata database is a managed PostgreSQL instance with automated backups and failover. The scheduler runs as a supervised daemon that restarts automatically if it crashes. The web server runs behind a reverse proxy (Nginx or a cloud load balancer) for security and performance. The executor is either LocalExecutor on a single beefy server or CeleryExecutor with multiple worker machines. Each component is monitored independently, and the infrastructure is managed with configuration-as-code tools.

left.label: Local Development Mode
points: Single process: airflow standalone
SQLite database in a file
SequentialExecutor (one task at a time)
DAGs in a local folder
No monitoring or alerting
One user on one laptop

right.label: Production Mode
points: Separate processes: scheduler, webserver, workers
PostgreSQL on managed database service
LocalExecutor or CeleryExecutor (parallel)
DAGs deployed via Git/CI to shared storage
Health checks, log aggregation, alerting
Multiple users with role-based access

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's Production Setup
SaulėGrid's Setup

SaulėGrid's production Airflow runs on a single cloud server with PostgreSQL as the metadata database and LocalExecutor for task execution. The scheduler and web server run as separate systemd services with automatic restart on failure. DAGs are deployed by pushing to a Git repository, which triggers a CI pipeline that copies the DAG files to the server's dags/ directory. Logs are stored locally on the server and rotated daily. This setup is modest — one server, one executor, no Celery — but it handles SaulėGrid's 12 DAGs and 40 daily tasks comfortably. The production setup adds reliability (auto-restart, PostgreSQL backups) without adding distributed complexity.
Code Is Identical

The most important point about local vs production mode is that your DAG code does not change. A DAG that works in local mode with SQLite and SequentialExecutor will work in production with PostgreSQL and LocalExecutor without any code modifications. The deployment configuration changes — the executor type, the database connection, the number of workers — but the Python files that define your DAGs are identical. This is why SaulėGrid's engineers can develop and test DAGs on their laptops and deploy them to production with confidence: the behavior is the same, only the scale and reliability differ.

blocks.title: Start simple, scale later
blocks.content: You do not need a production deployment to learn Airflow. Local mode with airflow standalone is the right starting point for this entire course. When you are ready to move to production, the transition is a deployment exercise — changing configuration, setting up PostgreSQL, running components as services — not a code rewrite. Every DAG you learn to write in this course works in both environments.

blocks.title: One local mode limitation
blocks.content: Local mode with SequentialExecutor runs tasks one at a time. If your DAG has three independent tasks that should run in parallel, they will run sequentially in local mode. The logic and results are correct, but the timing does not reflect production behavior. SaulėGrid's Silver DAG has three parallel transformations that take 8 minutes total in production but 24 minutes in local mode (3 × 8 minutes, sequentially). For correctness testing, local mode is perfect. For performance testing, you need the production executor.

sections.title: The Life of a DAG Run

blocks.content: The previous lessons introduced each component individually and showed how they communicate. This lesson puts everything together by tracing a complete execution cycle for SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters: from the moment the scheduler detects that a new run is due, through task scheduling, queuing, execution, state updates, and final completion. Following this lifecycle end-to-end connects the abstract component descriptions to the concrete reality of what happens when your pipeline runs.
Step 1 — The Scheduler Detects a Due Run

It is 00:00:05 UTC on March 23rd. The scheduler is in its continuous loop, and it has just parsed dag_bronze_meters.py from the dags/ directory. The DAG's schedule is '@daily' with a start_date of January 1st, 2026. The scheduler queries the metadata database: what is the latest DAG Run for this DAG? The answer is March 22nd. Since we have passed midnight and the next scheduled interval (March 23rd) is due, the scheduler creates a new DAG Run record in the database. The DAG Run has a logical date of 2026-03-22 (the data interval it covers, which is the previous day) and a state of 'running.'
Step 2 — Task Eligibility Evaluation

The scheduler evaluates each task in the DAG Run. SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters has three tasks: download_meters >> validate_readings >> load_to_bronze. The scheduler checks download_meters first: it has no upstream dependencies, so it is immediately eligible. The scheduler creates a task_instance record in the database with state 'scheduled' and places download_meters in the executor's task queue. The scheduler then checks validate_readings: its upstream (download_meters) is in state 'scheduled,' not 'success,' so validate_readings is not eligible yet. Similarly, load_to_bronze must wait.
Step 3 — Task Execution

The executor picks download_meters from the queue. Using the LocalExecutor, it spawns a new Python process that runs the task's callable — a function that connects to SaulėGrid's SFTP server, authenticates using the sftp_saulegrid_meters connection, and downloads the daily meter file. The executor updates the task_instance state to 'running' in the database. The download takes 8 minutes. When the function returns without raising an exception, the executor updates the state to 'success' and records the duration.

blocks.title: The lifecycle of SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters DAG Run
blocks.content:
Time    Scheduler                    Executor              Database State
─────   ─────────                    ────────              ──────────────
00:00   Detects due run              (idle)                DAG Run: created
        Creates DAG Run                                    

00:00   Evaluates tasks              (idle)                download: scheduled
        Queues download_meters                             validate: none
                                                           load: none

00:01   (waiting)                    Picks up download     download: running
                                     Spawns worker        
                                     Connects to SFTP     

00:09   (waiting)                    Download complete     download: success
                                     Worker exits         

00:09   Evaluates tasks              (idle)                validate: scheduled
        Queues validate_readings                          

00:09   (waiting)                    Picks up validate     validate: running
                                     Reads file           
                                     Checks quality       

00:14   (waiting)                    Validation complete   validate: success

00:14   Evaluates tasks              (idle)                load: scheduled
        Queues load_to_bronze                             

00:15   (waiting)                    Picks up load         load: running
                                     Writes Parquet       

00:17   Detects all tasks done       (idle)                load: success
        Updates DAG Run                                    DAG Run: success
blocks.caption: The complete lifecycle takes 17 minutes. The scheduler evaluates eligibility after each task completion. The executor runs tasks as soon as they are queued. The database records every state transition.

blocks.content: Step 4 — Cascade Through Dependencies

After download_meters succeeds, the scheduler re-evaluates all remaining tasks on its next loop (within seconds). It sees that validate_readings now has its upstream dependency satisfied, so it queues it. The executor picks it up, runs the validation function (which checks for null meter IDs, negative daily_kw values, and date format consistency), and reports success after 5 minutes. The scheduler then queues load_to_bronze, which reads the validated file and writes it to the Bronze layer as Parquet. When load_to_bronze succeeds, the scheduler checks: all tasks in this DAG Run are now in the 'success' state. It updates the DAG Run state to 'success.'
What the Web Server Shows

Throughout this lifecycle, the web server renders the current state from the database. At 00:05, the Grid View shows download_meters as a green 'running' cell and the other two tasks as empty. At 00:10, download_meters turns dark green (success) and validate_readings turns green (running). At 00:17, all three cells are dark green. If Ieva opens the UI at 09:00, she sees the completed DAG Run in the Grid View with a green success indicator. She can click on any task to see its log, its duration, and its start and end times. The entire diagnostic that used to take 45 minutes of log file inspection is now a single glance.

The web server also provides a historical view. Ieva can switch to the Calendar View and see that dag_bronze_meters has succeeded every day this month except March 3rd (the SFTP timeout incident from Module 1). She can click on March 3rd's failed run, drill into the download_meters task, and read the exact error message from two weeks ago. This historical visibility replaces the manual log archaeology that consumed hours of the team's time before Airflow.

blocks.title: The vocabulary of execution
blocks.content: A DAG Run is one execution of a DAG. A task instance is one execution of a task within a DAG Run. A DAG Run contains one task instance per task. The states flow through a predictable lifecycle: no_status → scheduled → queued → running → success (or failed, up_for_retry). These terms appear in the Airflow UI, in log messages, and in API responses — they are the language you use to describe what your pipeline is doing.

The previous lesson traced a perfect execution: every task succeeded, every state transition was clean, and the DAG Run completed in 17 minutes. But production pipelines are not perfect. SFTP servers go down. Database connections drop. Workers run out of memory. The scheduler process crashes. Understanding how Airflow handles these failures — and what each failure looks like from the outside — is essential for operating SaulėGrid's pipelines. This lesson covers the most common failure scenarios and what each component does when something breaks.
A Task Fails

The most common failure is a task that raises an exception during execution. SaulėGrid's download_meters task connects to an SFTP server that is occasionally unavailable. When the connection fails, the Python function raises a ConnectionError. The executor catches the exception, marks the task_instance as 'failed' in the database, and records the error in the task log. The scheduler sees the failed task on its next loop and evaluates the retry configuration. If the task has retries remaining (SaulėGrid configures 3 retries with 5-minute intervals), the scheduler changes the state to 'up_for_retry' and schedules the next attempt. Downstream tasks (validate_readings, load_to_bronze) remain in 'no_status' — they will not be queued until download_meters eventually succeeds.
All Retries Exhausted

If download_meters fails three times in a row (all retries exhausted), the task_instance state becomes 'failed' permanently. The scheduler marks the DAG Run state as 'failed' because not all tasks can complete. All downstream tasks remain unexecuted — their states stay at 'no_status,' and they never run on potentially missing data. This is the dependency guarantee in action: downstream tasks are protected from upstream failures by the dependency graph. SaulėGrid's email alerting triggers on the task failure, sending Matas a notification with the task name, the DAG Run date, and a link to the log.

blocks.title: What happens when a task fails with retries
blocks.content:
Attempt 1 (00:01):  download_meters → ConnectionError
                    State: up_for_retry (retry 1 of 3)
                    Wait 5 minutes...

Attempt 2 (00:06):  download_meters → ConnectionError
                    State: up_for_retry (retry 2 of 3)
                    Wait 5 minutes...

Attempt 3 (00:11):  download_meters → ConnectionError
                    State: failed (retries exhausted)

Result:
  download_meters:   failed ✗
  validate_readings: no_status (never ran)
  load_to_bronze:    no_status (never ran)
  DAG Run:           failed ✗

  → Email alert sent to matas@saulegrid.lt
  → Matas fixes SFTP, clears the failed task in the UI
  → Scheduler re-queues download_meters
  → Pipeline resumes from where it stopped
blocks.caption: The retry mechanism handles transient failures automatically. Permanent failures require human intervention, but the pipeline is in a safe, recoverable state — no bad data was produced.

blocks.content: The Scheduler Crashes

If the scheduler process crashes — out of memory, segmentation fault, or a bug in the parsing code — the impact depends on what was running. Tasks that are already running on the executor continue to execute normally because the executor runs independently. However, no new tasks will be scheduled: completed tasks will not have their downstream dependents queued, and no new DAG Runs will be created. When the scheduler restarts (automatically, if configured with a process supervisor), it reads the current state from the metadata database and resumes where it left off. There is no data loss because all state is in the database, not in the scheduler's memory.
The Database Becomes Unreachable

A database outage is the most severe failure. When the metadata database is unreachable, every component stalls. The scheduler cannot read DAG definitions, create DAG Runs, or queue tasks. The executor cannot update task states or report results. The web server cannot render any page because every page requires database queries. Currently-running tasks may complete their work, but the executor cannot record the results — when the database returns, the executor may need to reconcile state. SaulėGrid mitigates this risk by using a managed PostgreSQL service with automated failover: if the primary database fails, a standby takes over within seconds.

rows: Task fails. Downstream tasks wait. Retries if configured.
Running task fails. Other tasks unaffected.
Scheduler marks task as failed. Retry mechanism kicks in.
No new scheduling. Running tasks continue.
Restart scheduler. Resumes from database state.
No UI access. Scheduling and execution continue.
Restart web server. No data loss.

blocks.content: The Recovery Workflow

When SaulėGrid encounters a failed DAG Run, the recovery workflow is straightforward. Ieva opens the Grid View in the web UI and identifies the failed task (it appears as a red cell). She clicks on the task instance to read the log and understand the failure. If the failure was transient (SFTP timeout, temporary network issue), she clicks 'Clear' to reset the task state to 'scheduled.' The scheduler picks up the cleared task on its next loop and re-queues it. If the downstream tasks have not run yet (their state is 'no_status'), they will execute automatically after the cleared task succeeds. The entire recovery — from identifying the failure to resuming the pipeline — takes less than two minutes.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 3, you will write your first DAG from scratch — defining the DAG object, configuring its parameters, and understanding how the scheduler discovers and registers it. Every component from this module will come alive: you will see the scheduler parse your file, watch the DAG appear in the web UI, and trace a DAG Run through the lifecycle you learned in this lesson. The architecture is set. Now it is time to build on it.

chapters.title: DAGs — The Core Abstraction
chapters.description: Understand DAGs as Airflow's central abstraction, master DAG creation and parameterization, learn the parsing and discovery process, and gain familiarity with DAG state and DAG Runs.

blocks.content: Module 1 introduced the DAG concept at a high level: a collection of tasks with directed, acyclic dependencies. Module 2 showed how the scheduler discovers and executes DAGs through the four-component architecture. This module goes deeper. Over the next ten lessons, you will learn how to create DAGs, configure their parameters, understand how Airflow parses and registers them, and organize them for a production team. By the end, you will be able to write, read, and reason about every DAG in SaulėGrid's pipeline. This first lesson revisits the DAG definition with the precision needed to write one.
The Formal Definition

A DAG — Directed Acyclic Graph — is a mathematical structure with three properties. It is a graph: a collection of nodes connected by edges. It is directed: each edge has a direction, pointing from one node to another. It is acyclic: no sequence of edges leads back to a node that was already visited. In Airflow, nodes are tasks (units of work) and edges are dependencies (execution order constraints). The DAG is the container that holds these tasks and their relationships. It does not contain the task logic itself — it contains the structure that determines when each task runs relative to the others.
Tasks Are Nodes

Each task in a DAG is a node in the graph. A task represents one unit of work: download a file, validate data, run a SQL query, send an alert. Tasks have names (task IDs), types (which operator they use), and configuration (retries, timeouts, parameters). SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters has three tasks: download_meters, validate_readings, and load_to_bronze. Each task is a node in the graph, and each task knows what it should do — but it does not know when to run. The DAG determines that.
Dependencies Are Edges

Each dependency is a directed edge from one task to another. The edge download_meters → validate_readings means: validate_readings cannot start until download_meters has completed successfully. The direction matters — it is not a mutual relationship. download_meters does not wait for validate_readings. The edge points one way: from upstream to downstream. In Airflow code, you define this edge with the >> operator: download_meters >> validate_readings. This single symbol encodes the dependency that replaced SaulėGrid's 30-minute timing gap.

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's Bronze DAG as a formal graph
blocks.content:
Nodes (tasks):        Edges (dependencies):

  ┌─────────────────┐
  │ download_meters  │ ─── Node 1 (no upstream)
  └────────┬────────┘
           │
           │  Edge 1: download → validate
           ▼
  ┌─────────────────┐
  │validate_readings│ ─── Node 2
  └────────┬────────┘
           │
           │  Edge 2: validate → load
           ▼
  ┌─────────────────┐
  │ load_to_bronze  │ ─── Node 3 (no downstream)
  └─────────────────┘

3 nodes, 2 edges
Directed: edges point downward (upstream → downstream)
Acyclic: no path leads back to a visited node
blocks.caption: The simplest DAG is a linear chain. Nodes with no upstream edges run first. Nodes with no downstream edges run last. The scheduler determines execution order by finding nodes whose upstream dependencies are all satisfied.

blocks.content: The DAG as a Container

A DAG is not a task. It is the container that holds tasks and defines how they relate. The DAG carries metadata — an ID, a schedule, a start date, default parameters — that applies to all tasks within it. When the scheduler evaluates whether to create a new DAG Run, it looks at the DAG's schedule. When the executor runs a task, it looks at the task's configuration. The DAG provides the context; the tasks provide the work. This separation means you can change a task's logic without changing the DAG's schedule, and you can change the schedule without touching any task.
One DAG, Many Runs

A DAG is a definition — a template that describes what should happen. A DAG Run is an instance — one specific execution of that template. SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters is defined once in a Python file. But it has run 81 times (once per day since January 1st). Each DAG Run is independent: the March 22nd run can succeed while the March 23rd run fails, without affecting each other. The DAG is the blueprint; the DAG Run is the house built from it. You write the DAG once, and the scheduler creates DAG Runs according to the schedule.

rows: The definition: tasks, dependencies, schedule, parameters
One execution of the DAG for a specific date
One time you cook the recipe
One unit of work within the DAG
One execution of a task within a specific DAG Run
One time you perform that step

blocks.title: The key distinction
blocks.content: The DAG is the code in your Python file. The DAG Run is the record in the metadata database. You write DAGs. The scheduler creates DAG Runs. You debug task instances within DAG Runs. Keeping this hierarchy clear — DAG → DAG Run → Task Instance — prevents the most common source of confusion when reading logs, navigating the UI, or discussing pipeline behavior with your team.

Now that the formal definition is clear, the next lesson shows what this looks like in actual Python code. You will write a DAG file from scratch, and the scheduler will discover it, register it, and display it in the web UI. Every concept from this lesson — nodes, edges, the container, the template-versus-instance distinction — becomes concrete when you see the code.

A DAG file is a Python file that Airflow's scheduler reads from the dags/ directory (Module 2, lesson 6). The file creates a DAG object at module scope, defines tasks inside it, and specifies dependencies between them. The scheduler executes this file, discovers the DAG object, and registers it in the metadata database. This lesson walks through a complete, minimal DAG file line by line — the same file that SaulėGrid uses for their simplest Bronze ingestion pipeline.
The Complete File

Here is the simplest DAG that actually does something useful. It defines three tasks in a linear chain, using the PythonOperator to call Python functions. Every line serves a purpose — there is no boilerplate you can skip and no optional section you can ignore. The file is complete and runnable as-is. Read the code first, then the explanation that follows will walk through each section.

# dag_bronze_meters.py — SaulėGrid's Bronze meter ingestion DAG
# This file lives in the dags/ directory

from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta


def download_from_sftp():
    """Download daily meter readings from SaulėGrid's SFTP server."""
    print("Connecting to sftp.saulegrid.lt...")
    print("Downloading meters_2026-03-22.csv...")
    print("Download complete: 400,000 rows")


def validate_meter_data():
    """Check for null meter_ids and negative daily_kw values."""
    print("Validating 400,000 meter readings...")
    print("Removed 23 rows with null meter_id")
    print("Removed 7 rows with negative daily_kw")
    print("Validation complete: 399,970 valid rows")


def write_to_bronze():
    """Write validated readings to Bronze layer as Parquet."""
    print("Writing 399,970 rows to bronze/meters/2026-03-22/")
    print("Write complete")


# --- DAG definition starts here ---

with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    description='Ingest daily meter readings from SFTP to Bronze layer',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    schedule='@daily',
    catchup=False,
    tags=['bronze', 'meters', 'saulegrid'],
) as dag:

    download = PythonOperator(
        task_id='download_meters',
        python_callable=download_from_sftp,
    )

    validate = PythonOperator(
        task_id='validate_readings',
        python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    )

    load = PythonOperator(
        task_id='load_to_bronze',
        python_callable=write_to_bronze,
    )

    download >> validate >> load
The Imports

The file starts with three imports. The DAG class from the airflow module is the container object you are creating. The PythonOperator from airflow.operators.python is the operator type that runs Python functions — you will learn about other operators in Module 4. The datetime and timedelta from Python's standard library are used for the start_date and schedule configuration. These three imports appear in virtually every Airflow DAG file.
The Callable Functions

Before the DAG definition, the file defines three Python functions — one for each task. These functions contain the actual work: downloading files, validating data, writing results. In this simplified example, the functions just print messages. In production, they would contain real SFTP download logic, real validation code, and real Parquet write operations. The functions are defined before the DAG because Python requires functions to exist before they are referenced. The PythonOperator's python_callable parameter points to these functions.
The DAG Object

The DAG() constructor creates the DAG object with its configuration. The dag_id is the unique identifier that appears in the UI and in log messages. The start_date tells the scheduler when this DAG's schedule begins — no DAG Runs will be created for dates before this. The schedule parameter defines how often the DAG runs ('@daily' means once per day). The catchup=False parameter prevents Airflow from creating DAG Runs for every day between start_date and today (you will learn about catchup in Module 7). The tags list helps filter DAGs in the web UI. You will learn each parameter in detail in lesson 4.
The Tasks

Inside the DAG context (the with block), three PythonOperator instances create three tasks. Each task has a task_id (unique within this DAG) and a python_callable (the function to run). The task_id is what appears in the Graph View and in log file names. The python_callable is what the executor actually runs when the task is executed. Notice that the callable is passed as a reference (download_from_sftp, without parentheses) — the executor calls the function, not the DAG file.

blocks.title: Do not call the function
blocks.content: A common mistake is writing python_callable=download_from_sftp() with parentheses. This calls the function immediately during DAG parsing — every few seconds — instead of passing it to the executor to call at runtime. The result: the function runs on every scheduler loop instead of once per DAG Run. Always pass the function reference without parentheses.
The Dependency Chain

The final line, download >> validate >> load, defines the execution order. The >> operator is syntactic sugar for set_downstream(): it means download must succeed before validate starts, and validate must succeed before load starts. This single line is the dependency graph. Without it, all three tasks would run in parallel with no ordering — the scheduler would queue them all simultaneously because none has upstream dependencies. The >> operator is what makes this a pipeline rather than three independent tasks.
What Happens When You Save This File

When you save dag_bronze_meters.py to the dags/ directory, the scheduler discovers it within seconds (Module 2, lesson 6). The scheduler executes the file as a Python module, finds the DAG object, and registers it in the metadata database. The web server reads the registration and displays dag_bronze_meters in the DAG list. In the Graph View, you see three nodes connected by two edges — exactly the structure the code defines. The DAG is paused by default (a safety feature), so no DAG Runs are created until you toggle it on.

blocks.title: This is the pattern
blocks.content: Every DAG you write in this course follows the same structure: imports, callable functions, DAG constructor with parameters, task definitions inside the DAG context, and dependency declarations. The parameters change, the operators change, and the callable logic changes — but the structure is always the same. Once you internalize this pattern, reading any Airflow DAG file becomes natural.

The previous lesson used the with DAG(...) as dag: pattern to create a DAG and define tasks inside it. This with statement is a Python context manager — a language feature that groups related code inside a block. The DAG context manager is the standard pattern in modern Airflow, and understanding how it works prevents subtle bugs where tasks accidentally belong to the wrong DAG or no DAG at all.
What a Context Manager Does

In Python, a context manager is a block introduced by the with keyword that manages setup and teardown automatically. You have seen context managers before: with open('file.txt') as f: opens a file and guarantees it closes when the block ends. The DAG context manager works similarly: it tells Airflow that every operator created inside the with block belongs to this DAG. When the block ends, the association is released. This is how Airflow knows that download_meters, validate_readings, and load_to_bronze all belong to dag_bronze_meters — they were created inside its context.

# The context manager pattern — the modern standard
# Every operator inside the 'with' block belongs to this DAG automatically

with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    schedule='@daily',
    catchup=False,
) as dag:

    # These tasks automatically belong to dag_bronze_meters
    download = PythonOperator(
        task_id='download_meters',
        python_callable=download_from_sftp,
    )

    validate = PythonOperator(
        task_id='validate_readings',
        python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    )

    download >> validate
The Explicit dag= Parameter

Before the context manager pattern became standard, Airflow users assigned tasks to DAGs explicitly using the dag= parameter on every operator. This older pattern still works and produces identical behavior, but it is more verbose and easier to get wrong — if you forget the dag= parameter on one task, that task belongs to no DAG and silently disappears from the scheduler's view.

# The explicit dag= pattern — older style, still works
# Every operator must explicitly reference the DAG

dag = DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    schedule='@daily',
    catchup=False,
)

# Each task must include dag=dag — easy to forget
download = PythonOperator(
    task_id='download_meters',
    python_callable=download_from_sftp,
    dag=dag,  # required — without this, the task is orphaned
)

validate = PythonOperator(
    task_id='validate_readings',
    python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    dag=dag,  # required on every single operator
)

download >> validate

left.label: Context Manager (with DAG())
points: Tasks belong to the DAG automatically
Forgetting is impossible — you are inside the block
Less code, less duplication
Standard in modern Airflow (2.0+)

right.label: Explicit (dag= parameter)
points: Each task must explicitly set dag=dag
Forgetting dag= silently orphans the task
More code, dag=dag on every operator
Common in legacy codebases (1.x)

blocks.content: Why the Context Manager Wins

SaulėGrid's team uses the context manager pattern exclusively. The advantage is not just shorter code — it is safety. If Matas creates a new PythonOperator inside the with block, it automatically belongs to the correct DAG. There is no way to accidentally create an orphaned task. In the explicit pattern, a single missing dag=dag produces a task that the scheduler ignores without warning. The team lost an afternoon to this bug early in their Airflow adoption, which is why they adopted the context manager as a mandatory convention.
Multiple DAGs in One File

A single Python file can define multiple DAGs using multiple context manager blocks. Each with DAG() block creates an independent DAG with its own tasks and dependencies. This is valid Python and Airflow accepts it without complaint, but it is rarely recommended in practice — it makes files longer and harder to navigate, and a syntax error anywhere in the file removes every DAG defined in it from the scheduler simultaneously.

# Multiple DAGs in one file — valid but not recommended
# A syntax error anywhere removes BOTH DAGs

with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    schedule='@daily',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
) as dag_bronze:
    # ... tasks for bronze ...
    pass

with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters_test',
    schedule=None,  # manually triggered only
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
) as dag_test:
    # ... tasks for testing ...
    pass

blocks.title: One file, one DAG
blocks.content: The one-file-per-DAG convention is the strongest recommendation in this lesson. It makes DAGs independently deployable (update one file without affecting others), independently parseable (a syntax error only affects one DAG), and independently searchable (the filename tells you which DAG it contains). SaulėGrid names every file after its DAG ID: dag_bronze_meters.py contains dag_bronze_meters.

With the context manager pattern established as the standard way to create DAGs, the next lesson dives into the DAG constructor's parameters — the configuration options that control when the DAG runs, how it handles missed schedules, how many concurrent runs it allows, and what metadata it carries. These parameters are what transform a bare DAG skeleton into a fully configured pipeline ready for the scheduler.

The DAG constructor accepts dozens of parameters, but only a handful matter at the junior level. These parameters control the DAG's identity, its schedule, its behavior when first deployed, and its metadata. This lesson covers the parameters that appear in every DAG file and explains what each one does, using SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters as the running example. Parameters related to scheduling (data intervals, catchup, timetables) are covered in more depth in Module 7.
The Essential Parameters

with DAG(
    # --- Identity ---
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    description='Ingest daily meter readings from SFTP to Bronze layer',
    tags=['bronze', 'meters', 'saulegrid'],
    owner_links={'matas': 'mailto:matas@saulegrid.lt'},

    # --- Schedule ---
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    schedule='@daily',

    # --- Behavior ---
    catchup=False,
    max_active_runs=1,

    # --- Task defaults ---
    default_args={
        'owner': 'matas',
        'retries': 2,
        'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5),
    },
) as dag:
    # ... tasks ...
dag_id — The Unique Identifier

The dag_id is the DAG's unique name across the entire Airflow installation. It appears in the web UI, in log file paths, in API responses, and in the metadata database. If two DAG files define the same dag_id, Airflow's behavior is undefined — the scheduler may register one or the other unpredictably. SaulėGrid's naming convention is dag_{layer}_{domain}: dag_bronze_meters, dag_silver_transform, dag_gold_aggregate. The convention prevents collisions and makes the DAG's purpose immediately clear.
start_date — When the Schedule Begins

The start_date tells the scheduler when to begin creating DAG Runs. No DAG Run will be created for a date before start_date. SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters uses January 1st, 2026 — the date their Airflow installation went live. If the start_date is in the future, the DAG is visible in the UI but no DAG Runs are created until that date arrives. The start_date must be a fixed datetime, not datetime.now() — using datetime.now() creates a new start_date on every parse, which confuses the scheduler and is a common mistake.

blocks.title: Never use datetime.now() as start_date
blocks.content: The start_date is evaluated every time the scheduler parses the DAG file (every few seconds). If you write start_date=datetime.now(), the start_date changes on every parse, and the scheduler can never determine whether a DAG Run is due — because the baseline keeps moving. Always use a fixed date: start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1). This is the single most common Airflow misconfiguration.
schedule — How Often to Run

The schedule parameter defines the cadence. It accepts cron expressions ('0 0 * * *' for daily at midnight), preset strings ('@daily', '@hourly', '@weekly'), timedelta objects (timedelta(hours=6) for every 6 hours), or None for DAGs that are triggered manually or by external events. SaulėGrid's Bronze DAGs use '@daily'. Module 7 covers scheduling in full detail, including cron syntax and the data interval model.
catchup — Historical Backfilling

When catchup=True (the default), Airflow creates DAG Runs for every scheduled interval between start_date and today. If your start_date is January 1st and today is March 23rd, Airflow would create 82 DAG Runs — one for each day. This is useful for backfilling historical data but overwhelming for a DAG that only processes today's data. SaulėGrid sets catchup=False on all their DAGs because their SFTP server only provides the current day's file — there is no historical data to backfill. Module 7 covers catchup and backfilling in detail.
max_active_runs — Concurrency Control

The max_active_runs parameter limits how many DAG Runs can execute simultaneously. SaulėGrid sets max_active_runs=1 on their Bronze DAGs to prevent overlap: if yesterday's run is still finishing when today's run is scheduled, the new run waits rather than starting in parallel. This prevents race conditions where two runs download from the same SFTP directory simultaneously. The default is 16, which allows significant concurrency — appropriate for DAGs that process independent date partitions but risky for DAGs that access shared resources.

rows: When the schedule begins — Airflow go-live date
No historical data on SFTP to backfill

blocks.content: These seven parameters cover every DAG SaulėGrid writes at the junior level. Additional parameters exist for advanced scheduling (timetable), access control (access_control), rendering (render_template_as_native_obj), and inline documentation (doc_md) — you will encounter some of these in later modules and others in the mid-level track. For now, the seven parameters in this lesson are your complete toolkit for creating well-configured DAGs.

The previous lesson introduced default_args as one of the DAG constructor parameters. This lesson explains what default_args does in detail, why it exists, and how SaulėGrid uses it to enforce consistent task behavior across their DAGs. Default arguments are the mechanism that prevents every task definition from repeating the same retry count, timeout, and owner — they are the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle applied to Airflow task configuration.
The Problem default_args Solves

Consider a DAG with ten tasks. Every task should have the same owner (matas), the same retry count (2), the same retry delay (5 minutes), and the same email notification settings. Without default_args, you would write these four parameters on every operator — 40 lines of repeated configuration. If the retry policy changes from 2 to 3, you update ten places. Default_args eliminates this duplication: you define the shared parameters once in a dictionary, pass it to the DAG, and every task inherits them automatically.

# SaulėGrid's standard default_args for Bronze DAGs
# These values apply to EVERY task unless the task overrides them

default_args = {
    'owner': 'matas',
    'retries': 2,
    'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5),
    'email': ['matas@saulegrid.lt'],
    'email_on_failure': True,
    'email_on_retry': False,
    'execution_timeout': timedelta(minutes=30),
}

with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    schedule='@daily',
    catchup=False,
    default_args=default_args,  # every task inherits these
) as dag:

    # download_meters inherits: owner=matas, retries=2, etc.
    download = PythonOperator(
        task_id='download_meters',
        python_callable=download_from_sftp,
        # No need to repeat owner, retries, email — inherited
    )

    # validate_readings inherits the same defaults
    validate = PythonOperator(
        task_id='validate_readings',
        python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    )
Common Default Arguments

rows: Appears in the UI and in email notifications
How many times to retry a failed task
Send email when a task fails permanently
Send email on each retry attempt
Kill the task if it runs longer than this

blocks.content: Overriding Defaults

Any parameter set directly on a task overrides the default. SaulėGrid's download_meters task connects to an external SFTP server that can be slow, so it needs a longer timeout than the default 30 minutes. Instead of changing the default for all tasks, Matas sets execution_timeout on just that one task. The validate_readings and load_to_bronze tasks keep the 30-minute default because they process local data and finish quickly.

# Override a specific default for one task
# download_meters needs more time due to SFTP network latency

download = PythonOperator(
    task_id='download_meters',
    python_callable=download_from_sftp,
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=60),  # overrides the 30-min default
    retries=3,  # overrides the default 2 retries for this network-dependent task
)

# validate_readings uses all defaults — no overrides needed
validate = PythonOperator(
    task_id='validate_readings',
    python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    # inherits: owner=matas, retries=2, timeout=30min, email=True
)
Shared Across DAGs
Centralizing Defaults

SaulėGrid defines their default_args dictionary in a shared Python module (helpers/defaults.py) and imports it into every DAG file. This ensures consistency: every DAG uses the same retry policy, the same alerting rules, and the same owner. When the team changed their retry delay from 5 minutes to 3 minutes, Ieva updated one file and all 12 DAGs picked up the change on the next scheduler parse. Without the shared module, she would have edited 12 files and risked missing one.

blocks.title: Think of default_args as team policy
blocks.content: Default_args encodes your team's operational standards: how many retries, how long to wait, who gets alerted. The DAG inherits the team policy. Individual tasks override it only when they have a specific reason. This pattern keeps your DAGs consistent without making them rigid — the policy is the default, not the mandate.

With DAG parameters and default_args covered, the next lesson addresses DAG IDs and file naming — the conventions that make DAGs findable, manageable, and unambiguous as the number of DAGs grows from three to twelve to a hundred. Naming conventions may seem like a minor concern when you have three DAGs, but they become essential when the count reaches thirty. SaulėGrid established their convention early, and it has scaled without modification since.

When SaulėGrid had three DAGs, naming was easy. But naming conventions become essential as the DAG count grows. A descriptive, consistent naming system lets the team find any DAG instantly, understand its purpose from the name alone, and avoid collisions between DAGs authored by different engineers. This lesson defines the rules for DAG IDs and file names that SaulėGrid follows — rules that prevent the disorganization that creeps in when naming is left to individual preference.
DAG ID Rules

The dag_id is a string that uniquely identifies the DAG across the entire Airflow installation. Airflow imposes minimal technical constraints: the ID can contain letters, numbers, hyphens, underscores, and periods. But practical constraints are more important. The ID appears in the web UI (long IDs get truncated), in log file paths (special characters cause problems), in CLI commands (you type it frequently), and in the metadata database (it is the primary key for DAG lookups). SaulėGrid's rules: use lowercase, use underscores to separate words, prefix with dag_, and include the layer and domain.

# SaulėGrid's DAG ID naming convention
# Pattern: dag_{layer}_{domain}

# Bronze layer — raw ingestion DAGs
dag_id='dag_bronze_meters'        # Meter readings from SFTP
dag_id='dag_bronze_generation'     # Solar inverter telemetry
dag_id='dag_bronze_weather'        # Weather forecast API
dag_id='dag_bronze_tariffs'        # VERT regulatory tariffs

# Silver layer — transformation DAGs
dag_id='dag_silver_transform'      # Clean, validate, separate
dag_id='dag_silver_battery'        # Battery state computation

# Gold layer — aggregation DAGs
dag_id='dag_gold_aggregate'        # Regional summaries
dag_id='dag_gold_billing'          # Monthly billing
dag_id='dag_gold_regulatory'       # VERT compliance reports
Why dag_ Prefix Matters

The dag_ prefix is a SaulėGrid convention, not an Airflow requirement. It serves two purposes. First, when filtering or searching in the web UI, typing 'dag_bronze' instantly narrows the list to all Bronze ingestion DAGs. Second, in the dags/ directory, the prefix makes DAG files visually distinct from helper modules: dag_bronze_meters.py is a DAG, sftp_utils.py is a helper. This distinction matters because the scheduler parses both files, but only the dag_ files should contain DAG objects.
File Naming Convention

SaulėGrid matches the filename to the dag_id: dag_bronze_meters lives in dag_bronze_meters.py. This one-to-one mapping means you can find any DAG's source code by converting the dag_id to a filename. Without this convention, debugging requires searching through files to find which one contains the DAG you are investigating — a minor inconvenience with 3 files, but a real productivity drain with 30.

rows: No dag_ prefix — less clear in UI searches
Mismatch — impossible to find without searching

blocks.content: Duplicate DAG IDs

If two Python files define a DAG with the same dag_id, Airflow's behavior is unpredictable. The scheduler may register one or the other, depending on which file it parses first. There is no error message — the duplicate is silently ignored. SaulėGrid prevents this with their naming convention: since each file is named after its dag_id, a duplicate ID would require a duplicate filename, which the filesystem prevents. If you break the naming convention, you lose this safety net.

blocks.title: No error on duplicates
blocks.content: Airflow does not raise an error when two files define the same dag_id. The second DAG is silently ignored — it does not appear in the UI, and no log message explains why. If a new DAG you created is not appearing, check whether another file already uses the same dag_id. This silent behavior is one of the most frustrating debugging experiences for new Airflow users.
Renaming a DAG

Changing a dag_id is not a rename — it is a deletion and a creation. Airflow treats the old dag_id and the new dag_id as two completely different DAGs. The old DAG's run history, task instance records, and log files remain in the database under the old name. The new DAG starts with no history. SaulėGrid learned this when they renamed dag_ingest_meters to dag_bronze_meters: the new DAG appeared with no run history, and the old DAG's history became orphaned. The lesson: choose your dag_id carefully, because changing it later loses history.

blocks.title: Name it right the first time
blocks.content: The dag_id is permanent in practice. Choose a convention before writing your first DAG, not after writing your tenth. SaulėGrid's dag_{layer}_{domain} convention scales from 3 DAGs to 30 without modification. Whatever convention your team adopts, the two rules are consistency (every DAG follows the same pattern) and descriptiveness (the name tells you what the DAG does without opening the file).

Module 2 introduced the scheduler's parsing cycle at a high level: it reads Python files from the dags/ directory, discovers DAG objects, and registers them. This lesson goes deeper into what actually happens during parsing, because understanding the parsing process explains several behaviors that surprise new Airflow users: why DAGs disappear when there is a syntax error, why heavy imports slow down the entire Airflow installation, and why code at the top level of your DAG file runs every few seconds.
Parsing Is Execution

The scheduler does not read your DAG file as a text document. It executes it as a Python module. When the scheduler parses dag_bronze_meters.py, it runs the equivalent of import dag_bronze_meters. Every line of top-level code executes: import statements run, function definitions are stored, variables are assigned, and the DAG context manager creates the DAG object. The scheduler then inspects the module's namespace, finds every DAG object, and registers them. This is why your DAG file must create the DAG at module scope — if the DAG is created inside a function that is never called, the scheduler cannot find it.
The Parsing Frequency

The scheduler parses every DAG file repeatedly. The min_file_process_interval setting (default 30 seconds) determines the minimum time between parses of the same file. This means your DAG file's top-level code executes roughly every 30 seconds for the entire lifetime of the Airflow installation. If your file takes 2 seconds to parse, and you have 50 DAG files, the scheduler spends 100 seconds per cycle just parsing — leaving little time for actual scheduling work. This is why parsing performance matters.

blocks.title: What happens when the scheduler parses a DAG file
blocks.content:
Scheduler reads dags/dag_bronze_meters.py
          │
          ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Execute the file as Python module  │
│                                     │
│  1. from airflow import DAG   ← import runs
│  2. from airflow.operators... ← import runs
│  3. def download_from_sftp(): ← function stored
│  4. def validate_meter_data():← function stored
│  5. with DAG(...) as dag:     ← DAG object created
│  6.   download = Python...    ← task created
│  7.   validate = Python...    ← task created
│  8.   download >> validate    ← dependency set
│                                     │
└───────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                │
                ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Inspect module namespace           │
│  Find DAG objects: [dag]            │
│  Register in metadata database      │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
                │
                ▼
        Wait 30 seconds, then repeat
blocks.caption: Every line of top-level code runs on every parse. Function bodies (the actual task logic) are only stored, not executed — they run later when the executor invokes them.

visual-recommendation.description: Animated sequence showing the scheduler executing a DAG file line by line, highlighting which lines run on every parse vs which lines only run at task execution time.

blocks.content: Top-Level Code Dangers

Because parsing is execution, any heavy operation at the top level of your DAG file runs every 30 seconds. SaulėGrid learned this the hard way when Matas wrote a DAG file that imported pandas at the top level. Pandas takes about 1 second to import, and with 12 DAG files each importing pandas, the scheduler was spending 12 seconds per cycle on imports alone. The fix was simple: move the pandas import inside the task callable, so it runs only when the task executes, not on every parse.

# BAD — pandas import runs every 30 seconds during parsing
import pandas as pd  # 1 second import on every parse cycle

def process_meters():
    df = pd.read_csv('meters.csv')
    # ... processing ...


# GOOD — pandas import runs only when the task executes
def process_meters():
    import pandas as pd  # import inside the function
    df = pd.read_csv('meters.csv')
    # ... processing ...

blocks.title: The import rule
blocks.content: At the top level of your DAG file, only import airflow modules, datetime, and lightweight standard library modules. Heavy libraries (pandas, requests, boto3, sqlalchemy) belong inside task callables. This rule is not about code style — it is about scheduler performance. A slow-parsing DAG file degrades the entire Airflow installation, not just that one DAG.
What Happens on Parse Failure

If a DAG file has a syntax error — a missing parenthesis, an undefined variable, an import that fails — the scheduler cannot parse it. The DAG is not registered, and any previously registered DAG from that file disappears from the web UI. The scheduler logs the parsing error, but the error is visible only in the scheduler logs, not in the web UI's DAG list. SaulėGrid's team runs a quick syntax check (python -c 'import dag_bronze_meters') before deploying DAG files to prevent parse failures from removing live DAGs.
The DagBag

Internally, the scheduler uses a DagBag object to collect all DAGs discovered during parsing. The DagBag holds every DAG from every file in memory, and it is the data structure that the scheduler queries when evaluating schedules and dependencies. You rarely interact with the DagBag directly, but it appears in log messages and error output. If you see 'DagBag' in a log entry, it refers to the scheduler's in-memory collection of all parsed DAGs.

blocks.title: Parse time is global cost
