blocks.content: Every second your DAG file takes to parse is a second added to every parsing cycle for the life of the Airflow installation. A file that takes 3 seconds to parse costs 3 seconds every 30 seconds — roughly 10% of the scheduler's capacity, consumed by one file. Keep DAG files lightweight: define the DAG structure and task references, but put the heavy logic inside task callables where it runs once per execution, not once per parse.

A DAG file is a static definition — it describes what tasks exist and how they relate. A DAG Run is a dynamic execution — it represents one specific run of the DAG, with its own state, its own task instances, and its own timeline. Understanding the distinction between the DAG (the definition) and the DAG Run (the execution) is essential for reading the Airflow UI, interpreting logs, and communicating about pipeline behavior. This lesson covers how DAG Runs are created, what states they pass through, and how they relate to the task instances inside them.
How DAG Runs Are Created

DAG Runs are created by two mechanisms. The primary mechanism is the scheduler: when the current time passes the next scheduled interval, the scheduler creates a DAG Run in the metadata database. SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters has schedule='@daily', so the scheduler creates one DAG Run per day. The secondary mechanism is manual triggering: a user clicks the 'Trigger DAG' button in the web UI or runs an API call, which inserts a DAG Run record directly. Manually triggered runs have a run_type of 'manual' and are independent of the schedule.
DAG Run States

A DAG Run passes through a lifecycle of states that reflect its progress. When the scheduler creates the run, its state is 'queued.' When the first task begins executing, the state changes to 'running.' When all tasks have completed successfully, the state becomes 'success.' If any task fails and retries are exhausted, the state becomes 'failed.' These state transitions are visible in the web UI's Grid View, where each column represents a DAG Run and the column header shows the state.

headers: What You See in the UI
rows: DAG Run is created, waiting for tasks to be scheduled
At least one task is currently executing
At least one task failed with no retries remaining

blocks.content: DAG Run vs Task Instance

A DAG Run contains one task instance for each task in the DAG. SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters has three tasks, so each DAG Run contains three task instances: one for download_meters, one for validate_readings, and one for load_to_bronze. The DAG Run's state is derived from the states of its task instances: if all three are 'success,' the DAG Run is 'success.' If one is 'failed,' the DAG Run is 'failed.' If any is 'running,' the DAG Run is 'running.' The DAG Run is a summary; the task instances are the details.

blocks.title: The relationship between DAG, DAG Run, and Task Instance
blocks.content:
DAG Definition (dag_bronze_meters):
  Tasks: download_meters, validate_readings, load_to_bronze
  Schedule: @daily

DAG Run: March 22                  DAG Run: March 23
┌──────────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────────┐
│ logical_date: 3/22   │          │ logical_date: 3/23   │
│ state: success ✓     │          │ state: failed ✗      │
│                      │          │                      │
│ Task Instances:      │          │ Task Instances:      │
│  download:  success  │          │  download:  failed   │
│  validate:  success  │          │  validate:  no_status│
│  load:      success  │          │  load:      no_status│
└──────────────────────┘          └──────────────────────┘

One DAG → many DAG Runs → each Run has its own Task Instances
blocks.caption: Each DAG Run is independent. The March 22nd run succeeded. The March 23rd run failed because the download task failed — the downstream tasks never ran, which is exactly what dependency protection provides.

visual-recommendation.description: Hierarchical diagram showing a DAG definition at the top, branching into multiple DAG Runs below it, each containing task instances with color-coded states.

blocks.content: Identifying DAG Runs
The Logical Date

Every DAG Run has a logical_date (formerly called execution_date) that identifies which data interval it covers. For a daily DAG, the logical_date is the start of the data interval — not the time the DAG Run actually started. SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters runs after midnight to process the previous day's data. The DAG Run that executes at 00:05 on March 23rd has a logical_date of March 22nd — because it processes March 22nd's meter readings. This distinction is covered in detail in Module 7, but knowing it exists prevents confusion when you see dates in the UI that seem to be off by one day.

blocks.title: Why this matters for debugging
blocks.content: When Ieva investigates a failure, she starts with the DAG Run: which date failed? She then drills into the task instances within that run: which task failed? She reads the failed task's log: what was the error? This top-down approach — DAG Run → Task Instance → Log — is the standard debugging workflow. Every view in the Airflow UI is organized around this hierarchy.

The DAG Run and task instance model is what transforms Airflow from a scheduler into an orchestrator. Cron fires and forgets — there is no record of what happened. Airflow creates a DAG Run, tracks every task within it, records the outcome, and keeps the history permanently. SaulėGrid can answer questions like 'how often has the Bronze pipeline failed in the last month?' or 'what is the average duration of the download task?' — questions that were unanswerable before Airflow.

A well-configured DAG has more than just tasks and dependencies. It has metadata that makes it findable, attributable, and understandable — tags for filtering, owners for accountability, and documentation for context. These metadata fields are not required by Airflow, but they are required by any team that operates more than a handful of DAGs. SaulėGrid treats metadata configuration as seriously as task configuration, because a DAG that nobody can find, nobody owns, and nobody understands is a maintenance burden waiting to happen.
Tags — Finding DAGs

Tags are string labels attached to a DAG that enable filtering in the web UI. When SaulėGrid's DAG list shows 12 DAGs, Ieva can click on the 'bronze' tag to see only the four Bronze ingestion DAGs. She can click 'meters' to see all DAGs that involve meter data, regardless of layer. Tags are additive — a DAG can have multiple tags, and filtering by a tag shows all DAGs that include it. SaulėGrid uses three categories of tags: layer (bronze, silver, gold), domain (meters, generation, weather, billing, regulatory), and team (saulegrid).

# SaulėGrid's tagging convention — three categories
# Layer + Domain + Team

# Bronze meter ingestion
with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    tags=['bronze', 'meters', 'saulegrid'],
    # ...
) as dag:
    pass

# Gold billing aggregation
with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_gold_billing',
    tags=['gold', 'billing', 'saulegrid'],
    # ...
) as dag:
    pass

# Gold regulatory reporting — multiple domain tags
with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_gold_regulatory',
    tags=['gold', 'regulatory', 'vert', 'saulegrid'],
    # ...
) as dag:
    pass
Owner — Accountability

The owner field in default_args identifies who is responsible for the DAG. It appears in the UI's DAG list, in failure notification emails, and in the Airflow REST API. SaulėGrid sets owner to the engineer who authored and maintains the DAG. When the billing team reports an issue with the Gold aggregation, they check the owner field to know who to contact. The owner_links parameter on the DAG constructor can link the owner name to an email address or Slack handle, making the contact information one click away.
Documentation — Understanding

The doc_md parameter accepts a Markdown string that Airflow renders in the web UI when you click on a DAG's documentation tab. This is where SaulėGrid documents the DAG's purpose, its data sources, its dependencies, and any operational notes that future engineers need. Good documentation answers three questions: what does this DAG do, what does it depend on, and what should I check if it fails?

# SaulėGrid's documentation convention — doc_md on every DAG

with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    doc_md="""
    ## Bronze Meter Ingestion

    Downloads daily meter readings from SaulėGrid's SFTP server
    and writes them to the Bronze layer as raw Parquet files.

    **Source:** sftp.saulegrid.lt:/exports/meters/
    **Schedule:** Daily at midnight (UTC)
    **Owner:** Matas (matas@saulegrid.lt)

    **Dependencies:**
    - SFTP server must be accessible (connection: sftp_saulegrid_meters)
    - Bronze storage path must be writable

    **If this DAG fails:**
    1. Check SFTP connectivity (most common cause: server maintenance)
    2. Check the download_meters task log for timeout errors
    3. If SFTP is down, wait and retry — the file is available until noon
    """,
    # ... other parameters ...
) as dag:
    pass
The description Parameter

The description parameter is a short, one-line summary that appears next to the DAG name in the web UI list. It complements doc_md: the description gives a quick overview at a glance, while doc_md provides full context when you click through. SaulėGrid keeps descriptions under 80 characters and uses them to distinguish similar DAGs: 'Ingest daily meter readings from SFTP to Bronze layer' versus 'Ingest hourly solar inverter telemetry from SFTP to Bronze layer.'

blocks.title: Documentation is for future you
blocks.content: The engineer who writes a DAG understands it perfectly. Six months later, that same engineer — or their replacement — will not remember why the retry count is 5, why the schedule is 06:00 instead of midnight, or why there is a 10-minute sleep between tasks. The doc_md field is where you record those decisions. SaulėGrid's rule: every DAG must have doc_md that answers 'what does this do, what does it need, and what should I check if it breaks.'
Keyword Pills in the Reader

Tags, owner, and description are the metadata that makes the Airflow web UI usable at scale. Without tags, you scroll through a flat list. Without owner, you do not know who to ask. Without description, you open every DAG to understand what it does. These three fields take 30 seconds to add during DAG authoring and save hours of confusion over the DAG's lifetime. SaulėGrid treats incomplete metadata as a code review blocker — no DAG is deployed without tags, owner, and doc_md.

The previous nine lessons taught DAG concepts individually: the definition, the file structure, the context manager, parameters, defaults, naming, parsing, state, and metadata. This lesson brings everything together by presenting SaulėGrid's complete DAG organization — how all six DAGs are structured, named, tagged, documented, and arranged in the dags/ directory. Seeing the full picture shows how the individual concepts compose into a coherent, maintainable pipeline system.
The Complete DAG Inventory
The Directory Layout

# SaulėGrid's dags/ directory — complete layout

dags/
├── dag_bronze_meters.py          # Daily meter SFTP ingestion
├── dag_bronze_generation.py      # Daily inverter SFTP ingestion
├── dag_bronze_weather.py         # 6-hourly weather API ingestion
├── dag_silver_transform.py       # Daily Bronze → Silver pipeline
├── dag_gold_aggregate.py         # Daily Silver → Gold aggregation
├── dag_gold_regulatory.py        # Weekly VERT regulatory report
├── .airflowignore                # Excludes test files from parsing
└── helpers/
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── defaults.py               # Shared default_args dictionary
    ├── sftp_utils.py             # SFTP download and checksum helpers
    ├── validation.py             # Data quality check functions
    ├── alerting.py               # Email and Slack notification helpers
    └── constants.py              # Region codes, file paths, thresholds
Shared Configuration

The helpers/defaults.py module exports the standard default_args dictionary that every DAG imports. It also exports the SaulėGrid-wide constants: the four region codes (VILNIUS, KAUNAS, KLAIPEDA, SIAULIAI), the Bronze/Silver/Gold storage paths, and the quality threshold for daily_kw validation. By centralizing these values, SaulėGrid ensures that all DAGs use the same retry policy, the same storage paths, and the same quality rules. When a constant changes, one file update propagates to all DAGs.
The Scheduling Strategy

SaulėGrid staggers their schedules to ensure that upstream DAGs complete before downstream DAGs start. Bronze DAGs run between midnight and 01:00. The Silver transformation runs at 02:00, giving Bronze two hours to complete. The Gold aggregation runs at 04:00, giving Silver two hours. The regulatory report runs on Monday mornings at 06:00. These time gaps are safety buffers, not dependencies — the actual dependency enforcement comes from sensors and triggers that you will learn in Module 10. But the staggered schedule reduces the frequency of downstream DAGs waiting for upstream data.

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's nightly pipeline timeline
blocks.content:
00:00   00:15   02:00                04:00                06:00 (Mon)
  │       │       │                    │                    │
  ▼       ▼       ▼                    ▼                    ▼
[Bronze  [Bronze  [Silver             [Gold                [Gold
 meters]  gen.]    transform]          aggregate]           regulatory]
  ~15m    ~10m     ~45m                ~30m                 ~20m

  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
  Bronze layer         Silver layer         Gold layer
  (raw ingestion)      (clean + validate)   (business-ready)
blocks.caption: The staggered schedule creates a natural flow: Bronze ingests raw data, Silver cleans it, Gold produces business outputs. Each layer starts after the previous layer's typical completion time, with buffer for slow runs.

visual-recommendation.description: Timeline visualization showing SaulėGrid's six DAGs arranged on a nightly schedule with color-coded bars indicating Bronze, Silver, and Gold layers and their typical durations.

blocks.content: The Tagging Convention

Every SaulėGrid DAG carries three categories of tags: layer, domain, and team. In the web UI, Ieva can filter by 'bronze' to see all three Bronze DAGs, filter by 'meters' to see the Bronze and Silver DAGs that touch meter data, or filter by 'ieva' to see DAGs she owns. The tags also serve as documentation: anyone looking at the DAG list can immediately understand each DAG's layer and domain without opening the file. SaulėGrid's tag list is short and controlled — adding a new tag requires team agreement to prevent tag sprawl.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: You now know how to create, configure, name, document, and organize DAGs. In Module 4, you will learn about the tasks inside those DAGs — the operators that define what each task actually does. The DAG is the container; the operators are the contents. The pattern from this module (define, configure, connect with >>) remains the same — you are adding new types of building blocks, not changing the structure.

SaulėGrid's six DAGs, twelve helper functions, and one shared configuration module form a complete orchestration platform for a two-person team managing 400,000 meters. Every concept from this module — the DAG abstraction, the context manager, parameters, defaults, naming, parsing, state, and metadata — appears in every one of these files. The concepts are not academic; they are the daily tools that Matas and Ieva use to keep SaulėGrid's data flowing reliably from SFTP to billing reports.

chapters.title: Tasks and Operators
chapters.description: Understand tasks as the units of work within a DAG, learn the core operators (BashOperator, PythonOperator, EmptyOperator), master task parameterization and retry configuration, and establish naming and design conventions.

blocks.content: Module 3 taught the DAG — the container that defines dependencies and schedules. But a DAG without tasks is an empty shell. Tasks are the units of work inside a DAG: each task represents one step in the pipeline, one action the executor runs. SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters has three tasks: download the meter file, validate the data, and load the result to Bronze storage. Each task does one thing, knows what it should do, and reports whether it succeeded or failed. This module teaches you how to define, configure, and organize tasks — the building blocks that fill the DAG container you built in Module 3.
Tasks vs Operators

Airflow makes a distinction between two related concepts that confuse newcomers. An operator is a class — a Python template that defines a type of work. A task is an instance of that class — a specific, configured piece of work inside a specific DAG. The PythonOperator is a class that knows how to call a Python function. When you write PythonOperator(task_id='download_meters', python_callable=download_from_sftp), you are creating a task (an instance of the PythonOperator class) that will call a specific function with a specific name inside a specific DAG. The operator defines what kind of work; the task defines the specific work.

blocks.title: The relationship between operators and tasks
blocks.content:
OPERATOR (class — the template)
┌──────────────────────────────────┐
│         PythonOperator            │
│                                  │
│  Knows how to:                   │
│  - Accept a Python callable      │
│  - Run it on the executor        │
│  - Capture the return value      │
│  - Handle exceptions             │
└──────────────┬───────────────────┘
               │ creates instances
       ┌───────┴────────┐
       ▼                ▼
TASK (instance 1)    TASK (instance 2)
┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐
│ download_meters│  │validate_readings│
│                │  │                │
│ Calls:         │  │ Calls:         │
│ download_sftp()│  │ validate_data()│
│ In DAG:        │  │ In DAG:        │
│ dag_bronze_... │  │ dag_bronze_... │
└────────────────┘  └────────────────┘
blocks.caption: One operator class can produce many tasks. Each task has its own task_id, its own configuration, and its own execution history. The operator is the blueprint; the task is the building.

blocks.content: What a Task Contains

Every task has four essential properties. First, a task_id — a unique name within its DAG that identifies it in the UI, in logs, and in the metadata database. Second, an operator type — which class it instantiates, determining what kind of work it performs. Third, configuration — parameters like retries, timeout, and retry_delay that control the task's execution behavior. Fourth, the work itself — the Python callable, the bash command, the SQL query, or whatever the operator type requires. Together, these four properties make a task a self-contained unit that the executor can run independently.
Tasks Are Independent at Runtime

At runtime, each task is an independent unit. The executor picks up a task from the queue, runs it in its own process (or container, depending on the executor type), and reports success or failure to the metadata database. The task does not know what other tasks are in the DAG, what their states are, or when they will run. It does not know whether it is the first task or the last. It receives its configuration, executes its work, and returns. The dependency logic that determines execution order lives in the DAG and the scheduler — not in the tasks themselves. This independence is what makes tasks retry-safe: if a task fails and is retried, the retry runs the same isolated unit of work without side effects on other tasks.
Task Instances

A task is a definition in your DAG file. A task instance is one execution of that task within a specific DAG Run. SaulėGrid's download_meters task runs once per day — each daily execution is a separate task instance with its own state (success, failed, running), its own log file, its own start time and duration, and its own retry count. The metadata database stores every task instance, which is why SaulėGrid can answer questions like 'how often did the download task fail in March?' or 'what is the average duration of the validation task?' — each data point comes from a task instance record.

rows: PythonOperator — knows how to run Python functions
download_meters on March 22nd — succeeded in 8 minutes

blocks.title: One task, one job
blocks.content: A well-designed task does exactly one thing. SaulėGrid's download_meters downloads the file. It does not validate the data, compute aggregates, or send alerts. If the download fails, you know the problem is the download — not a validation bug hiding inside a download task. This single-responsibility principle makes tasks easier to understand, easier to debug, and easier to retry. A task that does three things must be debugged as three things, retried as three things, and monitored as three things.
The Operator Toolkit

The next lesson introduces the three core operators that SaulėGrid uses to build tasks: the BashOperator for running shell commands, the PythonOperator for running Python functions, and the EmptyOperator for structural checkpoints. These three operators cover the vast majority of tasks in a junior-level Airflow installation, and understanding them gives you the vocabulary to read and understand any SaulėGrid DAG file. Each operator fills the task container with a different type of work.

Airflow ships with dozens of operator classes, and the provider ecosystem adds hundreds more. But at the junior level, three operators cover nearly every use case: the PythonOperator for running Python functions, the BashOperator for running shell commands, and the EmptyOperator for structural placeholders. This lesson provides an overview of the operator ecosystem and positions these three core operators before the next three lessons explore each one in depth.
The Operator Hierarchy

Every operator in Airflow inherits from the BaseOperator class, which provides the common functionality: task_id, retries, retry_delay, execution_timeout, trigger_rule, and all the other parameters that every task shares. When you create a PythonOperator, it inherits everything from BaseOperator and adds one thing: the ability to call a Python function. When you create a BashOperator, it inherits the same base and adds the ability to run a shell command. This inheritance means that every parameter you learn for one operator works on all operators — retries, timeouts, and callbacks are universal.
Core Operators

rows: Validation logic, data transformation, API parsing
SFTP download scripts, file operations, CLI tools

blocks.content: Provider Operators

Beyond the core operators, Airflow's provider packages add operators for specific platforms. The PostgresOperator runs SQL queries against a PostgreSQL database. The SFTPOperator transfers files to or from an SFTP server. The SimpleHttpOperator makes HTTP requests to REST APIs. The S3CreateObjectOperator writes files to Amazon S3. Each provider operator encapsulates the connection logic for its platform, so your DAG code focuses on what to do rather than how to connect. SaulėGrid uses provider operators for their database writes and SFTP transfers — you will learn about them in Module 8 when you study connections and hooks.
Choosing an Operator

The decision framework for choosing an operator is straightforward. If the work is Python logic — data transformation, validation, parsing, computation — use the PythonOperator. If the work is a shell command or an external script — file operations, CLI tools, system commands — use the BashOperator. If there is no work to do but you need a dependency anchor — a checkpoint between pipeline phases or a start sentinel — use the EmptyOperator. If the work involves a specific external platform (database, cloud storage, API) and a provider operator exists for it, use the provider operator instead of writing custom code in a PythonOperator.

blocks.title: Operator decision tree
blocks.content:
What does the task need to do?
         │
         ├── Run Python code?
         │         │
         │         ▼
         │   PythonOperator
         │   (validation, transformation, API parsing)
         │
         ├── Run a shell command?
         │         │
         │         ▼
         │   BashOperator
         │   (scripts, file ops, CLI tools)
         │
         ├── Nothing — just a dependency anchor?
         │         │
         │         ▼
         │   EmptyOperator
         │   (checkpoints, start/end sentinels)
         │
         └── Interact with a specific platform?
                   │
                   ▼
             Provider Operator
             (PostgresOperator, SFTPOperator, etc.)
blocks.caption: Start with the three core operators. Use provider operators when they exist for your target platform. Write custom Python code in PythonOperator only when no provider operator matches your need.

visual-recommendation.description: Decision tree flowchart with color-coded operator boxes showing PythonOperator (blue), BashOperator (green), EmptyOperator (gray), and Provider Operator (orange) as decision outcomes.

blocks.content: SaulėGrid's Operator Distribution

Across SaulėGrid's six DAGs, the PythonOperator is the most common — it handles validation logic, data parsing, and transformation orchestration. The BashOperator appears in Bronze DAGs for calling SFTP download scripts that were originally cron jobs (Matas wrapped the existing scripts rather than rewriting them). The EmptyOperator appears as a start and end sentinel in multi-branch DAGs, providing clean dependency anchors that make the Graph View easier to read. Provider operators (PostgresOperator, SFTPOperator) handle direct interactions with external systems. This distribution is typical — most Airflow installations are PythonOperator-heavy.

blocks.title: Operators are not the logic
blocks.content: An operator defines how a task runs — not what it computes. The PythonOperator knows how to call a function, but the function's body is your code. The BashOperator knows how to run a command, but the command is your script. When debugging a task failure, the problem is almost always in your code (the callable, the script), not in the operator. Operators are infrastructure; your callables and scripts are the business logic.

The next three lessons explore each core operator in depth, one per lesson: the BashOperator for shell commands and existing scripts, the PythonOperator for Python functions and custom logic, and the EmptyOperator for structural organization and dependency anchoring. Each lesson shows the operator's complete syntax, its most important parameters, its edge cases, and its specific role in SaulėGrid's production pipeline architecture.

The BashOperator runs a shell command on the worker machine. When the executor picks up a BashOperator task, it spawns a Bash shell, executes the command, captures the exit code, and reports success (exit code 0) or failure (any non-zero exit code). The BashOperator is the simplest way to call existing scripts, run CLI tools, or perform file operations from an Airflow task. SaulėGrid uses it for their SFTP download scripts — scripts that existed before Airflow and work perfectly as shell commands.
Basic Usage

from airflow.operators.bash import BashOperator

# Run a simple shell command
list_files = BashOperator(
    task_id='list_bronze_files',
    bash_command='ls -la /data/bronze/meters/',
)

# Run an existing script — SaulėGrid's original SFTP download
download = BashOperator(
    task_id='download_meters',
    bash_command='/scripts/download_meters.sh ',
    # Note the trailing space — explained below
)

The bash_command parameter accepts any string that is valid in a Bash shell. Single commands, pipelines with pipes, multi-line scripts with semicolons, and script file paths all work. The executor runs the command in a temporary Bash subprocess on the worker machine and captures both stdout and stderr in the task log. When the command finishes, the exit code determines the task state: 0 means success, anything else means failure. This exit-code convention is the same one Unix has used for decades — your existing scripts work without modification.
The Trailing Space

If your bash_command ends with a filename (like a script path), Airflow's Jinja template engine may interpret it as a template file reference rather than a command. Adding a trailing space after the path prevents this misinterpretation. SaulėGrid adds a trailing space to every bash_command that references a script file: bash_command='/scripts/download_meters.sh ' (note the space after .sh). This is a well-known Airflow quirk that trips up every new user exactly once. The space is harmless to Bash but prevents the template engine from looking for a file to render.

blocks.title: Always add a trailing space
blocks.content: When bash_command contains a file path as the last element, add a trailing space. Without it, Airflow may try to render the file as a Jinja template instead of executing it as a command. The error message — 'template not found' — is cryptic if you do not know about this behavior. The fix is always a single space character at the end of the string.
Multi-Line Commands

# Multi-line bash commands using triple-quoted strings
# SaulėGrid's SFTP download with logging and verification

download_and_verify = BashOperator(
    task_id='download_and_verify',
    bash_command="""
        echo "Starting SFTP download at $(date)" 
        sftp etl@sftp.saulegrid.lt:/exports/meters/daily.csv \
            /data/bronze/meters/{{ ds }}.csv 
        
        ROW_COUNT=$(wc -l < /data/bronze/meters/{{ ds }}.csv)
        echo "Downloaded $ROW_COUNT rows"
        
        if [ "$ROW_COUNT" -lt 100 ]; then
            echo "ERROR: Row count too low — possible partial download"
            exit 1
        fi
    """,
)

Multi-line commands use Python triple-quoted strings. The {{ ds }} syntax is an Airflow template variable that expands to the logical date in YYYY-MM-DD format — you will learn about templating in Module 7. The example above downloads a file, counts its rows, and fails the task (exit 1) if the row count is suspiciously low. This pattern — download then verify — is SaulėGrid's standard approach for Bronze ingestion tasks that use the BashOperator. The exit code drives the task state: if the row count check fails, the task is marked as failed and the retry mechanism kicks in.
Environment Variables

The BashOperator's env parameter lets you pass environment variables to the command. SaulėGrid uses this for scripts that need configuration without hardcoded values: the SFTP hostname, the download directory, and the expected row count threshold are all passed as environment variables rather than embedded in the script. This keeps the scripts portable — the same script works in development (with a test SFTP server) and production (with the real server) by changing only the environment variables passed by the DAG.

# Passing environment variables to a BashOperator
# The script reads these variables instead of using hardcoded values

download = BashOperator(
    task_id='download_meters',
    bash_command='/scripts/download_meters.sh ',
    env={
        'SFTP_HOST': 'sftp.saulegrid.lt',
        'SFTP_USER': 'etl',
        'DOWNLOAD_DIR': '/data/bronze/meters/',
        'MIN_ROW_COUNT': '100000',
    },
    append_env=True,  # keep existing env vars, add these on top
)
When to Use BashOperator

Use the BashOperator when the work is a shell command or an existing script. SaulėGrid's SFTP download was originally a Bash script run by cron — wrapping it in a BashOperator was a one-line change. Use the BashOperator for file system operations (mv, cp, rm, ls), for calling CLI tools (aws s3 cp, gsutil, psql), and for running existing scripts that you do not want to rewrite in Python. Do not use the BashOperator for complex Python logic — that belongs in the PythonOperator, which provides better error handling, type safety, and debugging.

blocks.title: BashOperator as a migration bridge
blocks.content: Many teams adopt Airflow by wrapping their existing cron scripts in BashOperator tasks. This is a valid first step — you get dependency management, state tracking, and monitoring without rewriting any logic. Over time, teams often migrate high-value scripts from BashOperator to PythonOperator for better error handling and testability. SaulėGrid keeps their SFTP download as a BashOperator because the script is simple and stable, but they wrote all new logic as PythonOperator tasks.

The PythonOperator is the most commonly used operator in Airflow. It runs a Python function on the executor — the same kind of function you write in any Python program. The function receives its arguments, does its work, and returns. If the function raises an exception, the task fails. If it returns normally, the task succeeds. SaulėGrid uses the PythonOperator for all their validation logic, data quality checks, and transformation orchestration — every task that involves Python logic rather than shell commands.
Basic Usage

from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator


def validate_meter_data():
    """Check SaulėGrid's meter readings for quality issues."""
    print("Loading meter data from Bronze...")
    # In production, this would read from the Bronze Parquet file
    total_rows = 400000
    null_meters = 23
    negative_kw = 7
    valid_rows = total_rows - null_meters - negative_kw
    print(f"Validation complete: {valid_rows} valid rows")
    print(f"Removed {null_meters} null meter_ids, {negative_kw} negative daily_kw")
    return valid_rows  # return value is stored in XCom (Module 5)


# The task — an instance of PythonOperator
validate = PythonOperator(
    task_id='validate_readings',
    python_callable=validate_meter_data,
)

The python_callable parameter takes a reference to a Python function — not a function call. You pass validate_meter_data (without parentheses), and the executor calls it at runtime. If you accidentally pass validate_meter_data() (with parentheses), the function executes during DAG parsing — every 30 seconds — which is almost certainly not what you want. This is the most common PythonOperator mistake and it was covered in Module 3, but it bears repeating because the consequences range from surprising log output to production data being modified on every scheduler parse cycle.
Passing Arguments

If your callable needs arguments, the PythonOperator provides two ways to pass them. The op_args parameter passes positional arguments as a list. The op_kwargs parameter passes keyword arguments as a dictionary. SaulėGrid uses op_kwargs exclusively for readability — named keyword arguments are significantly easier to understand when reviewing DAG code than positional arguments that require checking the function signature to know what each value represents.

def validate_meter_data(file_path, min_row_count, quality_threshold):
    """Validate meter readings with configurable thresholds."""
    print(f"Validating {file_path}...")
    print(f"Minimum rows: {min_row_count}")
    print(f"Quality threshold: {quality_threshold} kW")
    # ... validation logic ...


# Passing arguments with op_kwargs (preferred — explicit names)
validate = PythonOperator(
    task_id='validate_readings',
    python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    op_kwargs={
        'file_path': '/data/bronze/meters/2026-03-22.csv',
        'min_row_count': 350000,
        'quality_threshold': 0.5,
    },
)

# Alternative: op_args (positional — less readable)
validate_alt = PythonOperator(
    task_id='validate_readings_alt',
    python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    op_args=['/data/bronze/meters/2026-03-22.csv', 350000, 0.5],
)
The Context Dictionary

Every PythonOperator callable can access Airflow's runtime context — metadata about the current DAG Run, the task instance, the logical date, and the configuration. To access it, add **kwargs to your function signature or use the get_current_context() function. The context includes the execution date, the DAG Run object, the task instance object, and template variables. SaulėGrid uses the context to dynamically construct file paths based on the logical date, so the same callable works for any day's data without hardcoded dates.

def validate_meter_data(**kwargs):
    """Use the Airflow context to build the file path dynamically."""
    # The context provides the logical date of this DAG Run
    logical_date = kwargs['logical_date']
    date_str = logical_date.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
    file_path = f'/data/bronze/meters/{date_str}.csv'
    print(f"Validating {file_path}...")
    # ... validation logic using the date-specific file ...


validate = PythonOperator(
    task_id='validate_readings',
    python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    # No op_kwargs needed — the date comes from the context
)
Error Handling

When the callable raises an exception, the PythonOperator catches it and marks the task as failed. The exception type, message, and full traceback appear in the task log. SaulėGrid's validation function raises a ValueError when critical quality checks fail — for example, when the row count drops below a threshold that suggests a partial download. This explicit failure mechanism is preferable to returning silently with bad data, because the retry and alerting systems only activate on task failure, not on successful tasks that produced wrong results.

blocks.title: Fail loudly, not silently
blocks.content: If your callable encounters a problem — missing file, bad data, unexpected schema — raise an exception. Do not return None and continue. Do not print a warning and hope someone reads the log. Raise the exception, let the task fail, and let Airflow's retry and alerting mechanisms handle the rest. Silent failures (Module 1, lesson 2) are the most expensive failures in data engineering. Loud failures — exceptions that trigger retries and alerts — are cheap.

blocks.title: PythonOperator is your workhorse
blocks.content: In most Airflow installations, the PythonOperator accounts for 60-80% of all tasks. It is the default choice when you need to run any Python logic: validation, transformation, API calls, file parsing, metric computation, notification sending. Master the PythonOperator and you can build any pipeline. The other operators are optimizations for specific use cases, not replacements.

The EmptyOperator is the simplest operator in Airflow: it does nothing. When the executor runs an EmptyOperator task, it immediately succeeds without performing any work. This sounds useless, but it is one of the most valuable organizational tools in DAG design. The EmptyOperator serves as a dependency anchor — a structural node in the DAG graph that groups dependencies, marks pipeline boundaries, and improves readability. SaulėGrid uses EmptyOperator tasks in every DAG that has more than a linear chain of tasks.
Basic Usage

from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator

# Start and end sentinels for the Bronze ingestion DAG
start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')
end = EmptyOperator(task_id='end')

# The actual work tasks
download = PythonOperator(task_id='download_meters', ...)
validate = PythonOperator(task_id='validate_readings', ...)
load = PythonOperator(task_id='load_to_bronze', ...)

# Dependencies — start and end frame the pipeline
start >> download >> validate >> load >> end
Why a Do-Nothing Task Is Useful

The EmptyOperator solves three problems. First, it provides clear visual boundaries in the Graph View. A DAG that starts with download_meters and ends with load_to_bronze is functional but visually ambiguous — where does the pipeline begin and end? Adding start and end sentinels makes the boundaries explicit. Second, it serves as a dependency anchor for fan-in and fan-out patterns. When three parallel tasks must all complete before the next phase begins, an EmptyOperator named validation_complete can serve as the convergence point. Third, it acts as a placeholder during development — you can define the DAG structure with EmptyOperator tasks and replace them with real operators one at a time.
The Checkpoint Pattern

SaulėGrid's Silver transformation DAG uses EmptyOperator as checkpoints between pipeline phases. After Bronze data is read and validated, a checkpoint task named bronze_validated serves as the gateway to the parallel transformation phase. After the three parallel transformations (consumption, generation, battery) complete, a checkpoint named transforms_complete serves as the gateway to the write phase. These checkpoints make the pipeline structure visible in the Graph View and provide natural points for monitoring — if transforms_complete is green, you know all three transformations succeeded without checking each one individually.

The checkpoint pattern is especially valuable during debugging. When SaulėGrid's Silver DAG fails at the battery computation task, Ieva can see in the Grid View that bronze_ready is green (the read phase succeeded) and transforms_done is missing (the parallel phase did not complete). Without the checkpoints, she would need to check each individual task to determine which phase failed. The checkpoints provide phase-level status at a glance, reducing the time from 'something failed' to 'I know which phase failed' from minutes to seconds.

# SaulėGrid's Silver DAG with checkpoint EmptyOperators

with DAG(dag_id='dag_silver_transform', ...) as dag:

    start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')

    read_bronze = PythonOperator(task_id='read_bronze', ...)
    
    # Checkpoint after Bronze read — makes the Graph View clear
    bronze_ready = EmptyOperator(task_id='bronze_ready')

    # Three parallel transformations
    consumption = PythonOperator(task_id='aggregate_consumption', ...)
    generation = PythonOperator(task_id='aggregate_generation', ...)
    battery = PythonOperator(task_id='compute_battery_soc', ...)

    # Checkpoint after parallel phase — all three must complete
    transforms_done = EmptyOperator(task_id='transforms_done')

    write_silver = PythonOperator(task_id='write_silver', ...)
    end = EmptyOperator(task_id='end')

    # Dependencies
    start >> read_bronze >> bronze_ready
    bronze_ready >> [consumption, generation, battery] >> transforms_done
    transforms_done >> write_silver >> end

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's Silver DAG with EmptyOperator checkpoints
blocks.content:
[start]                        (EmptyOperator — entry point)
     │
  [read_bronze]                  (PythonOperator — reads Bronze data)
     │
  [bronze_ready]                 (EmptyOperator — checkpoint)
     │
  ┌──┴───────────┬───────────┐
  ▼              ▼           ▼
[consumption] [generation] [battery]  (3 PythonOperators in parallel)
  │              │           │
  └──────┬───────┴───────────┘
         ▼
  [transforms_done]              (EmptyOperator — checkpoint)
         │
  [write_silver]                 (PythonOperator — writes Silver Parquet)
         │
  [end]                          (EmptyOperator — exit point)
blocks.caption: The EmptyOperator checkpoints (bronze_ready, transforms_done) add no execution time but make the pipeline structure visible. The Graph View shows clear phases: read, transform in parallel, write.

visual-recommendation.description: Color-coded DAG graph showing EmptyOperator checkpoint nodes in a distinct color from PythonOperator work nodes, with the fan-out and fan-in pattern clearly visible.

blocks.title: EmptyOperator is free
blocks.content: An EmptyOperator executes in milliseconds and consumes no meaningful resources. The cost of adding checkpoints to your DAG is essentially zero. The benefit — clearer structure, easier monitoring, better debugging — is significant. SaulėGrid's rule: if a DAG has a fan-out or fan-in pattern, add EmptyOperator checkpoints at the boundaries. If a DAG has distinct phases, add EmptyOperator sentinels between them.
EmptyOperator in Development

During DAG development, the EmptyOperator serves as a placeholder for tasks that have not been implemented yet. When Matas designs a new DAG, he starts by defining the structure with EmptyOperator tasks: start >> download >> validate >> load >> end. He runs the DAG to verify the dependency graph looks correct in the Graph View, then replaces each EmptyOperator with the real operator one at a time. This incremental approach catches structural problems early — before the task logic adds complexity — and is SaulėGrid's standard practice for building new DAGs.

With the three core operators covered — BashOperator for shell commands, PythonOperator for Python functions, and EmptyOperator for structural anchors — the next lesson examines the parameters that all operators share. These are the configuration options inherited from BaseOperator that control task behavior regardless of whether the task runs Python code, executes a Bash script, or serves purely as a structural dependency anchor in the DAG graph.

Every operator inherits parameters from BaseOperator — the parent class that provides common task configuration. These parameters control task identity (task_id), reliability (retries, retry_delay), safety (execution_timeout), execution behavior (trigger_rule, pool), and prioritization (priority_weight). You have seen task_id in every code example so far. This lesson covers the remaining parameters that appear in production DAGs, using SaulėGrid's task configurations as concrete examples.
The Essential Parameters

rows: Unique name within the DAG — appears in UI, logs, database
Number of retry attempts after failure
Kill the task if it runs longer than this
Higher weight = scheduled first when resources are scarce

blocks.content: task_id — The Identity

The task_id is the task's unique identifier within its DAG. It must be unique within the DAG (two tasks in the same DAG cannot share a task_id) but can be reused across different DAGs (dag_bronze_meters and dag_bronze_generation can both have a task called download). The task_id appears in the Graph View as the node label, in log file paths as a directory name, in the metadata database as a primary key column, and in CLI commands when you reference a specific task. SaulėGrid's naming convention (verb_noun: download_meters, validate_readings) makes task_ids readable in all four contexts.
retries and retry_delay — Reliability

The retries parameter sets how many times Airflow should retry a failed task before marking it as permanently failed. The retry_delay parameter sets how long to wait between attempts. SaulėGrid configures different retry behavior for different task types: network-dependent tasks (SFTP downloads, API calls) get 3 retries with 5-minute delays because transient failures are common. Local computation tasks (validation, aggregation) get 1 retry with a 1-minute delay because their failures are usually code bugs rather than transient issues. The retry_exponential_backoff parameter can double the delay between each retry — useful for rate-limited APIs.
execution_timeout — Safety

The execution_timeout parameter kills a task that has been running longer than the specified duration. Without a timeout, a hung task — a stalled SFTP connection, an infinite loop, a deadlocked database query — runs forever, consuming an executor slot and blocking downstream tasks. SaulėGrid sets execution_timeout on every task: 60 minutes for download tasks (network-dependent, occasionally slow), 30 minutes for validation tasks (local processing, should be fast), and 15 minutes for notification tasks (should complete in seconds). The timeout raises an AirflowTaskTimeout exception, which triggers the retry mechanism if retries are configured.

# SaulėGrid's task parameter configuration by task type

# Network-dependent task — generous timeout, multiple retries
download = PythonOperator(
    task_id='download_meters',
    python_callable=download_from_sftp,
    retries=3,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5),
    retry_exponential_backoff=True,    # 5min, 10min, 20min
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=60),
)

# Local computation — short timeout, single retry
validate = PythonOperator(
    task_id='validate_readings',
    python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    retries=1,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=1),
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=30),
)

# Notification — very short timeout, no retries
alert = PythonOperator(
    task_id='send_completion_alert',
    python_callable=send_slack_alert,
    retries=0,
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=5),
)
Layered Configuration
Inheriting from default_args

As you learned in Module 3 lesson 5, task parameters can be inherited from the DAG's default_args. Parameters set directly on the task override the defaults. SaulėGrid's default_args set retries=2, retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5), and execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=30). Most tasks inherit these defaults without change. The download task overrides retries and timeout for its network-specific behavior. The alert task overrides retries to 0 because a failed notification should not block the pipeline. This layered approach — team defaults plus task-specific overrides — keeps configuration consistent without being rigid.

blocks.title: Parameters encode operational knowledge
blocks.content: The retry count, delay, and timeout for each task are not arbitrary numbers — they encode the team's operational experience. SaulėGrid knows that SFTP downloads occasionally time out (retries=3) but usually recover within 5 minutes (retry_delay). They know that validation tasks that run for more than 30 minutes are probably stuck, not just slow. These numbers come from observing task behavior over weeks and months. When you see a task with retries=5 and retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=15), someone has a story about why those numbers are what they are.

Module 3 covered DAG ID naming conventions. This lesson covers task ID naming — the conventions that make tasks identifiable, debuggable, and meaningful when viewed in the Airflow UI, in log files, and in team conversations. A task_id like task_1 tells you nothing. A task_id like download_meters tells you everything. The difference is a naming convention, and establishing one early saves the team from renaming tasks later — which, like renaming DAGs, loses history.
SaulėGrid's Convention — verb_noun

SaulėGrid uses the verb_noun pattern for every task_id: download_meters, validate_readings, load_to_bronze, aggregate_consumption, compute_battery_soc, send_alert. The verb describes the action (download, validate, load, aggregate, compute, send). The noun describes the target (meters, readings, bronze, consumption, battery_soc, alert). This pattern makes every task self-describing: you can understand what a task does from its ID alone, without opening the DAG file or reading the callable's docstring.

# SaulėGrid's task naming convention: verb_noun

# Good — descriptive, follows the convention
download_meters       = PythonOperator(task_id='download_meters', ...)
validate_readings     = PythonOperator(task_id='validate_readings', ...)
load_to_bronze        = PythonOperator(task_id='load_to_bronze', ...)
aggregate_consumption = PythonOperator(task_id='aggregate_consumption', ...)
compute_battery_soc   = PythonOperator(task_id='compute_battery_soc', ...)
send_completion_alert = PythonOperator(task_id='send_completion_alert', ...)

# Bad — ambiguous, inconsistent, or too generic
task_1       = PythonOperator(task_id='task_1', ...)        # meaningless
step_a       = PythonOperator(task_id='step_a', ...)        # meaningless
do_stuff     = PythonOperator(task_id='do_stuff', ...)      # too vague
process      = PythonOperator(task_id='process', ...)       # process what?
meters       = PythonOperator(task_id='meters', ...)        # noun only — do what with meters?
Where Task IDs Appear

The task_id appears in five places, and each place creates a different readability requirement. In the Graph View, the task_id is the node label — it must be short enough to fit without truncation but descriptive enough to distinguish from neighboring tasks. In log file paths (/logs/dag_bronze_meters/download_meters/2026-03-22/), the task_id becomes a directory name. In the metadata database, the task_id is part of the primary key for task instance records. In CLI commands (airflow tasks test dag_bronze_meters download_meters 2026-03-22), the task_id is typed by hand. In team conversations ('the download_meters task failed again'), the task_id must be unambiguous.

rows: Must be short enough to fit, clear enough to distinguish
Must be filesystem-safe (no spaces or special characters)
Must be stable — changing it loses history
Must be typeable without checking the spelling
Must be unambiguous — one name, one task

blocks.content: Uniqueness Within the DAG

Task IDs must be unique within a single DAG but can repeat across DAGs. SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters and dag_bronze_generation both have a task called download — this is fine because the full identifier is always (dag_id, task_id), and the combination is unique. However, within a single DAG, duplicate task IDs cause unpredictable behavior: one task silently overwrites the other, and the DAG runs with fewer tasks than expected. Airflow logs a warning but does not raise an error, making this a subtle bug. SaulėGrid's code review checklist includes a manual check for duplicate task IDs within each DAG file.

blocks.title: Task IDs are permanent in practice
blocks.content: Changing a task_id after a DAG has run in production is not a rename — it is a deletion and creation. The old task_id's history (task instances, logs, metrics) stays in the database under the old name. The new task_id starts with no history. SaulėGrid changed validate_data to validate_readings after one week in production and lost the first week's performance metrics. The lesson: choose task_ids carefully during DAG design, not after deployment.
Code and UI Alignment
Variable Names vs Task IDs

In Python, the variable name and the task_id do not have to match, but SaulėGrid requires them to be identical. This convention prevents confusion: when you see validate = PythonOperator(task_id='validate_readings'), the mismatch between the variable name (validate) and the task_id (validate_readings) means the Graph View shows one name and the code uses another. SaulėGrid's convention is validate_readings = PythonOperator(task_id='validate_readings') — the variable and the task_id are the same string, so the code and the UI always agree.

blocks.title: Match your variable to your task_id
blocks.content: When the Python variable name matches the task_id, you can search the codebase for the task_id and find exactly one definition. When they differ, searching for the task_id returns nothing (because the variable has a different name), and searching for the variable does not help someone who found the task_id in the UI. One name, used everywhere, eliminates this class of confusion entirely.

Lesson 6 introduced retries and timeouts as task parameters. This lesson explores them in depth: how the retry mechanism works step by step, how exponential backoff prevents overwhelming a recovering service, how timeouts protect against hung tasks, and how SaulėGrid configures these parameters based on their operational experience. Retries and timeouts are the two most important reliability mechanisms in Airflow — they transform fragile scripts into resilient pipeline steps.
The Retry Lifecycle

When a task fails (raises an exception or returns a non-zero exit code), Airflow checks the retry configuration. If retries is greater than zero and the current attempt number is less than the maximum, the task instance state changes from 'failed' to 'up_for_retry.' The scheduler notes the retry_delay and schedules the next attempt after the delay expires. When the delay passes, the scheduler queues the task for the executor again. The executor runs the task from scratch — it does not resume from where the previous attempt failed. Each retry is a complete, independent execution of the task.

blocks.title: The retry lifecycle for SaulėGrid's SFTP download task
blocks.content:
retries=3, retry_delay=5min, retry_exponential_backoff=True

Attempt 1 (00:01):
  download_meters → ConnectionError (SFTP server busy)
  State: up_for_retry
  Next attempt in: 5 minutes

Attempt 2 (00:06):
  download_meters → ConnectionError (SFTP still busy)
  State: up_for_retry
  Next attempt in: 10 minutes (5 × 2¹)

Attempt 3 (00:16):
  download_meters → ConnectionError (SFTP recovering)
  State: up_for_retry
  Next attempt in: 20 minutes (5 × 2²)

Attempt 4 (00:36):
  download_meters → Success!
  State: success
  Downstream tasks become eligible
blocks.caption: With exponential backoff, the delay doubles between each retry: 5, 10, 20 minutes. This gives the failing service progressively more time to recover while keeping total retry duration bounded.

blocks.content: Exponential Backoff

The retry_exponential_backoff parameter changes the retry behavior from fixed delays to exponentially increasing delays. Without it, every retry waits the same duration (retry_delay). With it, the delay doubles each time: 5 minutes, 10 minutes, 20 minutes, 40 minutes. SaulėGrid enables exponential backoff on network-dependent tasks because most transient failures recover within minutes, but some take longer. Fixed 5-minute retries would waste three attempts in 15 minutes. Exponential backoff spreads the attempts across 35 minutes, giving the remote service time to recover while still retrying aggressively at first.
When Retries Are Exhausted

If the task fails on every retry attempt, the final state is 'failed.' The DAG Run state also becomes 'failed' because not all tasks can complete. All downstream tasks remain in 'no_status' — they were never scheduled because their upstream dependency never succeeded. Airflow sends a failure notification if email_on_failure is configured. At this point, recovery requires human intervention: check the log, fix the root cause (SFTP server is down, API credentials expired, file is missing), and clear the failed task in the UI to trigger a fresh execution.
Timeouts — The Safety Net

The execution_timeout parameter kills a task that has been running longer than the specified duration. The timeout raises an AirflowTaskTimeout exception, which the executor catches and records as a failure. If the task has retries remaining, the retry mechanism kicks in. Without a timeout, a hung task — a stalled network connection, an infinite loop, a database query waiting on a lock — runs indefinitely, consuming an executor slot and blocking the entire pipeline. SaulėGrid experienced this when their weather API endpoint hung for 3 hours during a provider outage. Without a timeout, the task would have held an executor slot for the entire outage.

# SaulėGrid's timeout strategy by task type

# Network tasks: generous timeout (slow is possible, hung is not)
download_meters = PythonOperator(
    task_id='download_meters',
    python_callable=download_from_sftp,
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=60),  # SFTP can be slow
    retries=3,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5),
    retry_exponential_backoff=True,
)

# Compute tasks: tight timeout (should be fast)
validate_readings = PythonOperator(
    task_id='validate_readings',
    python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=15),  # 400K rows takes ~5min
    retries=1,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=2),
)

# API calls: moderate timeout with aggressive retries
fetch_weather = PythonOperator(
    task_id='fetch_weather',
    python_callable=call_weather_api,
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=10),
    retries=5,  # weather API is flaky
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=3),
    retry_exponential_backoff=True,
)
Configuring by Task Type

SaulėGrid groups their tasks into three categories with different retry and timeout profiles. Network-dependent tasks (SFTP downloads, API calls) get generous timeouts and multiple retries with exponential backoff, because external services have unpredictable latency and transient failures. Compute-bound tasks (validation, aggregation) get tight timeouts and few retries, because if they are slow it usually means something is wrong with the data, not the network. Notification tasks (emails, Slack messages) get short timeouts and no retries, because a failed notification should not block the data pipeline.

blocks.title: Timeouts are not optional
blocks.content: Every production task should have an execution_timeout. The default (no timeout) means a hung task runs forever. SaulėGrid sets timeouts on every task, even tasks that 'should never hang.' A validation function that processes 400,000 rows in 5 minutes could hang on a malformed row with a recursive parsing bug. A 15-minute timeout catches this in 15 minutes. No timeout catches it when someone notices the morning dashboard is stale.

blocks.title: Retries are not error handling
blocks.content: Retries handle transient failures — problems that go away on their own (network blips, temporary service outages, rate limits). They do not fix permanent failures: a bug in your code will fail on every retry, a deleted file will not reappear, and expired credentials will not renew themselves. If a task fails consistently on every retry, the problem is permanent and requires a code or configuration fix, not more retries.

The previous lessons introduced each operator individually. Real DAGs combine multiple operators into patterns — recurring combinations that solve common pipeline design problems. SaulėGrid's DAGs use four patterns repeatedly: the download-validate-load chain, the parallel fan-out with checkpoints, the conditional notification, and the heartbeat check. Recognizing these patterns helps you read existing DAGs faster and design new ones with proven structures.
Pattern 1 — Download-Validate-Load

The most common pattern in SaulėGrid's Bronze layer is a three-task chain: a BashOperator or PythonOperator downloads data from an external source, a PythonOperator validates the downloaded data, and a PythonOperator loads the validated data to the Bronze layer. The download task has aggressive retries (external source, transient failures). The validation task has tight timeouts (local computation, should be fast). The load task has moderate configuration. This pattern appears in every Bronze ingestion DAG and follows the single-responsibility principle: each task does exactly one thing.

# Pattern 1: Download-Validate-Load (SaulėGrid's Bronze standard)

download_meters = BashOperator(
    task_id='download_meters',
    bash_command='/scripts/download_meters.sh ',
    retries=3,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5),
    retry_exponential_backoff=True,
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=60),
)

validate_readings = PythonOperator(
    task_id='validate_readings',
    python_callable=validate_meter_data,
    retries=1,
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=15),
)

load_to_bronze = PythonOperator(
    task_id='load_to_bronze',
    python_callable=write_to_bronze_layer,
    retries=1,
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=15),
)

download_meters >> validate_readings >> load_to_bronze
Pattern 2 — Parallel Fan-Out with Checkpoints

SaulėGrid's Silver DAG reads validated Bronze data, then fans out into three parallel transformations: consumption aggregation, generation aggregation, and battery state computation. EmptyOperator checkpoints bracket the parallel phase. This pattern — checkpoint, fan-out, parallel work, fan-in, checkpoint — appears whenever multiple independent transformations apply to the same input data. The parallel tasks run simultaneously (on the LocalExecutor, as separate processes), reducing total pipeline duration compared to running them sequentially.
Pattern 3 — Conditional Notification

SaulėGrid adds a notification task at the end of critical DAGs. The notification is a PythonOperator that sends a Slack message confirming the pipeline completed successfully. The key design decision is the trigger_rule: the notification task uses trigger_rule='all_done' instead of the default 'all_success.' This means the notification runs regardless of whether the upstream tasks succeeded or failed — Ieva gets a message either way. If the upstream tasks succeeded, the message says 'Bronze meters ingestion complete.' If any task failed, the message says 'Bronze meters ingestion FAILED' with the failed task name. You will learn trigger rules in detail in Module 6.

# Pattern 3: Conditional notification that runs on success OR failure

notify = PythonOperator(
    task_id='send_notification',
    python_callable=send_pipeline_status,
    trigger_rule='all_done',  # runs even if upstream tasks failed
    retries=0,                # notification failure should not block anything
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=5),
)

# The notification runs after load_to_bronze, regardless of its state
load_to_bronze >> notify
Pattern 4 — Heartbeat Check

Before downloading data from an external source, SaulėGrid checks whether the source is available. A PythonOperator heartbeat task makes a lightweight request to the SFTP server or API endpoint and fails fast if the service is unreachable. This avoids waiting for the full download timeout when the service is completely down. The heartbeat has a short timeout (2 minutes) and no retries — if the service is unreachable, there is no point retrying immediately. The download task (downstream) has its own retry mechanism for transient failures.

# Pattern 4: Heartbeat check before expensive operation

def check_sftp_available():
    """Verify SFTP server is reachable before starting download."""
    import socket
    try:
        sock = socket.create_connection(('sftp.saulegrid.lt', 22), timeout=30)
        sock.close()
        print("SFTP server is reachable")
    except (socket.timeout, ConnectionRefusedError) as e:
        raise ConnectionError(f"SFTP server unreachable: {e}")


check_sftp = PythonOperator(
    task_id='check_sftp_available',
    python_callable=check_sftp_available,
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=2),
    retries=0,  # fail fast — no point retrying a heartbeat
)

check_sftp >> download_meters >> validate_readings >> load_to_bronze

blocks.title: Patterns are building blocks
blocks.content: These four patterns compose. SaulėGrid's complete Bronze DAG combines all four: a heartbeat check, then the download-validate-load chain, then a conditional notification. The Silver DAG combines the fan-out pattern with the notification pattern. Recognizing the patterns in an existing DAG is faster than reading each task individually — you see the shape of the pipeline before examining the details.

The next lesson brings every concept from this module together by presenting SaulėGrid's complete dag_bronze_meters with all tasks configured, all naming conventions applied, and all four patterns composed into a single, production-realistic pipeline. Seeing the full picture shows how the individual building blocks from the last nine lessons combine into a coherent orchestration design that Matas and Ieva rely on every day.

This module taught tasks from the ground up: what they are, how they relate to operators, the three core operator types, task parameters, naming conventions, retries and timeouts, and common patterns. This final lesson brings everything together by presenting SaulėGrid's complete dag_bronze_meters with every concept applied. The DAG is not a simplified teaching example — it is production-realistic, with all the configuration that SaulėGrid's team uses in their daily operations.
The Complete Bronze Meters DAG

# dag_bronze_meters.py — SaulėGrid's production Bronze ingestion DAG
# Complete version with all task design patterns from Module 4

from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from airflow.operators.bash import BashOperator
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from helpers.defaults import SAULEGRID_DEFAULT_ARGS


def check_sftp_available():
    """Heartbeat check — verify SFTP server is reachable."""
    import socket
    sock = socket.create_connection(('sftp.saulegrid.lt', 22), timeout=30)
    sock.close()
    print("SFTP server is reachable")


def validate_meter_data(**kwargs):
    """Validate downloaded meter readings for quality issues."""
    logical_date = kwargs['logical_date']
    date_str = logical_date.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
    file_path = f'/data/bronze/meters/{date_str}.csv'
    print(f"Validating {file_path}...")
    # Production: read file, check nulls, negatives, row count
    print("Validation complete: 399,970 valid rows")


def write_to_bronze(**kwargs):
    """Write validated readings to Bronze layer as Parquet."""
    logical_date = kwargs['logical_date']
    date_str = logical_date.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
    print(f"Writing to bronze/meters/{date_str}/")
    print("Write complete")


def send_pipeline_status(**kwargs):
    """Send Slack notification with pipeline result."""
    dag_run = kwargs['dag_run']
    status = 'SUCCESS' if dag_run.get_state() == 'success' else 'FAILED'
    print(f"Sending Slack: Bronze meters ingestion {status}")


with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',
    description='Ingest daily meter readings from SFTP to Bronze layer',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    schedule='@daily',
    catchup=False,
    max_active_runs=1,
    tags=['bronze', 'meters', 'saulegrid'],
    default_args=SAULEGRID_DEFAULT_ARGS,
    doc_md="""## Bronze Meter Ingestion
    Downloads daily meter readings from SaulėGrid's SFTP server.
    **Owner:** Matas | **Source:** sftp.saulegrid.lt
    """,
) as dag:

    start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')

    check_sftp = PythonOperator(
        task_id='check_sftp_available',
        python_callable=check_sftp_available,
        execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=2),
        retries=0,
    )

    download_meters = BashOperator(
        task_id='download_meters',
        bash_command='/scripts/download_meters.sh ',
        retries=3,
        retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5),
        retry_exponential_backoff=True,
        execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=60),
    )

    validate_readings = PythonOperator(
        task_id='validate_readings',
        python_callable=validate_meter_data,
        execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=15),
    )

    load_to_bronze = PythonOperator(
        task_id='load_to_bronze',
        python_callable=write_to_bronze,
        execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=15),
    )

    notify = PythonOperator(
        task_id='send_notification',
        python_callable=send_pipeline_status,
        trigger_rule='all_done',
        retries=0,
        execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=5),
    )

    end = EmptyOperator(task_id='end')

    start >> check_sftp >> download_meters >> validate_readings
    validate_readings >> load_to_bronze >> notify >> end
Connecting the Module
Tracing Every Concept

Every concept from this module appears in this DAG. The EmptyOperator provides start and end sentinels (lesson 5). The BashOperator wraps the existing SFTP download script (lesson 3). The PythonOperator handles validation, loading, and notification (lesson 4). The verb_noun naming convention makes every task self-describing (lesson 7). Retries and timeouts are configured per task type: aggressive for network tasks, moderate for compute tasks, minimal for notifications (lesson 8). The heartbeat check and conditional notification patterns frame the pipeline (lesson 9). Default_args from the shared module provide team-wide defaults (Module 3, lesson 5).
The Graph View

blocks.title: SaulėGrid's complete dag_bronze_meters in the Graph View
blocks.content:
[start] → [check_sftp] → [download_meters] → [validate_readings]
                                                       │
                                              [load_to_bronze]
                                                       │
                                              [send_notification]
                                                       │
                                                    [end]

Task count: 7
Operator types: 2 EmptyOperator, 1 BashOperator, 4 PythonOperator
Patterns: heartbeat → download-validate-load → notification
blocks.caption: Seven tasks using three operator types and three patterns. The Graph View shows a clean linear chain with clear task names. Any engineer on the team can understand the pipeline at a glance.

visual-recommendation.description: Annotated screenshot of the Airflow Graph View showing SaulėGrid's dag_bronze_meters with color-coded operator types, task names visible on each node, and dependency edges labeled.

blocks.content: Why These Choices
Design Decisions

rows: Fails fast (2 min) instead of waiting for download timeout (60 min)
Script predates Airflow and works; no need to rewrite
Complex quality checks benefit from Python's data handling
Team wants to know about failures, not just successes
Clean graph boundaries, consistent across all DAGs
Prevents parallel SFTP downloads from conflicting

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 5, you will learn the TaskFlow API — a modern, Python-native way to define tasks using decorated functions that pass data through return values instead of XCom calls. TaskFlow does not replace what you learned here; it provides an alternative syntax for PythonOperator tasks that makes data flow between tasks explicit and code more readable. The operator concepts, naming conventions, and retry strategies from this module apply equally to TaskFlow tasks.
The Complete Picture

SaulėGrid's seven-task Bronze DAG is a small but complete orchestration pipeline. Every task has a clear purpose, a descriptive name, and appropriate reliability configuration. The three operator types cover every task type in the pipeline. The patterns — heartbeat, download-validate-load, conditional notification — compose into a structure that is readable in the Graph View and debuggable in the logs. This is the standard that all of SaulėGrid's DAGs follow.

chapters.title: The TaskFlow API
chapters.description: Understand the TaskFlow API as the modern Python-native approach to Airflow, learn the @task decorator pattern, master automatic data passing through return values, and build the judgment for choosing between TaskFlow and classic operators.

blocks.content: Module 4 taught you to build tasks using the classic operator pattern: create a PythonOperator, pass it a callable, and wire dependencies with >>. This pattern works, but it has a friction point that becomes obvious when tasks need to share data. If the download task produces a file path and the validate task needs to consume it, the classic pattern requires you to manually push the value into Airflow's XCom storage in one task and pull it out in another. The code for pushing and pulling obscures the actual data flow — you see XCom calls everywhere, but the logical connection between producer and consumer is buried in boilerplate. The TaskFlow API exists to solve this problem.
The Boilerplate Tax
The XCom Problem

XCom — short for cross-communication — is Airflow's mechanism for passing small pieces of data between tasks. In the classic pattern, a PythonOperator callable pushes a value to XCom using the task instance's xcom_push method, and a downstream task pulls it using xcom_pull. The data flows through the metadata database: the producer writes to the xcom table, the consumer reads from it. This works, but the code is verbose. To pass a single file path between two tasks, you write four lines of XCom boilerplate in addition to the actual logic. With five tasks passing data in a chain, the XCom calls outnumber the business logic.

# Classic pattern — XCom push and pull are manual and verbose

def download_from_sftp(**kwargs):
    """Download meter file and push the file path to XCom."""
    file_path = '/data/bronze/meters/2026-03-22.csv'
    print(f"Downloaded to {file_path}")
    # Manual XCom push — boilerplate
    kwargs['ti'].xcom_push(key='meter_file', value=file_path)


def validate_meter_data(**kwargs):
    """Pull the file path from XCom, then validate."""
    # Manual XCom pull — boilerplate
    file_path = kwargs['ti'].xcom_pull(
        task_ids='download_meters', key='meter_file'
    )
    print(f"Validating {file_path}...")
    valid_count = 399970
    # Push the result for the next task
    kwargs['ti'].xcom_push(key='valid_count', value=valid_count)


# The data flow is hidden inside the function bodies
# Reading the DAG definition alone does not reveal what data moves where
download = PythonOperator(task_id='download_meters', python_callable=download_from_sftp)
validate = PythonOperator(task_id='validate_readings', python_callable=validate_meter_data)
download >> validate

The manual XCom pattern becomes especially painful when a task consumes data from multiple upstream tasks. SaulėGrid's write_to_bronze function pulls from two different tasks — download_meters for the file path and validate_readings for the row count. Each pull requires the producing task's ID and key as string arguments, which are prone to typos and silently return None if misspelled. In a DAG with ten tasks passing data, the XCom calls form a web of string-based references that is difficult to read and fragile to maintain.
What TaskFlow Changes

The TaskFlow API replaces manual XCom calls with Python's natural data-passing mechanism: function return values and parameters. A TaskFlow task returns a value, and a downstream TaskFlow task receives it as a function parameter. Airflow handles the XCom storage transparently — the return value is automatically pushed, and the parameter is automatically pulled. The data flow is visible in the code: if task_b(data) calls task_a(), you can see that task_b receives data from task_a without opening either function's body.

# TaskFlow pattern — data flows through return values and parameters
from airflow.decorators import task

@task
def download_from_sftp():
    """Download meter file and return the path."""
    file_path = '/data/bronze/meters/2026-03-22.csv'
    print(f"Downloaded to {file_path}")
    return file_path  # automatic XCom push


@task
def validate_meter_data(file_path: str):
    """Receive the file path directly as a parameter."""
    print(f"Validating {file_path}...")
    return 399970  # automatic XCom push


# The data flow is visible in the DAG definition
path = download_from_sftp()       # returns the file path
count = validate_meter_data(path)  # receives it as a parameter
Reading the Data Flow
Visible Data Flow

Compare the two patterns. In the classic version, the DAG definition shows download >> validate — a dependency with no indication of what data passes between them. You must read both function bodies to discover that a file path moves from download to validate via XCom. In the TaskFlow version, the DAG definition shows count = validate_meter_data(download_from_sftp()) — the data flow is explicit in the calling structure. The function signatures tell you what data each task produces and consumes. For SaulėGrid's team, this visibility reduced the time to understand a new DAG from minutes of tracing XCom calls to seconds of reading the function chain.

blocks.title: TaskFlow is syntactic sugar over XCom
