This module taught the Airflow UI view by view, from the DAG list through the Grid, Graph, Gantt, Calendar, and log views, culminating in a systematic troubleshooting workflow. This final lesson shows how SaulėGrid integrates these tools into their daily operations: the morning health check, the incident response procedure, the weekly performance review, and the monthly reliability assessment. The UI is not just a debugging tool — it is the operational interface that makes SaulėGrid's pipeline team effective.
The Morning Health Check

Every morning at 08:00, the on-call engineer (alternating between Matas and Ieva) opens the Airflow UI and performs a 2-minute health check. Step one: scan the DAG list for red indicators — if all indicators are green, the check is complete. Step two (if any red indicators): click into each failing DAG's Grid View to identify the failed task and date. Step three: read the task log to determine whether the failure is transient (retry will fix it) or permanent (code fix needed). Step four: either clear the failed task for retry or create a ticket for investigation. This routine catches overnight failures before the billing team's 09:00 dashboard review.
The Incident Response

When a failure is detected — either through the morning check or through an email alert triggered by the on_failure_callback — the team follows the five-step troubleshooting workflow from lesson 9. The response prioritizes business impact: Gold DAG failures (which affect the billing team) are investigated immediately, Silver failures (which will cascade to Gold tomorrow) are investigated within the hour, and Bronze failures (which will cascade to Silver tonight) are investigated before end of day. This priority framework ensures that the most business-critical issues get attention first.
The Weekly Performance Review

Every Friday, the team reviews pipeline performance using three UI views. First, the Calendar View for each DAG to count success and failure days — are we meeting the 95% and 98% reliability targets? Second, the Gantt View for the Silver and Gold DAGs to check whether task durations are trending upward — a sign that data volume growth is outpacing the pipeline's capacity. Third, the Grid View filtered to failed runs to review whether the same tasks fail repeatedly — a sign of a systemic issue that needs a permanent fix rather than repeated manual recovery.
The Monthly Reliability Assessment

On the first Monday of each month, the team produces an operations report. The report includes the daily success rate for each DAG (from the Calendar View), the average task durations for critical tasks (from the Gantt View), the total number of manual interventions (from the team's incident log), and a list of recurring issues with their proposed fixes. The report is shared with the engineering manager and the operations team. It serves as both an accountability document and a planning tool — recurring issues identified in the report become the basis for the next month's engineering improvements.
Operational Maturity

SaulėGrid's monitoring practices evolved over their first six months with Airflow. In month one, monitoring was ad hoc — Ieva checked the UI when she remembered. In month two, the morning health check became a daily habit. In month three, the weekly performance review started. By month six, the full operational cadence was established: daily checks, immediate incident response, weekly reviews, and monthly assessments. Each step reduced the mean time between failure and detection, from 8 hours in month one to less than 30 minutes by month six. The UI did not change — the team's discipline in using it did.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: In Module 10, you will bring everything together by building SaulėGrid's complete orchestration pipeline — Bronze, Silver, and Gold DAGs with cross-DAG dependencies, error handling, logging, and the full operational pattern. Module 10 is the synthesis of the entire course: every concept from Modules 1 through 9 appears in the final pipeline design. The UI skills from this module will be your primary tool for verifying that the pipeline works as designed.

The Airflow UI is not just a monitoring tool — it is the operational nerve center for SaulėGrid's data engineering team. The five-step troubleshooting workflow, the daily health check, the weekly performance review, and the monthly reliability assessment form a monitoring practice that transforms pipeline operations from reactive fire-fighting to proactive engineering. The UI provides the visibility. The practice provides the discipline. Together, they keep SaulėGrid's data flowing reliably from SFTP to billing reports.

chapters.title: Building SaulėGrid Pipelines
chapters.description: Synthesize every concept from the course into complete, production-aware SaulėGrid pipelines, covering end-to-end DAG design from requirements to monitoring, cross-DAG dependencies, error handling patterns, and the course's forward bridge to the mid-level track.

blocks.content: The previous nine modules taught Airflow's concepts individually: architecture, DAGs, tasks, TaskFlow, dependencies, scheduling, connections, and the UI. This final module brings everything together by building SaulėGrid's complete orchestration platform — not as isolated examples, but as a coherent system where every concept serves a specific role. This first lesson establishes the design process: how SaulėGrid translates a business requirement into a DAG, moving from a plain-language description of what needs to happen to a fully parameterized, dependency-aware, production-configured Airflow pipeline.
The Design Process

SaulėGrid follows a five-step process for every new DAG. Step one: define the requirement in business terms — what data moves where, how often, and who consumes it. Step two: identify the data flow — which sources provide input, which transformations produce output, and where the output is stored. Step three: decompose the data flow into tasks — one task per distinct operation, following the single-responsibility principle from Module 4. Step four: define dependencies — which tasks depend on which, using the patterns from Module 6. Step five: configure operational parameters — schedule, retries, timeouts, connections, and monitoring, using the concepts from Modules 7 and 8.
A Worked Example — The Bronze Meters DAG

Consider SaulėGrid's requirement: every morning, download the previous day's meter readings from the SFTP server, validate them for quality issues, and store the validated readings in the Bronze layer as Parquet files. The billing team needs the data by 09:00 Lithuanian time. The SFTP server is occasionally slow and sometimes unreachable during maintenance windows. The data engineering team (Matas and Ieva) should be notified of any failures within 30 minutes. This single paragraph contains every input the design process needs.
Step 1 — Data Flow

blocks.title: Translating the requirement into a data flow
blocks.content:
Requirement: Download meters from SFTP → Validate → Store in Bronze

Data Flow:
┌──────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌───────────┐     ┌───────────┐
│  SFTP    │────▶│  Download  │────▶│  Validate │────▶│  Write    │
│  Server  │     │  CSV file  │     │  Quality  │     │  Parquet  │
└──────────┘     └────────────┘     └───────────┘     └───────────┘
                  Source: external    Transform: local  Sink: Bronze
                  Conn: sftp_meters  Logic: Python     Path: /data/bronze/
blocks.caption: The data flow identifies three operations (download, validate, write), one external system (SFTP), and one storage destination (Bronze). Each operation becomes a task. The external system becomes a Connection.

visual-recommendation.description: Data flow diagram showing the transformation from a business requirement paragraph into a three-stage pipeline with labeled tasks, connections, and storage destinations.

blocks.content: Step 2 — Task Decomposition

The data flow has three core operations, but the design adds two more tasks based on operational requirements. A heartbeat check before the download detects SFTP unavailability within 2 minutes instead of waiting for the 60-minute download timeout. A notification task after the pipeline sends a Slack message regardless of success or failure. The final task list is five operational tasks plus two EmptyOperator sentinels: start, check_sftp, download_meters, validate_readings, load_to_bronze, send_notification, and end. Each task maps to a specific module's concepts.
Step 3 — Configuration

rows: Grid View morning check, Calendar weekly

blocks.title: Every decision traces to a module
blocks.content: The design process is not inventing new concepts — it is composing the concepts you already know. The schedule comes from Module 7. The dependency pattern comes from Module 6. The operator choice comes from Module 4. The retry strategy comes from Module 4's lesson on retries. The Connection comes from Module 8. The monitoring plan comes from Module 9. Building a production DAG is an exercise in assembly, not invention.
From Design to Implementation

The next three lessons present SaulėGrid's three core DAGs — Bronze, Silver, and Gold — as complete, production-configured implementations. Each DAG is the result of this same five-step design process, and each traces every configuration decision back to the module where you learned the underlying concept. Together, the three DAGs form a complete orchestration platform that handles SaulėGrid's daily data flow from SFTP ingestion to billing report delivery.

The Bronze ingestion DAG is SaulėGrid's simplest production pipeline and the one you have seen evolve throughout the course — from a three-task sketch in Module 3 to a seven-task production DAG in Module 4, and now to its complete, annotated form with every configuration decision documented. This lesson presents the final version with all parameters, connections, variables, and operational settings applied. Every line traces back to a concept from the previous nine modules.
The Complete DAG
The Production Code

# dag_bronze_meters.py — SaulėGrid's production Bronze ingestion
# Complete version synthesizing Modules 1-9

import pendulum
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.decorators import task
from airflow.operators.bash import BashOperator
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator
from datetime import timedelta
from helpers.defaults import SAULEGRID_DEFAULT_ARGS


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=2), retries=0)
def check_sftp_available():                          # Module 4: heartbeat pattern
    """Verify SFTP server is reachable before starting download."""
    import socket
    sock = socket.create_connection(('sftp.saulegrid.lt', 22), timeout=30)
    sock.close()


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=15))
def validate_readings(**kwargs) -> int:               # Module 5: TaskFlow
    """Validate downloaded meter readings for quality issues."""
    from airflow.models import Variable               # Module 8: Variables
    ds = kwargs['ds']                                  # Module 7: logical dates
    threshold = float(Variable.get('quality_threshold_kw'))
    min_rows = int(Variable.get('min_row_count'))
    file_path = f'/data/bronze/meters/{ds}.csv'
    # Production: read file, check nulls, negatives, row count
    valid_count = 399970
    if valid_count < min_rows:
        raise ValueError(f"Only {valid_count} rows — below {min_rows} threshold")
    return valid_count


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=15))
def load_to_bronze(valid_count: int, **kwargs):       # Module 5: data passing
    """Write validated readings to Bronze layer as Parquet."""
    ds = kwargs['ds']
    print(f"Writing {valid_count} rows to bronze/meters/{ds}/")


@task(trigger_rule='all_done', retries=0,             # Module 6: trigger rules
      execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=5))
def send_notification(**kwargs):
    """Send pipeline status via Slack — runs on success or failure."""
    dag_run = kwargs['dag_run']
    status = dag_run.state
    print(f"Bronze meters ingestion: {status}")


with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',                       # Module 3: naming
    description='Ingest daily meter readings from SFTP to Bronze layer',
    start_date=pendulum.datetime(2026, 1, 1, tz='Europe/Vilnius'),  # Module 7: TZ
    schedule='0 0 * * *',                             # Module 7: cron
    catchup=False,                                    # Module 7: catchup
    max_active_runs=1,                                # Module 3: concurrency
    tags=['bronze', 'meters', 'saulegrid'],           # Module 3: tags
    default_args=SAULEGRID_DEFAULT_ARGS,              # Module 3: defaults
    doc_md='## Bronze Meter Ingestion\nDownloads daily meters from SFTP.',
) as dag:

    start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')             # Module 4: EmptyOperator
    end = EmptyOperator(task_id='end')

    sftp_check = check_sftp_available()

    download = BashOperator(                           # Module 4: BashOperator
        task_id='download_meters',
        bash_command='/scripts/download_meters.sh ',
        retries=3, retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5),  # Module 4: retries
        retry_exponential_backoff=True,
        execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=60),
    )

    validated = validate_readings()
    load_to_bronze(validated)
    notify = send_notification()

    start >> sftp_check >> download >> validated       # Module 6: dependencies
    load_to_bronze(validated) >> notify >> end
How the Concepts Compose
Tracing the Concepts

Every line in this DAG traces to a specific module. The pendulum import and timezone-aware start_date come from Module 7's timezone lesson. The @task decorators come from Module 5's TaskFlow API. The BashOperator wrapping the SFTP script comes from Module 4. The Variable.get() calls inside the task callable (not at module level) follow Module 8's performance warning. The trigger_rule='all_done' on the notification task comes from Module 6. The {{ ds }} template variable (accessed as kwargs['ds']) comes from Module 7's logical date lesson. The EmptyOperator sentinels come from Module 4's checkpoint pattern. The result is a DAG where no configuration is accidental — every setting exists because a module taught you why it matters.
Verifying the Structure
The Graph View

In the Airflow UI's Graph View (Module 9), this DAG appears as a clean linear chain: start → check_sftp → download → validate → load → notify → end. Seven nodes, six edges, no branches. The Graph View confirms that the dependency structure matches the design. The Grid View shows a column per day with the familiar color pattern: mostly green columns, with occasional yellow cells on the download task (retries succeeding) and rare red columns (retries exhausted, requiring manual recovery via the Clear action from Module 9, lesson 5).
Runtime Characteristics
Operational Behavior

At midnight Lithuanian time, the scheduler creates a DAG Run. The heartbeat check verifies SFTP availability in under 30 seconds. The BashOperator downloads the meter file — typically 8-12 minutes, occasionally up to 45 minutes with retries. The TaskFlow validation function checks for null meter IDs, negative daily_kw values, and row count thresholds — 3-5 minutes. The load task writes validated data to Bronze storage — 2-3 minutes. The notification task sends the pipeline status to Slack. The total typical duration is 15-20 minutes, well within the two-hour buffer before the Silver DAG starts at 02:00.

blocks.title: Production readiness checklist
blocks.content: This DAG passes every production readiness check from the course: timezone-aware schedule (Module 7), no credentials in code (Module 8), descriptive task IDs following verb_noun convention (Module 4, lesson 7), execution timeouts on every task (Module 4, lesson 8), retries configured by task type (Module 4, lesson 8), all_done notification (Module 6), doc_md documentation (Module 3, lesson 9), and tags for UI filtering (Module 3, lesson 9). A DAG that passes this checklist is ready for production.

visual-recommendation.description: Annotated screenshot of the Airflow Graph View showing the complete dag_bronze_meters with seven nodes, labeled with the module reference for each task type.

blocks.content: The Silver DAG is SaulėGrid's most structurally complex pipeline — it reads validated Bronze data, separates consumption and generation flows, runs three parallel aggregations, and writes the combined results to Silver storage. The diamond dependency pattern (Module 6), the multiple_outputs TaskFlow pattern (Module 5), and the EmptyOperator checkpoint pattern (Module 4) all converge in this single DAG. If the Bronze DAG demonstrates linear chain mastery, the Silver DAG demonstrates pattern composition.
The Complete DAG
The Production Code

# dag_silver_transform.py — SaulėGrid's Silver transformation pipeline
# Demonstrates: diamond pattern, multiple_outputs, TaskFlow data flow

import pendulum
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.decorators import task
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator
from datetime import timedelta
from helpers.defaults import SAULEGRID_DEFAULT_ARGS


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=20))
def read_bronze(**kwargs) -> str:
    ds = kwargs['ds']
    path = f'/data/bronze/meters/{ds}/'
    print(f"Reading Bronze data from {path}")
    return path


@task(multiple_outputs=True, execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=30))
def separate_flows(bronze_path: str) -> dict:
    """Separate meter data into consumption, generation, and prosumer flows."""
    return {
        'consumption_path': f'{bronze_path}consumption/',
        'generation_path': f'{bronze_path}generation/',
        'prosumer_count': 45200,
    }


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=25))
def aggregate_consumption(path: str) -> str:
    print(f"Aggregating consumption from {path}")
    return '/data/silver/consumption/'


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=25))
def aggregate_generation(path: str) -> str:
    print(f"Aggregating generation from {path}")
    return '/data/silver/generation/'


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=20))
def compute_battery_soc(prosumer_count: int) -> str:
    print(f"Computing battery SoC for {prosumer_count} prosumers")
    return '/data/silver/battery/'


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=15))
def write_silver(cons: str, gen: str, batt: str):
    print(f"Writing Silver: {cons}, {gen}, {batt}")


with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_silver_transform',
    start_date=pendulum.datetime(2026, 1, 1, tz='Europe/Vilnius'),
    schedule='0 2 * * *',
    catchup=False, max_active_runs=1,
    tags=['silver', 'transform', 'saulegrid'],
    default_args=SAULEGRID_DEFAULT_ARGS,
    doc_md='## Silver Transformation\nCleans Bronze data, separates flows.',
) as dag:

    start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')
    end = EmptyOperator(task_id='end')

    bronze = read_bronze()
    flows = separate_flows(bronze)

    cons = aggregate_consumption(flows['consumption_path'])
    gen = aggregate_generation(flows['generation_path'])
    batt = compute_battery_soc(flows['prosumer_count'])

    write_silver(cons, gen, batt)

    start >> bronze
    write_silver(cons, gen, batt) >> end
Visible Architecture
The Data Flow

The DAG definition reveals the complete data flow without opening any function body. The read_bronze function returns a path. The separate_flows function receives that path and returns three separate outputs (consumption_path, generation_path, prosumer_count) via multiple_outputs. Each output feeds a different aggregation function. The three aggregation results converge into write_silver. This data flow is the dependency graph — TaskFlow inferred every dependency from the function call chain, and the Graph View shows the diamond pattern automatically. No >> operators were needed between the TaskFlow tasks; the data flow defined the structure.
Execution Timing
The Parallel Phase

The three aggregation tasks run in parallel because they have no dependencies on each other — each depends only on separate_flows, and none depends on the others' output. The Gantt View (Module 9, lesson 7) confirms the parallelism: three overlapping bars during the aggregation phase. With SaulėGrid's LocalExecutor providing sufficient parallelism, the three tasks execute simultaneously, reducing the aggregation phase from 75 minutes (sequential) to 25 minutes (parallel, limited by the slowest task). This is the fan-out optimization from Module 6 in action.
Synchronization Point
The Convergence

The write_silver function accepts three parameters — one from each aggregation. TaskFlow creates a fan-in dependency: write_silver runs only after all three aggregation tasks have completed successfully. If any aggregation fails, write_silver never runs, and the Silver layer is not updated with partial data. This convergence is the data integrity guarantee from Module 6: either all three aggregations make it into Silver, or none do. The Grid View shows this as three green cells followed by a green write cell, or three cells with at least one red followed by a gray write cell.

blocks.title: TaskFlow made the diamond visible
blocks.content: In a classic operator version of this DAG, the diamond pattern would be defined by >> operators and the data would flow through manual XCom calls. In this TaskFlow version, the diamond emerges from the function signatures — you can see the fan-out (three functions consuming different keys from one dictionary) and the fan-in (one function consuming three return values) by reading the DAG definition alone. This visibility is why SaulėGrid chose TaskFlow for their Silver DAG.

visual-recommendation.description: DAG graph showing the Silver pipeline's diamond pattern with color-coded TaskFlow task nodes, labeled data edges (consumption_path, generation_path, prosumer_count), and EmptyOperator sentinels.

blocks.content: The Gold DAG is SaulėGrid's most operationally complex pipeline — it reads Silver data, computes regional summaries, conditionally generates a regulatory report, and produces the billing output that the business team consumes every morning. The branching pattern (Module 6), the provider operator integration (Module 8), and the all_done notification pattern appear together in this single DAG. If the Bronze DAG demonstrates linear mastery and the Silver DAG demonstrates pattern composition, the Gold DAG demonstrates conditional orchestration.
The Complete DAG
The Production Code

# dag_gold_aggregate.py — SaulėGrid's Gold business output pipeline
# Demonstrates: branching, provider operators, conditional execution

import pendulum
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.decorators import task
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator
from airflow.operators.python import BranchPythonOperator
from airflow.providers.postgres.operators.postgres import PostgresOperator
from datetime import timedelta
from helpers.defaults import SAULEGRID_DEFAULT_ARGS


def choose_reporting_path(**kwargs):
    """Branch: include regulatory report on business days only."""
    day = kwargs['logical_date'].weekday()
    return 'generate_regulatory' if day < 5 else 'skip_regulatory'


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=20))
def read_silver(**kwargs) -> str:
    ds = kwargs['ds']
    return f'/data/silver/{ds}/'


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=25))
def compute_national_summary(silver_path: str) -> dict:
    """Compute national consumption and generation totals."""
    return {'total_kw': 2850000, 'meter_count': 399970}


@task(execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=20))
def generate_regulatory(**kwargs):
    """Generate VERT compliance report for Lithuanian regulator."""
    print(f"Generating VERT report for {kwargs['ds']}")


with DAG(
    dag_id='dag_gold_aggregate',
    start_date=pendulum.datetime(2026, 1, 1, tz='Europe/Vilnius'),
    schedule='0 4 * * *',
    catchup=False, max_active_runs=1,
    tags=['gold', 'aggregate', 'billing', 'saulegrid'],
    default_args=SAULEGRID_DEFAULT_ARGS,
    doc_md='## Gold Aggregation\nProduces billing and regulatory outputs.',
) as dag:

    start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')
    end = EmptyOperator(task_id='end')

    silver = read_silver()
    summary = compute_national_summary(silver)

    branch = BranchPythonOperator(
        task_id='check_business_day',
        python_callable=choose_reporting_path,
    )

    regulatory = generate_regulatory()
    skip_reg = EmptyOperator(task_id='skip_regulatory')

    write_billing = PostgresOperator(
        task_id='write_billing',
        postgres_conn_id='postgres_warehouse',
        sql="INSERT INTO billing.daily (date, total_kw) VALUES ('{{ ds }}', 2850000);",
        trigger_rule='none_failed',
    )

    notify = EmptyOperator(task_id='send_notification',
                           trigger_rule='all_done')

    start >> silver >> summary >> branch
    branch >> regulatory >> write_billing
    branch >> skip_reg >> write_billing
    write_billing >> notify >> end
Conditional Execution
The Branching Logic

The BranchPythonOperator (Module 6, lesson 7) checks the day of the week. On Monday through Friday, it returns 'generate_regulatory,' and the VERT compliance report is generated before billing. On Saturday and Sunday, it returns 'skip_regulatory,' and the regulatory task is skipped. The write_billing task uses trigger_rule='none_failed' so it runs regardless of which branch was taken — because 'skipped' is not 'failed.' This pattern ensures that billing always runs while regulatory runs only on business days.
Database Integration
The Provider Operator

The PostgresOperator (Module 8, lesson 7) writes billing data directly to the analytics warehouse using the postgres_warehouse Connection. The SQL statement uses the {{ ds }} template variable (Module 7, lesson 5) to insert the correct date. The operator handles connection management, execution, commit, and error handling internally — no Hook code is needed. This is the provider operator pattern in its purest form: one operator, one Connection reference, one SQL statement, and zero boilerplate.
Rule Composition
The Trigger Rules

This DAG uses three different trigger rules. The default all_success governs most tasks — each runs only after its upstream succeeds. The none_failed rule on write_billing allows it to run after the branch, where one path is skipped. The all_done rule on the notification ensures the team is alerted regardless of outcome. These three rules, introduced in Module 6 lesson 6, work together to create a DAG that handles conditional paths, data integrity, and operational visibility in a single dependency graph.

blocks.title: Three DAGs, three patterns
blocks.content: SaulėGrid's three core DAGs each demonstrate a different architectural pattern. Bronze is a guarded linear chain — simple, reliable, optimized for external system integration. Silver is a diamond with parallel aggregation — optimized for throughput through fan-out parallelism. Gold is a conditional pipeline with branching — optimized for business logic that varies by day. Together, they show that production pipelines are not complex by accident — they are complex because business requirements demand conditional execution, parallel processing, and multi-system integration.

SaulėGrid's three DAGs depend on each other: Silver reads Bronze output, and Gold reads Silver output. So far, these dependencies have been managed through staggered schedules — Bronze at midnight, Silver at 02:00, Gold at 04:00 — with timing buffers that assume the upstream DAG completes before the downstream DAG starts. This approach works for SaulėGrid's current scale, but it has the same fundamental weakness as the cron timing assumptions from Module 1: if Bronze runs late, Silver processes stale data. The ExternalTaskSensor replaces timing assumptions with explicit cross-DAG dependency checks.
The ExternalTaskSensor

An ExternalTaskSensor is a task that waits for a specific task in another DAG to reach a specific state (usually 'success') before proceeding. SaulėGrid adds an ExternalTaskSensor at the start of the Silver DAG that waits for the Bronze DAG's load_to_bronze task to succeed for the same logical date. If Bronze is still running at 02:00 when Silver is scheduled, the sensor keeps the Silver DAG waiting. When Bronze completes, the sensor detects the state change and the Silver DAG proceeds. No timing assumptions, no stale data risk.

from airflow.sensors.external_task import ExternalTaskSensor

# In dag_silver_transform.py — wait for Bronze to complete

wait_for_bronze = ExternalTaskSensor(
    task_id='wait_for_bronze_meters',
    external_dag_id='dag_bronze_meters',       # the DAG to watch
    external_task_id='load_to_bronze',          # the task to check
    allowed_states=['success'],                 # proceed when this state
    execution_delta=timedelta(hours=2),         # adjust for schedule offset
    timeout=7200,                               # wait up to 2 hours
    poke_interval=300,                          # check every 5 minutes
    mode='poke',                                # check periodically
)

# Insert at the start of the Silver DAG's dependency chain
start >> wait_for_bronze >> read_bronze >> ...
The execution_delta Parameter

Because the Bronze and Silver DAGs run on different schedules (midnight and 02:00), their logical dates for the same data are different. The Bronze DAG Run for March 22nd data has a logical_date of March 22nd 00:00. The Silver DAG Run for the same data has a logical_date of March 22nd 02:00 (two hours later). The execution_delta=timedelta(hours=2) parameter tells the sensor to look for the Bronze DAG Run that is 2 hours earlier than the Silver DAG Run's logical date — aligning the two DAGs on the same data interval despite their different schedules.
Sensor Configuration
Timeout and Poke Interval

The timeout parameter sets how long the sensor will wait before failing. SaulėGrid sets timeout=7200 (2 hours) — if Bronze has not completed within 2 hours of Silver's scheduled start, something is seriously wrong and the team should be alerted via the sensor's failure. The poke_interval sets how frequently the sensor checks the external task's state. SaulėGrid uses 300 seconds (5 minutes) to balance responsiveness against database query load. The sensor occupies an executor slot while waiting, so the poke_interval should not be too frequent — every poke queries the metadata database.
The Trade-Off
When Sensors Are Necessary

SaulėGrid uses ExternalTaskSensors in two places: Silver waits for Bronze, and Gold waits for Silver. The sensors replace the two-hour timing buffers with explicit dependency checks. The trade-off is that sensors occupy executor slots while waiting — a sensor that waits 90 minutes for Bronze to complete holds an executor slot for 90 minutes. At SaulėGrid's scale (three core DAGs, one LocalExecutor), this is acceptable. At larger scale with hundreds of DAGs, sensor-heavy architectures can exhaust executor capacity — a topic covered in the mid-level track's discussion of Datasets and event-driven triggers.

blocks.title: Sensors are not free
blocks.content: Each sensor task occupies an executor slot for its entire wait duration. A sensor with a 2-hour timeout and a 5-minute poke interval holds one slot for up to 2 hours, making 24 database queries. SaulėGrid's three sensors (two cross-DAG waits plus one SFTP file check) are manageable. A team with 50 sensors waiting simultaneously could exhaust the executor's parallelism. The mid-level track introduces Datasets — an event-driven alternative that does not hold executor slots.

Cross-DAG dependencies complete the dependency picture that this course has built across ten modules: the >> operator for intra-DAG dependencies (Module 6) and the ExternalTaskSensor for cross-DAG dependencies. Together, they ensure that every task in SaulėGrid's pipeline — from the Bronze SFTP download through Silver transformation to the Gold billing report — runs only when its input data is ready, whether that input comes from an upstream task in the same DAG or from a task in a completely different DAG.

Modules 4 and 6 introduced individual error handling mechanisms: retries for transient failures, timeouts for hung tasks, and trigger rules for conditional execution after failures. This lesson shows how SaulėGrid combines these mechanisms into error handling patterns — coordinated strategies that provide defense in depth across the entire pipeline. Each pattern addresses a different failure category, and the patterns layer on top of each other so that a single failure is caught by the first applicable layer.
Pattern 1 — Retry with Backoff

The first layer of defense is the retry mechanism configured on every task (Module 4, lesson 8). SaulėGrid's retry strategy is task-type-specific: network-dependent tasks (SFTP downloads, API calls) get 3-5 retries with exponential backoff, local computation tasks get 1 retry with a short delay, and notification tasks get 0 retries. The exponential backoff prevents hammering a recovering service while still providing multiple recovery opportunities. This layer handles 80% of SaulėGrid's failures — most are transient network issues that resolve within one or two retries.
Pattern 2 — Dependency Protection

The second layer is the dependency graph itself (Module 6). When a task fails and retries are exhausted, all downstream tasks remain in 'no_status' — they never run. This prevents cascade failures where bad data from a failed upstream task propagates through the pipeline. SaulėGrid's Silver DAG will not process corrupted Bronze data because the Silver DAG's sensor waits for Bronze's load task to succeed. If Bronze fails, Silver waits. If Silver fails, Gold waits. The dependency graph is a containment mechanism that limits the blast radius of any single failure.
Immediate Notification
Pattern 3 — Failure Callbacks

The third layer is the on_failure_callback parameter, which executes a Python function when a task fails permanently (retries exhausted). SaulėGrid configures on_failure_callback on their critical tasks to send immediate Slack alerts with the task name, the DAG Run date, and a link to the task log in the UI. This callback fires as soon as the task fails — not at the end of the DAG Run — which means Matas receives the alert within minutes of the failure, not hours later during the morning check.

# SaulėGrid's error handling layers in the Bronze DAG

def alert_on_failure(context):
    """Send immediate Slack alert when a critical task fails."""
    task_id = context['task_instance'].task_id
    ds = context['ds']
    log_url = context['task_instance'].log_url
    print(f"ALERT: {task_id} failed for {ds}. Log: {log_url}")
    # Production: send to Slack webhook


# Layer 1: Retries with backoff
download = BashOperator(
    task_id='download_meters',
    bash_command='/scripts/download_meters.sh ',
    retries=3,
    retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=5),
    retry_exponential_backoff=True,
    execution_timeout=timedelta(minutes=60),
    on_failure_callback=alert_on_failure,   # Layer 3: immediate alert
)

# Layer 2: Dependency protection — validate only runs if download succeeded
# Layer 4: all_done notification catches any failure at DAG level
notify = PythonOperator(
    task_id='send_notification',
    trigger_rule='all_done',               # Layer 4: DAG-level notification
    python_callable=send_pipeline_status,
)
Pattern 4 — DAG-Level Notification

The fourth layer is the all_done notification task at the end of every DAG (Module 6, lesson 6). This task runs regardless of whether upstream tasks succeeded or failed, providing a reliable end-of-pipeline signal. SaulėGrid's notification task checks the DAG Run state and sends a summary: 'Bronze meters: SUCCESS, 399,970 rows, 15 minutes' or 'Bronze meters: FAILED, download_meters exhausted retries.' This DAG-level notification complements the task-level failure callback: the callback provides immediate alerting on failure, while the notification provides a daily summary of pipeline outcomes.
The Layered Defense

The four layers work together as defense in depth. A transient SFTP timeout is caught by layer 1 (retry succeeds on second attempt — no alert, no downstream impact). A persistent SFTP outage exhausts retries, triggering layer 3 (immediate Slack alert) and layer 2 (downstream tasks do not run). At the end of the DAG Run, layer 4 sends the failure summary. Each layer catches failures that slip through the previous layer, ensuring that no failure goes undetected and no bad data propagates downstream.

blocks.title: Error handling is architecture
blocks.content: Error handling in Airflow is not about try-except blocks in your Python code — it is about configuring the orchestration layer to detect, contain, and communicate failures automatically. The retry mechanism, the dependency graph, the failure callbacks, and the all_done notifications are architectural decisions that protect the entire pipeline. SaulėGrid configured these patterns once during DAG design, and they have handled hundreds of failures over six months without a single case of undetected bad data reaching the billing team.

Module 9 taught you to read task logs in the Airflow UI. This lesson covers the other side: how to write good log messages in your task code so that the logs are useful when someone reads them at 8 AM during a failure investigation. Good logging turns a 30-minute debugging session into a 3-minute diagnosis. SaulėGrid's logging conventions ensure that every task's log tells a complete story — what the task attempted, what it found, and what went wrong.
The Airflow Logger

Airflow provides a Python logger for every task through the standard logging module. In a PythonOperator callable or a TaskFlow function, you can use print() for simple messages (captured as INFO-level log entries) or the logging module for structured, level-aware logging. SaulėGrid uses print() for development and simple tasks, and the logging module for production tasks where distinguishing INFO, WARNING, and ERROR messages matters during debugging. Both approaches write to the same task log that the UI renders.

import logging

logger = logging.getLogger('airflow.task')

@task
def validate_readings(**kwargs):
    ds = kwargs['ds']
    logger.info(f"Starting validation for {ds}")
    logger.info(f"Reading from /data/bronze/meters/{ds}.csv")
    
    total_rows = 400000
    logger.info(f"Total rows: {total_rows}")
    
    null_meters = 23
    negative_kw = 7
    logger.warning(f"Rejected {null_meters} null meter_ids")
    logger.warning(f"Rejected {negative_kw} negative daily_kw")
    
    valid_rows = total_rows - null_meters - negative_kw
    logger.info(f"Valid rows: {valid_rows}")
    
    if valid_rows < 350000:
        logger.error(f"Row count {valid_rows} below threshold 350,000")
        raise ValueError(f"Validation failed: {valid_rows} < 350,000")
    
    logger.info(f"Validation complete: {valid_rows} valid rows")
    return valid_rows
The Convention
SaulėGrid's Logging Convention

SaulėGrid's logging convention follows three rules. First, log the context at the start of every task: the date being processed, the source file path, and the connection being used. This context makes logs self-contained — you can understand what the task was doing without checking the DAG Run metadata. Second, log the key metrics during processing: row counts, rejection counts, duration of external calls. These metrics reveal the root cause when the final result is wrong. Third, never log credentials, hostnames, or Connection objects — log the connection ID instead. These three rules produce logs that are informative, diagnostic, and secure.
Automated Alerting
Alerting Through Callbacks

SaulėGrid's alerting uses two mechanisms. The on_failure_callback on critical tasks sends an immediate Slack message when a task fails permanently (lesson 6). The all_done notification task at the end of each DAG sends a daily summary regardless of outcome. The combination ensures that failures are detected within minutes (callback) and that the team has a daily record of pipeline outcomes (notification). At the junior level, these two mechanisms provide sufficient alerting. The mid-level track adds email integration, PagerDuty, and SLA-based alerting for larger teams with on-call rotations.
The Quality Bar
What Makes a Log Useful

A useful log tells a story that a person who did not write the task can follow at 8 AM during a failure investigation. SaulėGrid's download task log reads: 'Connecting to sftp.saulegrid.lt... Downloaded 400,000 rows to /data/bronze/meters/2026-03-22.csv... Download complete in 8 minutes.' When the same task fails, the log reads: 'Connecting to sftp.saulegrid.lt... Connection timed out after 30 seconds... Retrying in 5 minutes... Retry 2: Connection timed out... Retry 3: Connection timed out... Retries exhausted.' The story is clear: the SFTP server was unreachable for 15 minutes. No code inspection needed.

blocks.title: Log for your future self
blocks.content: The person reading the log at 8 AM might be you, six months from now, having forgotten the task's implementation details. Or it might be a new team member who has never seen the code. The log should answer their three questions without requiring them to open the DAG file: what was the task trying to do? What happened during execution? Why did it fail (or succeed)? If the log answers all three, it is a good log.

SaulėGrid's DAGs are not static files deployed once and forgotten — they evolve. Schedules change, tasks are added, retry strategies are tuned, connections are updated. Managing this evolution requires the same practices used for any production software: version control, change documentation, code review, and deployment procedures. This lesson covers how SaulėGrid treats DAGs as code, using the same Git workflow, pull request process, and documentation standards that their data processing scripts follow.
DAGs as Code

SaulėGrid stores all DAG files in a Git repository alongside their helper modules, default arguments, and constants. Every change to a DAG — a new task, a schedule adjustment, a retry count update — goes through a pull request that is reviewed by the other engineer before merging. The review checks: does the dependency structure match the requirement? Are the trigger rules correct after any branching? Are retries and timeouts configured appropriately for each task type? Does the Graph View in the development environment match the expected structure? This code review process has caught three configuration errors in six months that would have caused production failures.
The Deployment Workflow

SaulėGrid's deployment workflow is straightforward. The engineer writes or modifies a DAG file locally, tests it in the local Airflow environment (airflow standalone with SequentialExecutor), commits the change to a feature branch, opens a pull request, and waits for review. After approval, the change is merged to the main branch, which triggers a CI pipeline that copies the updated DAG file to the production server's dags/ directory. The scheduler discovers the change on its next parse cycle (within 30 seconds). The engineer verifies the deployment by checking the Graph View in the production UI.
Documentation Practices

Every DAG has three levels of documentation. The doc_md parameter (Module 3, lesson 9) provides inline documentation visible in the Airflow UI — the what, the dependencies, and the troubleshooting guide. The Python docstrings on each task callable describe the function's purpose, parameters, and return values. The Git commit messages and pull request descriptions document the why — the business requirement or operational issue that motivated the change. SaulėGrid's convention: the doc_md tells you what the DAG does, the code comments tell you how, and the Git history tells you why each change was made.
Recording Changes
Change Tracking

SaulėGrid includes a changelog comment block at the top of each DAG file that records significant changes: when the retry count was increased, when a new task was added, when the schedule was modified. This in-file changelog is not a replacement for Git history — it is a quick reference for engineers who are reading the DAG file and want to understand recent changes without opening the repository. The changelog follows a simple format: date, author, one-line description. SaulėGrid's convention limits the changelog to the five most recent changes, with older history available in Git.
Pre-Deployment Verification
Testing DAGs

Before deploying a DAG change, SaulėGrid tests three things. First, the DAG file parses without errors: python -c 'import dag_bronze_meters' confirms no syntax errors or import failures. Second, the dependency structure is correct: the engineer checks the Graph View in the local Airflow UI. Third, individual tasks execute correctly: the airflow tasks test command runs a single task for a specific date without creating a DAG Run, which validates the task's logic without affecting the production schedule. These three tests catch the most common deployment failures: parse errors, dependency mistakes, and task logic bugs.

blocks.title: Treat DAGs like production code
blocks.content: DAGs are production code that runs automatically every night. A bug in a DAG file can silently produce wrong billing data, miss a regulatory deadline, or delete production data. SaulėGrid treats DAG files with the same rigor as their PySpark transformation scripts: version control, code review, testing, and documented deployment. The investment in process is small compared to the cost of a production incident caused by an unreviewed change.

This lesson introduces DAG performance concepts at a junior-level awareness — not the deep tuning covered in the mid-level track, but the baseline understanding needed to recognize performance problems and avoid the most common performance mistakes. SaulėGrid's pipelines are modest in scale (six DAGs, 40 daily tasks), but even at this scale, two performance issues have required investigation: slow DAG parsing caused by heavy imports, and excessive executor slot usage caused by long-running sensors.
Parsing Performance

The scheduler parses every DAG file every 30 seconds (Module 3, lesson 7). Heavy imports, Variable.get() calls at module level, and complex Python logic in the DAG file body all execute on every parse. SaulėGrid's most impactful performance fix was moving a pandas import from the module level to inside a task callable — a one-line change that reduced parse time from 1.2 seconds to 0.1 seconds per file. The rule from Module 3 bears repeating: keep DAG files lightweight. Imports of airflow modules, datetime, and standard library are fine. Everything else belongs inside task callables.
Watching for Trends
Task Duration Monitoring

The Gantt View (Module 9, lesson 7) is the primary tool for monitoring task duration. SaulėGrid reviews the Gantt View weekly to check whether any task's duration is trending upward. A download task that took 8 minutes last month but takes 15 minutes this month might indicate growing data volume, network degradation, or SFTP server slowdown. Early detection of duration trends prevents the surprise where a task that 'always completes in time' suddenly exceeds its timeout and fails during a critical business period.
Scheduler Overhead

The scheduler has a finite capacity for parsing DAG files and evaluating task dependencies. At SaulėGrid's scale (six DAG files, 40 tasks), the scheduler is lightly loaded. But teams that grow to 50+ DAG files start encountering scheduler bottlenecks: DAG discovery takes longer, task evaluation cycles slow down, and the gap between 'task is eligible' and 'task is queued' widens from seconds to minutes. SaulėGrid does not need to worry about scheduler performance yet, but knowing the limits prepares you for the mid-level track where scheduler tuning becomes a primary concern.
Common Performance Mistakes

rows: Database query on every parse cycle
Hung tasks hold executor slots forever
Too many sensors with long timeouts
Executor slots consumed by waiting tasks
Fan out independent tasks for parallelism

blocks.content: Right-Sizing Your Concern
Performance at Junior Scale

At SaulėGrid's current scale, performance tuning is not a daily concern. The default Airflow settings — 32 parallelism, 200-second scheduler heartbeat, 30-second min_file_process_interval — handle six DAGs with 40 tasks comfortably. The performance awareness from this lesson is about avoiding mistakes that create problems (heavy imports, missing timeouts) and recognizing symptoms that signal growing pains (increasing task durations, scheduling delays). The mid-level track covers active performance tuning: executor sizing, scheduler optimization, connection pooling, and DAG serialization — topics that matter when the DAG count reaches 50 or more.

blocks.title: Avoid mistakes now, tune later
blocks.content: At junior scale, performance is about avoiding mistakes, not about optimization. A team that keeps DAG files lightweight, sets timeouts on every task, and fans out independent tasks will not need performance tuning until they significantly outgrow their current scale. SaulėGrid's six-DAG installation has never required a single configuration change from the defaults — because they avoided the common mistakes from the start.

Ten modules. One hundred lessons. Forty-two hours. SaulėGrid's journey from unorchestrated cron jobs to a complete Airflow platform is also your journey — from zero Airflow experience to a confident understanding of every concept a junior data engineer needs to build, operate, and maintain production orchestration pipelines. This final lesson traces that journey one last time, connecting every module into a single narrative, and then looks forward to what the mid-level track teaches for engineers ready to go deeper.
The Journey

Module 1 showed you why orchestration exists — the timing assumptions, silent failures, and manual recovery that cron-based pipelines suffer from. Module 2 revealed the architecture that solves these problems — the scheduler, web server, metadata database, and executor working together as a coordinated system. Module 3 taught the DAG as the central abstraction — the container that holds tasks, dependencies, and schedules in a single Python file. Module 4 gave you the building blocks — operators that define what each task does, from BashOperator for shell commands to PythonOperator for Python functions to EmptyOperator for structural checkpoints.

Module 5 introduced the TaskFlow API — the modern Python-native approach where functions pass data through return values and dependencies emerge from the calling structure. Module 6 taught dependency patterns — fan-out for parallelism, fan-in for synchronization, branching for conditional execution, and trigger rules for flexible completion criteria. Module 7 covered the time dimension — cron expressions, data intervals, logical dates, catchup, backfilling, and the timezone handling that aligns Lithuanian business hours with UTC-based scheduling.
Monitoring and Synthesis

Module 8 built the integration layer — Connections for secure credential storage, Hooks for platform interaction, Variables for operational configuration, and provider packages for the ecosystem of external system integrations. Module 9 taught the monitoring interface — the Grid View for failure identification, the Graph View for dependency context, the Gantt View for performance analysis, the Calendar View for reliability trends, and the five-step troubleshooting workflow that diagnoses any failure in minutes. Module 10 brought it all together into complete SaulėGrid pipelines — Bronze, Silver, and Gold DAGs with cross-DAG dependencies, error handling, logging, and operational maturity.
What You Can Do Now

You can write Airflow DAGs from scratch — defining tasks, wiring dependencies, configuring schedules, and connecting to external systems. You can read any Airflow DAG file and understand its structure, its data flow, and its operational behavior. You can navigate the Airflow UI to monitor pipeline health, investigate failures, and recover from incidents using a systematic workflow. You can design pipelines using named patterns — linear chains, diamonds, branches, guards — and compose them into architectures that are efficient, observable, and resilient. You can participate in DAG code reviews, pipeline design discussions, and incident response with the vocabulary and the mental models that your team uses daily.
The Impact
SaulėGrid's Transformation

SaulėGrid's transformation mirrors what you have learned. Before Airflow, Matas and Ieva spent 10 hours per week on manual pipeline management — checking logs, rerunning scripts, tracing cascade failures. After Airflow, the morning health check takes 2 minutes, incident recovery takes 3-15 minutes, and the team spends their time building new pipelines instead of babysitting existing ones. The 400,000 meters, four regions, and three pipeline layers that once required constant manual attention now run autonomously through six DAGs with 40 tasks — monitored through a web UI that makes the entire system visible at a glance.
The Mid-Level Track

The mid-level track picks up where this course ends. You will learn production patterns that replace junior-level approaches: Datasets replace ExternalTaskSensors for event-driven cross-DAG dependencies. Dynamic task mapping replaces static fan-out when the number of parallel tasks is not known at DAG authoring time. Custom operators encapsulate reusable platform-specific logic. Secrets Backends replace database-stored Connections for enterprise security. CI/CD pipelines automate DAG testing and deployment. Performance tuning addresses scheduler bottlenecks at 50+ DAG scale. The mid-level track assumes everything you learned here — it does not re-teach it.

blocks.title: You are ready
blocks.content: You know why orchestration exists, how Airflow implements it, and how SaulėGrid uses it to run a production data platform. You can write DAGs, configure tasks, design dependencies, schedule pipelines, connect to external systems, and monitor everything through the web UI. The foundation is complete. Whether your next step is the mid-level track, your team's production Airflow instance, or your own project — you have the toolkit. SaulėGrid's data flows because of the concepts in this course. Now yours can too.
